Machine learning classification to the rescue. Scientists trained a custom image recognition model using photos of known geoglyphs and then set it loose on the landscape imagery. The categories were simple: “likely a geoglyph” or “not likely a geoglyph.” In only a few months, the system nearly doubled the discoveries of the entire previous century, identifying 303 additional geoglyphs. The task was perfect for classification: well-defined patterns that were nevertheless tedious and challenging for humans to spot.

The system amplified archaeological expertise. By taking on the drudge work of finding the unseeable, the system freed archaeologists to focus on the most valuable part of their job, interpreting the significance of these remarkable discoveries.

What are the needles in your users’ haystacks? How can rapid identification and categorization ease your audience’s work or let them focus on higher-order tasks? Content management systems can categorize incoming articles or documents based on their topics, tone, and structure. Customer support applications can route incoming tickets to the right department based on the language and context of the request.

p.span: Aerial photographs reveal anamorphic geoglyphs etched into the Nazca Desert thousands of years ago.

level3.p: These examples show how classification can be the right tool for fitting messy data into an established, known system. But what about the times when users don’t know what the system even is? When you need help locating the signal in the noise, that’s where classification gives way to clustering.

level2.p: Clustering is classification by machine logic instead of human logic. This is unsupervised learning: point a clustering algorithm at a heap of data, and it identifies natural groupings based on similarities and differences, without human guidance.

You’ve likely done your own kind of emergent classification with sticky notes. User researchers, for example, know the warm glow of wrapping up a series of insightful interviews, but they also know how the glow cools when they confront a daunting pile of interview notes and transcripts. Dovetail is a user research application that uses clustering to bring order to research quotes. Drop the quotes into a virtual pile of sticky notes, click the Cluster button, and Dovetail groups the quotes by topic (Figure 2.12). The tool gives researchers a first pass at surfacing the high-level themes buried deep in the research.

p.span: Dovetail’s clustering feature groups quotes from interviews based on common themes.

level2.p: Dovetail is doing more than shuffling quotes into categories—it’s creating the categories. While classification sorts things into predefined buckets, clustering identifies those categories in the first place. For both better and worse, these machine-generated groupings follow logic that might not line up with your own.

As data sprawls in size and complexity, clustering creates categories that might otherwise go unnoticed, uncovering hidden insights. It might find customer behavior patterns that don’t map neatly to typical demographic segments or highlight trends in user feedback before they’re obvious.

Recall how machine learning builds a metaphorical data map to measure the “distance” between items. Clustering goes a step further, charting “islands” in that map—groups of concepts that naturally cluster together in the sea of data (Figure 2.13). In Dovetail, for example, some quotes might share an island because they’re all about emotional connection, while others might group together around pricing and value.

p.span: Clustering seeks out similarities between items in a dataset and groups them together to create categories within previously unstructured data.

level2.p: Use clustering to surface invisible affinities and patterns—or to uncover outliers that don’t fit into any group. Outliers are data points that float outside of the clustered islands. Finding them is invaluable for spotting something amiss or emergent. Walmart, for example, uses clustering to find products where prices don’t match other products in their cluster. By identifying products that are priced too high or too low relative to their neighbors on the shelf, the superstore can correct errors that lose money or prevent sales.

This is anomaly detection, and it makes clustering helpful for detecting things like unusual spending patterns (fraud) or identifying symptoms that deviate from healthy norms (emerging health issues). More positively, clustering can uncover unconventional demographic blends (new customer segments) or unexpected behaviors (new product opportunities).

level3.p: Clustering doesn’t apply only to text or numbers; it can find natural groupings in any kind of information. Airbnb uses clustering to find behavioral similarities among property hosts based on when and how often they offer their properties for rent—seasonal, sporadic, event-driven, and so on. Clustering revealed eight distinct host segments to Airbnb’s product team and gave the company opportunities to tailor host experiences and messaging based on each segment’s rental patterns, engagement, and intention.
With such broad possibilities, how do you know when to apply clustering? The data always provides tells. Look for these signals that clustering can be an opportunity:

list.li: A pile of unstructured info makes it hard to find themes or order.
Data categories are fast-changing or highly personal.
You need to know when something doesn’t fit the usual patterns.

level3.p: Once you recognize these signals and apply clustering, the next challenge is creating interfaces that help users work productively with machine-discovered groupings. The very fact that clustering surfaces invisible patterns means those groupings are sometimes nonintuitive. Success depends on helping users apply human judgment to machine insight.

Balance discovery and control. Present clusters as starting points for exploration, not final answers. Dovetail handles this by making its clusters easily editable. Researchers can merge similar clusters, split broad ones, or create new ones entirely. The clusters become conversation starters: “Here’s what the system noticed. What patterns do you see?” This approach treats machine intelligence as a thought partner that can handle the heavy lifting of initial organization while leaving the meaningful analysis to human expertise.

Right-size the results. Dialing in the right number of clusters to present to users is more art than science. Too few clusters may miss important nuances; too many may overwhelm users with distinctions without a difference. Give users control over granularity. The data visualization tool Tableau, for example, allows you to specify the number of clusters directly, tailoring the level of detail to your needs. When analyzing sales data, for example, you might start with broad categories to identify general patterns across regions. If deeper insights are needed, you can drill down to reveal finer distinctions, such as regional variations in purchasing behavior. When in doubt, prefer fewer and more meaningful clusters; you can always split them further.

Design for change. Unlike classification’s fixed categories, clusters can shift as new data arrives or patterns change. Design for this fluidity. Help users track how clusters evolve over time, especially when that evolution reveals meaningful change. If customer support tickets suddenly cluster differently, that might signal emerging issues. If user behaviors form new clusters, that could mean new use cases. Create interfaces that not only show current patterns but also highlight significant changes in those patterns.

Like the other forms of machine learning, clustering shows how systems can surface potential patterns in vast amounts of data, but only humans can interpret whether those patterns matter and how. Then again, what’s considered the exclusive domain of human versus machine continues to be a moving boundary. This feels especially true in creative domains, where machine intelligence has pushed beyond analysis to tackle activities once reserved for people. That brings us to generative AI.

level2.p: We’ve arrived at the big leap. So far, you’ve seen machine learning surface patterns that help understand existing data. Generation takes a hard left turn from this sense-making path. It not only finds patterns in data, but it also uses those patterns as a springboard to invent something new—to create beyond the dataset. If machine learning’s other capabilities help you understand existing data, generation helps you manifest new data (and content and images and so on).

Generative models are creative mimics: They study large bodies of work to learn patterns, and then they use those patterns to produce new works based on what they have seen. Generation powers chatbots, assistants, and a host of “make it for me” creative requests. If something can be represented in bits and bytes, then generative models can churn it out. Text, video, music, images, and speech are all familiar examples by now. Generative models can even make new science: researchers use them to cook up new molecules, genetic sequences, and medical drugs.

All of this seems less like statistical analysis and more like artificial creativity‚ with the distinct downside that you can often feel the artificiality in the creativity. You can get results that bubble with seeming imagination, but in the next moment get something so bland, naïve, or clearly wrong that it’s hard to believe the same system made it. Other times the initial gloss wears off to be replaced by a beige sameness where everything occupies a safe middle ground—impressive individually but oppressive in aggregate.
What’s going on here?

level3.p: Generation is prediction’s ambitious cousin. Generative models don’t start with a complete “answer” in mind but instead construct the result one tiny bit at a time, playing a probability game with every step. When you give a large language model (LLM) a prompt like “Once upon a time,” it calculates the probability of every word in its vocabulary appearing next. It might determine that “there” has a 30% chance of being next, “in” has a 25% chance, and “lived” has a 20% chance. The model selects from these probabilities (usually with a dash of randomness to keep things interesting), adds that word to the story, and then starts the process again with the next word. Like a mosaic, each new piece influences the shape and placement of the next (Figure 2.14). This process is called inference, the moment when the model takes all the available context and determines the next small addition.

Technically, generation emerges as a continuation of the prompt it’s given. Many people understand a prompt as a question or instruction that you give to generative AI, but that’s not quite right. Prompts are not questions or descriptions that have a specific answer or output. Generative models understand prompts as starting points to extend, rather than queries to resolve. Think of a prompt like a seed. What grows depends on the kind of seed you plant and how you nurture it. If you provide a sentence fragment, the system will complete the sentence and keep on going. By contrast, if you ask a question, the system will provide an answer, the logical continuation of the text.

p.span: Generation creates in tiny steps, analyzing the overall context to shape the result one token (word, pixel, concept) at a time until it lands on the best match for the prompt.

level3.p: This makes generative systems very suggestible. Unless specifically programmed against, they default to rolling with what you propose. At the core of their architecture, these systems follow the “yes and” rule of improv. Minor changes to the prompt can create very different results.
NOTE THE TECHNICAL BITS
Today’s most powerful generative models typically rely on two key approaches:

list.li: Transformer architectures power many large language models. They calculate probabilities for which word should come next based on everything that came before; that choice becomes part of the context for the next choice, and so on. The transformer’s superpower is having a sense of the whole meaning of the prompt; they can analyze relationships across an entire passage of text all at once.

Diffusion models are mainly used for creating images, and they take a different approach. Starting with random visual noise (think TV static), they gradually remove the noise until a clear image emerges. Ask for a vintage postcard of San Francisco, and the model repeatedly predicts, “What should I change to make this look more like a vintage postcard?” Pixel by pixel, San Francisco emerges from the fog.

level3.p: This probabilistic approach explains why outputs can feel both familiar and strange. The step-by-step process creates results that follow recognizable patterns. They largely obey the rules of grammar, the physics of light and shadow, or the conventions of musical harmony. But because each step involves probability rather than certainty, the model might combine patterns in unexpected ways. Sometimes, the surprise delights; other times, it breaks the spell with obvious errors or bland clichés that remind us we’re working with artificial creativity.

Those broken spells are commonly called hallucinations. They feel like bugs, but they’re part of generation’s essential nature. When you ask a generative model to create something, it’s essentially dreaming up content based on its training data. And just like dreams, the results usually make sense at a high level but might include impossible or contradictory details.

That’s why machine-generated text can suddenly veer into fiction, confidently citing papers that don’t exist or inventing historical events that never happened. The system isn’t malfunctioning; it’s just following patterns to their statistically likely conclusion, even when that conclusion doesn’t match reality.

Generative models are dream machines, not answer machines. They do not deliver answers; they deliver results that look like answers. It should be more surprising that they deliver accurate responses at all than that they sometimes veer into fantasy. Although LLMs were trained by swallowing nearly the entire internet, the goal was to teach them language, not facts. Their ability to deliver (sometimes) accurate facts is incidental to how they work, not part of their essential design. If you want LLMs to deliver facts, you have to give them help, which we’ll touch on in a moment.

Hallucinations are among the most visible challenges of generative AI, but there are others. We’ll dig into hallucination further in Chapter 10, “What Could Possibly Go Wrong?” as well as broader challenges like data bias and other ethical concerns.

These quirks don’t have to be risks; used with intention, weird outputs can be channeled into creative exploration. When Adobe’s Firefly generates unusual color combinations or Midjourney creates surreal imagery, these “mistakes” can spark new directions. Even in the scientific realm, where you might expect type-A accuracy to rule, researchers have found hallucinations to be helpful. Nobel chemist David Baker and his colleagues used AI to invent ten million proteins “all brand-new; they don’t occur in nature,” Baker said. The process of discovery often starts with wild hunches and crazy ideas—and AI can deliver lots of them.

These systems excel at divergent results—generating many possible outcomes from a common seed prompt. If your users are doing creative exploration or brainstorming, these surprising combinations might be exactly what they need. Many of today’s most effective applications of generative AI support the creative process instead of replacing it. Developers, for example, use code generation to handle routine programming tasks while they focus on architecture.

But generative models often need human judgment to move on in a productive direction: which of those 10 million molecules are worth creating in Baker’s lab? If the generated content must be factually accurate or technically precise, hallucinations become a serious risk. (Not all outputs have such a high bar: the “correctness” of a song lyric is far more fluid than which wire to cut first. How much “give” does your topic area allow?)

You can limit this liability by pairing generative models with more reliable sources of data, a concept called grounding. (We’ll talk more about grounding as a fundamental pillar of Sentient Design experiences in the next chapter.)

Instead of using an LLM to provide answers on its own, you can use it to understand the intent of the user’s request. From there, the system can make requests of a knowledge-based system or a traditional algorithm to fetch trusted results and then present them in a contextually appropriate response. The “AI overviews” atop many Google search results aren’t generated by an LLM alone; the LLM is just summarizing Google’s traditional “ten blue links” results. Google search provides the data, and the AI overview provides the summary (Figure 2.15). We’ll explore this approach more deeply in Chapter 11.

p.span: Google’s AI overviews summarize the results found in the traditional search links below. Search is responsible for the data; generative AI packages it up.

level3.p: Let other systems do the fact-based work behind the scenes and let the LLM shine as the charming host that delivers the info.

level3.h3: More Than a Tool, a Mediator
level3.p: Generative AI can obviously generate, it says so right on the tin. But if you stop at “making stuff,” you’ll miss generation’s best qualities as a design material—in particular, its ability to understand and transform content.

Language and imagery boil down to symbols and signifiers. The same concept can be represented by different symbols—an English word, a Japanese character, a visual icon. Generative models are fundamentally expert at understanding and manipulating those symbols—translating and presenting concepts across languages and formats.

This works across media, too. Multimodal models can make sense of text, images, or video and then answer in kind—and they can “speak” in those media, too. That’s what lets some generative tools accept a PDF or video and then answer your questions about the contents.

This artificial understanding gives generative models a number of superpowers that you can put to work in product interaction. More than just “making,” here are a few ways you can use generative AI to embed intelligence into an interface.

level4.p: Generative features have infected interfaces with an epidemic of text fields, with little input boxes spreading like a rash. Often, these inputs get bolted onto existing applications like an afterthought, imposing a separate interaction mode and the context switching that goes with it.

Push harder. The command line is not the future of user experience. While text remains a useful and open-ended input, modern generative models can understand so many other formats. Your users can “talk” to these systems in ways that are far more elegant than yet another text box.

ChatGPT’s advanced voice mode goes beyond both screen and voice by folding in real-time video. Point your smartphone camera or smart glasses at the world around you, and the system can recognize objects, interpret their context, and suggest actions. Hold up a broken appliance part during a conversation, and it can help you find a replacement or walk you through a repair. Show don’t tell.

Multimodal models give your application new surfaces for interaction. They can understand input in whatever form makes the most sense for your users: documents, images, music, code, sketches, speech, data, or behavior patterns—or combinations of these. Multimodal prompts aren’t just versatile—they’re also powerful tools for accessibility, enabling interactions through voice, visuals, and even gestures to meet users where they are.

list.li: What’s the most natural way for your users to express what they want?
How can you help users express what they want beyond relying on text or specific wording?
How might combining different types of input—speech plus gesture, for example—make user intent more explicit?

level4.p: For years, voice assistants like Alexa and Siri could understand your words but struggled to grasp your meaning. “Set a timer for 15 minutes” worked because it mapped cleanly to a specific command. But try something more nuanced like “remind me to bring an umbrella if it’s going to rain tomorrow,” and you’d get a confused attempt to buy umbrellas. The assistant could parse the natural language into words but couldn’t bridge the gap to understanding the actual intent: check the weather forecast for tomorrow and create a conditional reminder based on the result.

Modern generative models are different. They don’t just translate words into commands—they grasp the context and implied meaning of an entire prompt. When you tell a generative interface “this looks too busy,” it understands you mean there’s too much visual noise and can suggest specific fixes like increasing white space or simplifying the color palette. The system is going beyond keyword parsing to get the gist of what you’re trying to accomplish.

Of course, generative models can still misinterpret ambiguous instructions, especially when context is missing or the task requires specialized knowledge. The work for the product team is to craft prompts or feedback mechanisms behind the scenes that help the AI refine its understanding in those cases. For example, you might add system prompts to ask users to clarify their intent if the system seems uncertain or provide feedback loops to improve AI predictions over time. (We’ll explore feedback loops in Chapter 11.)

list.li: Where can you ease user input through natural language—or by detecting intent in image, video, gesture, or another context?
How will you design for ambiguity and maintain user trust when the system’s understanding falls short?
What prompts, clarifications, or feedback loops can help the system clarify without frustrating users?

p.em: TL;DR: Use generative models to distill long-form content to essential meaning.

level4.p: E-commerce and review sites use generative AI as casual intelligence by adding a summary to the top of review sections. The summary whittles down hundreds or sometimes thousands of reviews to just a handful of sentences. Instead of wading through the hype and vitriol of the comment section to figure out whether to buy that steam iron, Amazon boils it down for you. A generated summary tells you the overall sentiment about the steam iron, the features people love and hate, and where there’s disagreement (Figure 2.16). The details are still there if needed, but the summary is often enough to go on.

p.span: Amazon (left) summarizes thousands of product reviews into a single, simple summary. TripAdvisor (right) does the same with hotel reviews to let you know how you’ll sleep and the state of the bathrooms.

level4.p: Summarization isn’t limited to straight text. Multimodal models can distill insights from heavy media like audio, video, images, or PDFs. They can provide quick summaries for meeting notes, research findings, or training videos, delivering the skinny without requiring a time-consuming deep dive.

list.li: Where does long-form text or media slow down users?
What’s the right format for that summary, and what’s the right moment to present it?
How will you make it easy to drill down to the full content when users need more depth?

level4.p: The ability to change writing style in text generation has become standard fare (“professional or casual?”). What happens when you apply the same tone shift to your interface? Generative models can help you shift voice and tone for different contexts.

Language tutor Duolingo, for example, applies a conversational tone in its AI-driven language practice to make interactions feel more approachable. Likewise, AI-powered customer service agents, like those supported by Zendesk, use sentiment analysis to adjust tones based on the seriousness of the situation, like calming a frustrated customer or engaging cheerfully with a satisfied one. (Go carefully: In high-stakes contexts, a mismatched tone could damage trust or escalate tensions.)

Tone and style adjustment works for media generation, too. Image generators can let you revise from “modern minimalist” to “vintage noir,” or a video editor can change your soundtrack from haunting to cheerful. For generative models, this all comes back to their fundamental ability to juggle symbols—textual, visual, audio—across a new dimension of tone or style.

list.li: Where in your interface could adaptive tone improve the user experience?
What contextual signals—like user sentiment, task complexity, or urgency—could guide those tone adjustments?
How can you make tone transitions feel natural rather than jarring?
In high-stakes situations, how will you ensure that tone adjustments help rather than harm?
How might users want to control tone? Should they have the ability to override system choices when tone adjustments miss the mark?

level4.h4: Translate Across Format, Language, or Medium
level4.p: Large language models are great at transforming content—they’re literally “transformer models”—which means they can liberate content from its original format to serve new audiences and use cases. This ability is essential to alchemist agents, which we’ll explore in detail in Chapter 8, “Agents of Change.” NotebookLM, for example, is a research organizer that goes beyond summarizing collections of documents to turning them into something completely different: a friendly podcast between AI “hosts” that illustrates key findings for a general audience. Converting an hour-long dense read into a casual five-minute listen changes the context of use and makes it accessible on the go.

Transforming content to a new format can make it easier to edit, too. Our agency Big Medium created a Figma plug-in that converts hand-drawn wireframes into editable layouts built with our client’s design system. (We call this the Pinocchio pattern, turning the puppet into a real boy; see Chapter 4, “Transactional Tools,” for more.) The process uses the model’s ability to understand visual wireframe symbols to ease the tedious work of assembling components, letting designers focus on creative refinement and polish (Figure 2.17).

p.span: A generative Figma plug-in converts a hand-drawn wireframe (left) into a layout built with the company’s design system (right).

level4.p: On the surface, this is simple format swapping, but something deeper is happening here. When you shift the format to something fundamentally new—PDF to podcast, sketch to editable Figma—you unlock a new context and use case. By shaking content free of its container, you introduce the possibility of shifting to an entirely new experience paradigm.

list.li: Where does content feel “stuck” in an inconvenient format? How would a different medium change or simplify workflow?
Where could a shift in format unlock an entirely new experience or find traction with a new audience?

level3.h3: “But What I Really Want to Do Is Direct”
level3.p: Add up all these talents, and you find that generative AI can do far more than churn out chat, images, or music. Because these models can understand intent, transform content, and speak both human and machine, they can orchestrate entire experiences. Generative models are the choreographers that make radically adaptive experiences dance.

In particular, generative AI can mediate the gap between unpredictable humans and rigid traditional systems. LLMs can make sense of the messy ways that humans communicate and then translate them into the structured formats that conventional systems prefer. Whether the input is a text prompt, spoken request, or UI interaction, these systems can translate that intent into instructions to update the interface—its content, structure, interaction, or all three.

In chat, this is straightforward: you send text to an LLM, which responds directly with only the barest interface between user and system. But as you saw in the last chapter, generative AI can speak GUI, too, and that’s when it steps into its director role. Instead of responding directly to the user, the LLM translates the request into instructions for the interface, “speaking” in JSON, XML, or any of the other structured formats that software devours.

Figure 2.18 shows a simplified example of this process. Say that you ask an AI-powered project management tool, “Show me the tasks due this week and highlight anything overdue.” The LLM receives your request along with a prompt to turn your command into instructions that the UI system can act on. Instead of responding directly to you, the LLM tells the UI system what to do, and it follows the instructions to filter and highlight the appropriate tasks.

This task-filtering example is simple; it basically flips UI switches for the user. But the same approach can unlock more complex possibilities, too. If you’re reading a dense report, for example, the system might trigger several tasks to generate explanatory text, create data visualizations, and build a custom dashboard to bring it all together.

p.span: With a little help from specialized prompts, LLMs can interpret user actions and respond with instructions formatted for the UI system to understand and act on. In this example, natural language is translated into specific JSON-structured commands to filter and highlight tasks.

level3.p: The result ventures into the territory of on-demand applications. It certainly embraces on-demand UI. This is the core capability that enables radically adaptive experiences, and it’s at the root of Sentient Design.

list.li: Instead of simply returning “a response,” what if you could return a new experience—a new workflow, tool, or interaction based on inferred intent?
What safeguards would ensure these generated experiences remain coherent, functional, and aligned with user goals?

level3.p: To make this work, generative interfaces need clear guardrails and design systems to keep the experience coherent. The wild and weird capabilities of generative models work best when they’re grounded in solid design principles and user needs.

This is where thoughtful prompt design comes in. As Figure 2.18 shows, these prompts can be plain-language instructions asking the system to translate intent to action. Designers excel at this kind of explanation, and they can (and should!) participate in this kind of natural-language system architecture. We’ll dig into that collaborative process in Chapters 12, “The Sentient Design Sprint,” and 13, “Work the Problem.” For now, your job is to challenge yourself to think beyond what generative AI can make—and instead consider what experiences it can enable.

level2.p: Knowing your materials matters. The Romans got it. By mastering the properties of their revolutionary material, their concrete, they knew which blend could bear more weight and which was best for building underwater. But understanding the physical properties was only the beginning. The Romans didn’t just build aqueducts and domes—they reimagined what a city could be.
Machine intelligence is the modern concrete and your new design material. What will you reimagine with it? What will be the Pantheon experiences of machine intelligence?

You’ve explored the core capabilities: recommendation, prediction, classification, clustering, and generation. Now that you understand your materials, you can begin exploring what new forms they enable and what new experiences they unlock.

level1.p: The Sentient Triangle

Intelligence comes in all shapes and sizes. Consider the humble slime mold: no brain, yet it can solve mazes to find food. Meanwhile, the octopus has more than a brain—a decentralized nervous system that lets its tentacles solve problems on their own. Dolphins have rich social intelligence—a collective savvy that seems at once similar but worlds apart from the swarm intelligence of ants. And let’s not forget the unparalleled emotional intelligence of the family dog … but your authors admit a certain bias.

The smarts of animals, insects, and even plants are inseparable from their form and their environment. The same is true for machine intelligence. The rapid evolution in machine learning and intelligent interfaces has triggered a Cambrian explosion of systems and interactions—each with its own shape, ability, and manner. New “species” of interfaces roam our screens and beyond—agents, assistants, copilots, adaptive interfaces, and on and on. It’s noisy, chaotic, exciting … the start of something new, even if the form of that “something” is still taking shape.

Just as naturalists need a framework to classify and understand the diversity of life, designers need a way to make sense of this burgeoning ecosystem of machine intelligence. How do you get your head around all the different Sentient Design experiences available to you as a designer? What are the new design patterns, and which old ones fall away? How do you name and organize those experiences? And how do you develop an intuition for how and when to use each interaction paradigm?

level2.p: Let’s start by going back to that link between intelligence and the forms and behaviors it manifests. What shapes can different machine-intelligent experiences take, and with what effect?
In other words: what’s the shape of Sentient Design? Turns out it’s a triangle (Figure 3.1).

p.span: The Sentient Triangle visualizes Sentient Design experiences, mapping them based on their mix of three machine-intelligent characteristics.

level2.p: The Sentient Triangle is a tool to visualize and explore Sentient Design experiences. This triangle diagram is based on a visual framework created by designer and technologist Matt Webb to map intelligent experience patterns.1 The triangle plots Sentient Design experiences based on how each blends these three pillar attributes of intelligent interfaces:

list.li: Grounded. The system has the training and info it needs to deliver reliably accurate results.
Interoperable. The system can work across systems and formats.
Radically adaptive. The system morphs in real time to meet user intent and context.

level2.p: NOTE WHAT IS GROUNDING?

Grounding is a machine-learning term for improving the relevance and accuracy of a model’s outputs. This is typically done by giving the model access to real-time info that wasn’t in its original training data.

One approach to providing this supplementary info is called Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG), which gives the system access to a database. Another route lets AI models use other tools or services; Google Gemini’s chat assistant, for example, can turn to Google’s search engine to help answer questions behind the scenes. In still other cases, the user can provide that info in the moment, like providing a PDF of background info for the system to reference. It’s like helping a student cram before a test, stashing it in the system’s short-term memory, or context window. We’ll explore the nature of memory and context in Chapter 5, “Chat and the Conversational Interface” (see “Conversations with an Amnesiac”).

Of these pillars, “radically adaptive” is the one that’s especially new and exciting for interaction design: interfaces that can practically design themselves. But let’s not undersell “grounded” and “interoperable.” While reliability and compatibility are hardly new to computing, recent leaps in intelligence and capability have changed their nature. When you give a system enough grounding in a specific domain, it can create relevant images, text, speech, music, etc. in ways that seem truly informed or even creative. Same with interoperability: When you crank up a system’s smarts for interacting with other services or formats, you get autonomous agents that work independently on your behalf.

These three pillars stake out the design space for intelligent interfaces. As you travel the triangle through its varying regions of grounding, interoperability, and adaptivity, the manner and interaction of the experience changes.

At a high altitude, the Sentient Triangle reveals four fundamental postures for Sentient Design, each claiming its own territory on the map, as shown in Figure 3.1. These are tools, chat, agents, and copilots.

Posture determines the system’s manner and relationship with the user. More than just differences in functionality, these postures describe the different ways users collaborate with intelligent interfaces:

list.li: People use tools.
People talk to chat.
People delegate to agents.
People are backed by copilots.

level2.p: These postures are the high-level categories that organize all Sentient Design experiences. Each embodies a particular blend of the triangle’s three pillars. Where a posture sits on the triangle tells you what it values most—grounding, interoperability, or adaptability—and how it trades between them.
We’ll dig into each of these postures in detail in the following chapters, but here’s a quick roundup.

level3.p: People use tools. They are efficient and often single purpose, the digital equivalent of a chef’s knife. The person wielding the tool is in control; tools don’t do anything until you pick them up to make something happen in a purposeful, guided way.
Tool interactions are simple and transactional: upload an audio file, get a text transcript; describe an image, get a generated picture. The entire journey is often one-and-done.

Because tools bank on reliability and control, they sit near the “grounded” point of the Sentient Triangle. The tight focus of tools is their strength, but it makes them less adaptive and interoperable. We’ll explore tools in detail in Chapter 4, “Transactional Tools.”

People talk to chat. The chat posture behaves as a conversational peer in turn-based exchange. Like any conversation, things can go in any direction, which makes chat both flexible and unpredictable. Because chat is so responsive and potentially unbounded, it lives near the radically adaptive corner of the triangle.

With the chat posture, Sentient Design extends the traditional notion of “conversation” to emphasize its give-and-take open-ended nature more than its specific form. While dialogue-based assistants and characters are part of chat as you’d expect, so are experiences like bespoke UI, which we introduced in Chapter 1, “Intelligent Interfaces.” Bespoke UI changes layout and interaction in response to user action, a back-and-forth exchange that takes place in interface, not dialogue—a conversation, nonetheless.
We’ll explore chat as text dialogue in Chapter 5 and this more expansive kind of exchange in Chapter 6, “Chat Is Not All Talk.”

People delegate to agents. Agents operate with little oversight, acting and coordinating across systems on the user’s behalf. Since their mission takes them into the world to use tools and get stuff done, the triangle’s “interoperable” corner is their home base.

Because agents do their work out of sight, the user experience becomes a manager experience, where the user gets updates at key moments. You’ll learn more about designing this manager experience in Chapter 7, “Agents and the Manager Experience.”

Agents vary from simple automatons that classify invoices, for example, to conductors of whole teams of agents tackling complex projects. This variety means they spread out across the triangle, snaking out from the “interoperable” corner and reaching toward the grounded and radically adaptive points. Many agent experiences overlap with tools and chat. You’ll meet these divergent agents in Chapter 9, “Copilots and Uncharted Territory.”
People are backed by copilots. Copilots keep a low profile: a continuous, deferential presence that is there when you need them and out of the way when you don’t.

Copilots share lineage with the humble but reliable spell-checker. Just like spell-check watches for mistakes and alerts you with a discreet underline, copilots stand by until they see an opportunity to help and quietly offer. Copilots can take over a call when you’re put on hold or offer to transcribe meetings when you fire up Zoom.

Copilots sit at the triangle’s center, making them equally grounded, adaptive, and interoperable. This crossroads location reflects copilots’ tendency to take on hybrid aspects of chat, agents, and tools, their form determined by situational context. You’ll meet these helpers in Chapter 9.
The four postures aren’t mutually exclusive. Just as nature doesn’t stick to rigid categories (hello, platypus), Sentient Design blends postures to create usefully varied experiences.

In fact, the unblended distillations of each experience posture, while potent, are often stunted. Chat experiences at the “radically adaptive” tip are glib and highly interactive, but they’re self-centered (not interoperable) and unreliable (not grounded)—fun friends you enjoy, but you’d never trust them to water your plants while you’re away.

Interesting things happen between the corners, often with overlapping postures: assistants that blend chat with agentic tool use, or tools that sculpt their result through chat-like back and forth. This is where Sentient Design’s variety takes form.

level2.p: When you dive down to a lower altitude in the triangle’s landscape, hybrid experiences come into view as specific experience patterns (Figure 3.2). While the postures define Sentient Design’s big behavioral archetypes, experience patterns describe the specific interfaces that users see and experience.
These experience patterns are Sentient Design’s 14 species of intelligent interfaces, roughly organized by the four postures.
These experience patterns reflect the nuances that develop as you migrate among postures, turning the dials to make experiences more or less grounded, radically adaptive, or interoperable.

For example, if you start with dedicated tools in the “grounded” corner but nudge the “radically” adaptive knob, the experience changes into inline tools that act on contextual content. Make it a notch more adaptive, and tools merge with chat creating “sculptor” experiences that can refine their output through back-and-forth collaboration with the user.

p.span: Different experience patterns emerge as you travel the zones of the Sentient Triangle.

level2.p: All of the triangle’s edges describe a similar journey, each establishing a common attribute for the experiences within these hybrid zones:

list.li: Collaborative = radically adaptive + interoperable. The interface works flexibly with the user and/or other systems.
Autonomous = interoperable + grounded. The interface is sometimes no interface at all; the system can confidently take action for you.
Iterative = radically adaptive + grounded. The interface works back and forth with you to refine and improve results.

level2.p: Now that you have your bearings on the map, all that’s left is to let it take you somewhere.

Understanding the Sentient Triangle first means understanding what it is not: the triangle is not a rigid taxonomy. We are in the early days of discovering the many species of intelligent interfaces. New experience patterns will certainly emerge, perhaps new postures, too.

In that sense, the triangle is perhaps more compass than map, a way to locate direction as much as fixed points. It describes what exists now but also points toward unexplored territories and potential innovations.

Sentient Design’s experiences are more multidimensional than this 2D map can perfectly capture. The triangle isn’t always strictly correct, for example, to insist that radically adaptive experiences always come at the expense of interoperability or grounding.

Even so, the Sentient Triangle is directionally accurate and provides a platform for exploration. By staking out the four postures of tools, chat, agents, and copilots, it gives visual explanation to their relative positioning and overlap. Use it as a prompt to think through available possibilities and test your assumptions. “What happens when I make this experience more adaptable, more grounded, more interoperable?”
The Sentient Triangle will be our rough guide to the landscape of intelligent interfaces, and we’ll use it to organize our exploration over the next several chapters.

We’ll use the triangle to tour each of the four postures. For each posture, we’ll take a close look at the experience patterns that inhabit each one to understand their unique behaviors and design considerations. From there, we’ll drill down even further: You’ll learn the UI patterns, interaction patterns, and functional patterns that animate each of these intelligent interfaces.

As you venture through the known territories mapped on the triangle, keep an eye out for the spaces between, too. Those gaps on the map are new experiences waiting to be developed. There’s a whole new world of design possibilities to discover. Those steps start with understanding what’s already in view. There’s more than enough known territory in the Sentient Triangle to stoke imagination and enthusiasm. Let’s start our exploration.

li.p: 1 Matt Webb, “Mapping the Landscape of Gen-AI Product User Experience,” July 19, 2024, https://interconnected.org/home/2024/07/19/ai-landscape.

level1.p: Transactional Tools

Tools don’t look like much at first glance, but don’t be fooled. They bundle their smarts in a humble interface that fronts as the simplest software: give an input, get an output, job done. The interface is a thin wrapper for the model beneath.

Tools provide a transactional interface to perform specific tasks with precision and reliability. The tool posture is one of blunt utility, emphasizing function over experience and capability over collaboration. This makes tools the least “sentient” of the Sentient Design postures. You use them through explicit controls or prompts in a way that feels more mechanical than aware or adaptive.

The simple interaction of tools often belies the complex processing happening under the hood. The experience of submitting a text prompt to AI image generator Midjourney, for example, gives little hint of the sophisticated technology that manifests the requested image moments later. Tools often feel like coin-slot delivery of near-magical results.

Tools find root in the “grounded” territory of the Sentient Triangle (Figure 4.1), where they deliver predictable interactions and steady results. Some tools venture toward interoperability to fit into larger workflows, while sculptors allow sustained interaction that begins to overlap with the radical adaptability of chat interfaces.

Throughout, the posture’s emphasis on efficient utility and control reflects an engineering perspective more than a design approach. Tools center what machine intelligence can do rather than how to do it experientially. But as you’ll see, sometimes this is all a product or feature requires.
There are four experience patterns in the tool posture (Table 4.1).

p.span: Tools anchor the grounded point of the Sentient Triangle.

level1.p: TABLE 4.1 TOOL EXPERIENCE PATTERNS

td.p: Piped to adjacent steps or systems
Enhancer at the point of action

level2.p: Dedicated tools are stand-alone utilities designed for narrow tasks with interfaces made of basic, explicit controls—sometimes only a button or text field, perhaps supplemented by a few switches and knobs for added control.

DeepL Translate is a dedicated tool for translating text, for example: enter text or post a file, et voilà, your translation appears. Basic form interaction like this is common in dedicated tools because they’re intended as efficient interfaces for submitting content for processing.

Even when the interaction is simple, it doesn’t have to be limited to text prompts. Use multimodal capability to let users work in whatever medium is most convenient. Photomath, for example, is a dedicated tool that lets students use the phone camera to take a picture of an equation to get math help (Figure 4.2). Shazam meanwhile uses the phone’s microphone to pluck songs out of the air and identify them. In both of these examples, the tool’s interaction surface is the environment around you. Years after Shazam’s introduction, this still feels like magic. For the best dedicated tools, delight is in their simplicity, no further experience design required.

The single-function purpose of dedicated tools means that the feature is the product. Dedicated tools are not designed to be part of a larger app, workflow, or experience journey. While other tool types are more integrated or flexible, dedicated tools stand apart, devoted solely to their specific tasks. Indeed, the power of dedicated tools is in the simplicity of their interface and mission.

p.span: Take a photo of an equation in Photomath to get step-by-step instructions to solve it.

level2.p: Beyond the quality or smarts of the result, machine intelligence also introduces interesting and sometimes quirky opportunities to deliver and control how that result turns out. You can use familiar tool-like controls to deliver new kinds of results that weren’t possible before.

level3.h3: A New Life for Basic Tool Controls
level3.p: Traditional tools use sliders or radio buttons or drop-down menus to control aspects of the output: brightness of an image, quantity of objects, and so on. This is the parameter UI pattern, giving the user explicit control to turn on options or nudge results along a specific spectrum. This familiar presentation has been around for decades in interactive tools—and in the dials and knobs of physical gadgets long before that.

div.p: PARAMETER

p.span: Explicit, fine-grained control over system output

div.p: Parameters provide controls to allow the user to enable or adjust aspects of a system’s output. They use familiar UI elements like sliders, drop-downs, or radio buttons to fine-tune results, including aspects that are unique to machine intelligence.

list.li: To give the user granular control over system output.
To make complex systems approachable through familiar controls.
To make implicit features or aspects of the system explicit and transparent.
Can overwhelm if too many parameters are presented.
May oversimplify complex processes.

level3.p: Parameters remain just as useful in intelligent interfaces, too. Only now, instead of adjusting only the mechanical output of a result, machine intelligence lets you adjust new kinds of qualities: emotion, style … weirdness. Even within the utilitarian nature of dedicated tools, machine intelligence gives you an opportunity to get creative with the attributes you surface (Figures 4.3 and 4.4). Should gift recommendations be creative or safe? Should recipes make you feel cozy or energized? Machine intelligence lets systems understand data and generate qualities that were previously opaque to them. As you build simple dedicated tools, consider what useful new parameters you might now surface.

p.span: Midjourney’s Aesthetics parameters let you crank up stylization, weirdness, or a variety of the images it generates. These parameters are nestled among more traditional “mechanical” options like size or speed.
Professional or casual? Wordy or concise? Figma’s slide editor suggests text edits based on your preferred tone and wordiness. Drag the dot around the Adjust Tone grid to make it just so.

level3.p: Many Sentient Design experiences begin life as a dedicated tool, an engineering prototype to demonstrate AI capabilities. In the examples we’ve seen here, the result is sharp enough to ship. Spare interaction is the point.

Just as often, though, a dedicated tool becomes a proof of concept to take forward into more nuanced experiences. Think of dedicated tools as single-celled organisms in the evolution of intelligent interfaces. Just as complex life evolved from these basic forms, sophisticated Sentient Design experiences tend to emerge from these powerful but rudimentary interfaces.

When you create a dedicated tool, you’ve laid a strong foundation—and there’s often an opportunity to build on top of it. Challenge yourself to go further: What are people really trying to accomplish by using this tool? How can you bring its capability closer to context? The answer often lies in adding the catalysts of integration or iteration—and integration is where workflow tools come in.

level2.p: Workflow tools take dedicated tools out of isolation and integrate them into a broader process. These tools move a step toward the Sentient Triangle’s “interoperable” corner. While they maintain the specialized, grounded nature of dedicated tools, they’re designed to work with other tools or services. Workflow tools accept input from a previous step and/or pump their output to the next.

Descript is an application that pipes together many features as workflow tools to create an intelligent interface for editing audio and video. Media editing is a lengthy and typically linear process of audio transcription, clip selection, editing, transition smoothing, adding audio/video effects, and more. Each of those steps is focused and technical, well suited to a pipeline of workflow tools. Descript seeks to provide that suite of tools, including several novel applications of machine intelligence. Each of those workflow tools has a specialized focus, but unlike dedicated tools, they’re integrated into a process focused on the same set of project files.

Descript’s flagship workflow tool is its clever interface for editing a video just by changing the text of its transcript (Figure 4.5). It makes video editing as familiar as word processing: Cut a sentence in the transcript, or add words to dub a new voiceover, and the video automatically updates to reflect the text changes.

p.span: Edit a video in Descript by editing its transcript. Cut text to remove that portion of the video; copy and paste in the transcript to move the corresponding clip to the new location.

level2.p: Editing is where the bulk of media projects happen, so this “text editor for video” is Descript’s main-event tool. But supporting that central experience are many other workflow tools that carry the project end to end, including these among others:

list.li: The “create clips” tool suggests and crops clips from long-form video.
The transcription tool transcribes the video of each clip.
The “remove retakes” tool automatically edits out muffed lines.
The “eye contact” tool adjusts your gaze in the video so that you always seem to be looking at the camera.

level2.p: All of these workflow tools apply machine intelligence to recommend, classify, or generate content within the specific confines of the task at hand. They’re only a degree away from dedicated tools but part of a larger and intentionally designed process. The fact that they’re stitched together makes them more than the sum of their parts.

list.li: How does the tool connect to the steps before and after it?
What contextual information can the tool access and use from the broader system?
How can the tool smooth transitions between different stages of work?

level3.p: Workflow tools are often bundled as features into a single application, as with Descript. Other times, they remain as stand-alone applications but are piped together through an organization’s formalized workflow routines.

Let’s stick with media editing but bump it up to a larger scale. Netflix deploys a bunch of internal workflow tools that use machine intelligence to ease the process for the streaming service’s content editors. These are the folks who cut trailers, choose video thumbnails, create social media campaigns, and more. Netflix built workflow tools to help them wade through the company’s catalog of hundreds of thousands of films and tv episodes. Each of these tools slot neatly into established editor workflows and integrate into the Netflix catalog and content management systems. For example:

list.li: A visual search tool supports the editing process by helping Netflix editors quickly find the clips they need—all the car explosions in an action film, say, or all the scenes where people shout “Merry Christmas” across the entire catalog.

An artwork discovery tool supports Netflix marketing and social media efforts by finding eye-catching freeze frames that cluster around the visual and narrative themes the tool identifies in the show. For the supernatural teen dramedy Wednesday, for example, the tool gives marketers images that match the themes “deadpan,” “psychic powers,” and “imaginative” (Figure 4.6).

p.span: An internal artwork discovery tool at Netflix identifies themes in a show and gathers eye-catching frames to match.

level3.p: Workflow tools are for more than just media editors, of course. In finance, the Bloomberg terminal offers workflow tools including earnings call summaries, risk scoring for investment companies, and suggestions for trading opportunities—all based on signals identified by machine intelligence. In the legal field, Kira is an application for handling project management for contract reviews and due diligence; its workflow tools use machine intelligence to address specific phases of that process, like evaluating key contract terms or letting you ask questions of the document.

Whatever the industry, the job of workflow tools is to provide tightly scoped functionality to ease and improve a narrow slice of a larger process and then pass along to the next. Like dedicated tools, they remain rigid and specific to their role.
Tools start to become more flexible as you integrate them more deeply into other experiences, and that’s where inline tools come in.

level2.p: Inline tools are embedded as features into other interfaces, offering contextual support right at the point of action. They maintain the single-purpose nature of other tool types, but you can apply them to the thing you’re working with right now without leaving context. That flexibility in the moment nudges inline tools along the edge of the Sentient Triangle from “grounded” toward “radically adaptive.”

In keeping with a tool’s posture of utility and control, inline tools remain mechanical and manual, requiring explicit action by the user to trigger them. In GUI interfaces, this typically happens through contextual menus, toolbar buttons, or gestures.

level3.p: The Notion web app is chockablock with inline tools. Notion is a collaborative workspace app that defies easy categorization; it’s the glorious love child of a note-taking app, project management tool, wiki, and database. In a nutshell, it lets you create and organize information in highly flexible, customized views. To help you sling and shape your work, the app embeds machine intelligence throughout the experience via inline tools that can create and edit content in context.

Like many inline tools, Notion leans on the contextual menu UI pattern to reveal tool options. Click Notion’s Ask AI button to conjure the options available in the current context. In a blank page, for example, the Ask AI menu offers to get you started with any of several common document types (Figure 4.7)

p.span: From a blank page, Notion’s Ask AI menu o#ers to help you get started in any of several formats.

level3.p: Once you’re past Notion’s blank page, different options become available when you click Ask AI, all based on your current selection (Figure 4.8). The menu offers several common options to apply to selected text, or you can type a free-form prompt to tell it what you want it to do based on the text in the page: “Make a checklist of steps for designers creating Sentient Design tools,” or “Summarize the key points of this transcript.” You can go in lots of directions right from where the cursor sits.

p.span: Notion provides lots of inline tools to revise, write, or summarize text. Select the text to edit, and a contextual menu offers all the options.

div.p: CONTEXTUAL MENU

p.span: Quick access to relevant actions based on current context

div.p: A contextual menu presents a set of actions or options that are specific to the user’s current selection. It typically appears on right-click, long-press, or via a dedicated button, staying out of the way until requested. These can be displayed as screen-bottom sheets on mobile.

list.li: To provide quick, tailored access to frequently used actions.
To reduce visual complexity of controls in the main interface.
To offer advanced features without overwhelming new or casual users.
Functionality hidden behind right-click or long-press may not be discovered by all. (A visible button helps.)

level3.p: JOSH’S TAKE

div.p: YOUR SPARKLES ARE FIZZLING
When you design a button to trigger an inline tool or any machine-intelligent feature, put the sparkles away.

In the heady days when generative AI features first started to land in consumer products, sparkles were suddenly everywhere. The sparkle emoji was the icon du jour of any feature that could write copy, make images, summarize PDFs, or otherwise generate content. Purple hues and rainbow gradients joined the aesthetic. The intended message: it’s magic, special, sparkly.

It’s really not magic, of course. It’s just software. And because so many companies rushed to bolt AI features onto existing products, the implementations were often experimental, sometimes shoddy. The sparkles became the inadvertent badge of half-baked beta features. Instead of “magic” and “new,” for many users sparkles now mean, “This feature is weird and probably broken. Good luck!”

So, what symbol should you use to trigger machine-intelligence tools? How about nothing. No need to segregate AI-powered features or treat them as anything special. You don’t announce that your app is powered by databases or implemented in JavaScript. Spare your users the implementation details and just deliver features that work. What matters to the user is what it does, not what powers it.
The goal is to make machine intelligence a seamless part of the user experience, not a novelty. Put the disco ball away. Play it cool.

(P.S. If you are worried that the feature is weird, unreliable, or broken, that’s a larger problem that sparkles won’t solve. We’ll dig into some ways to handle the weirdness of machine intelligence in Chapters 10, “What Could Possibly Go Wrong?,” and 11, “Defensive Design.”)

level3.p: Notion’s inline tools are powerful but mundane, only a few degrees more sophisticated than the humdrum spell-checker, itself both indispensable and ordinary. These features stay quietly out of the way until called up, but a subdued presence doesn’t mean the effects have to be subdued. Make sure that you’re adding meaningful, task-specific value, not generic functionality.

As machine-intelligent features become the norm, for example, all-purpose features like text generation or summarization will become commodity offerings built into the operating system or browser; they will be low-value features. Instead, think: How would a dash of intelligence in a specific application context improve, speed, or ease the user’s experience? What would that specially tailored intelligence look like? This means thinking beyond one-size-fits-all “write text” or “summarize this” features and zooming in on application- or domain-specific features.

If you’re working in a medical office, a feature to summarize a patient interview adds little novel value. But a feature that automatically uses the selected text to fill in the fields of the patient record … that’s a powerful, application-specific inline tool.

level3.h3: Turn the Puppet into a Real Boy
level3.p: Inline tools typically act to transform the selection in some way, often to elevate it from low-fidelity content to something more polished. This is the Pinocchio functional pattern, which fleshes an outline into text, zaps a sketch into an artwork, or transforms a wireframe into working code.

Apple’s Notes app for iPhone and iPad offers a Pinocchio feature called Image Wand. Draw a circle around a sketch or even an empty space in a note, and the app inserts a high-quality image inside the circle—using the sketch or surrounding context to turn rough ideas into “real boy” polished artwork (Figure 4.9).

p.span: Apple’s Image Wand inline tool lets you circle a sketch (left) to turn it into a polished image.

div.p: PINOCCHIO

p.span: Low-fidelity ideas transformed into high-fidelity results

div.p: The Pinocchio pattern turns the puppet into a real boy, zapping rough content into polished, functional output. It uses machine intelligence to elevate sketches, outlines, or rough ideas into something ready to share with the world.

list.li: To enable rapid prototyping of emerging ideas.
To bridge the gap between concept and execution.
To create inclusive experiences for users who don’t have the time or skill to do it on their own.
May not produce results that fully capture the nuances of the original concept.
Can lead to over-reliance on automated processes, potentially stifling creativity.
Might create unrealistic expectations about the ease of creating polished work.
Could lead to the user settling for and sharing “good enough” but not great results.

level3.p: The circle gesture interaction pattern helps to sidestep one of the pitfalls of inline tools: extra interface cruft cluttering the workspace. As a general rule, the more tools and features you add to an application, the busier and more complicated its interface becomes. The circle gesture dodges extra buttons and controls while letting you say, “Let’s do some work right here.” Contextual menus similarly hide controls until summoned via right-click or long-press, but the circle gesture does one better. It provides extra control and context with its explicit selection of the screen area you want to work with.

div.p: CIRCLE GESTURE

p.span: Method to select content and trigger action

div.p: The circle gesture allows users to select an area of interest by lassoing it with their finger, stylus, or cursor. The gesture triggers contextual actions or information related to the circled content, providing a natural way to interact with on-screen elements.

list.li: To offer a quick way to select and act on screen content.
To reduce UI clutter by hiding controls until needed.
To provide a consistent interaction across different types of content (text, images, etc.).
May not be immediately discoverable.
Can be challenging for users with motor control challenges.
Not well tuned for non-touch interfaces.
May conflict with other touch gestures in complex interfaces.

level3.p: The trouble with hidden gestures is that they’re … hidden. You don’t know they’re there until you learn about them, or they become a standard (like pinch to zoom or pull to refresh). Happily, the circle gesture has momentum for becoming just such a standard now that Android has built the gesture into its operating system.

In fact, it was Android’s “circle to search” feature that popularized the interaction. Long-press the home button or navigation bar to trigger the feature and then circle the thing on your screen that you’re curious about—text, image, whatever—to trigger a search or ask questions about it. A sheet pops up from the bottom of the screen to let you explore (Figure 4.10). That sheet could just as easily show options for actions to take on the selection—low friction access to inline tools and machine intelligence.

p.span: Android users can circle anything on their screen to launch a search using image recognition.

level3.p: No matter how you trigger them, the advantage of inline tools is their immediate contextual application: apply machine intelligence right here. But what if those smarts don’t get it right the first time? Alas, inline tools, like their dedicated and workflow counterparts, tend to be one-shot applications: here’s your result, done. In practice, things are rarely perfect on first try, especially for more sophisticated tasks. Tools become much more powerful when they can help to fine-tune the result. That’s the role of sculptor tools.

level2.p: Sculptors chip away at their material, collaborating with the user to shape the result until it matches the desired vision. Unlike other tool types specializing in one-shot results, sculptor experiences keep refining, refining, refining … until the polished result is revealed. It’s a back-and-forth collaboration between the user, the tool, and the material. The shape of the interaction is almost conversational, which is why sculptors straddle both the tool and chat postures in the Sentient Triangle. Sculptors are where tools begin to find the “sentient” in Sentient Design: They are radically adaptive, aware of immediate context/history, collaborative, and deferential.

level3.p: Photoshop’s “generative fill” feature is a basic sculptor tool with powerful functionality: Select an area of the image, describe what you want to see there, and tada …! Photoshop fills the selection with an image that matches the prompt and blends with the surrounding context (Figure 4.11). Need a hat on that lizard? Trace where you want Photoshop to draw the hat, describe it, and it gives you a few options to apply. If it doesn’t get it right the first time, you can experiment and refine it. Try a tinfoil hat, or a fedora, or a Napoleonic admiral hat. The tool creates yet more variations—and saves them all as options that you can apply and compare or just collect them all without fear of losing a good idea along the way.

This is the version vault functional pattern, turning each machine-generated result into a deposit in your creative account. Instead of discarding “almost-there” attempts, Photoshop banks them for later, letting you withdraw the best bits when inspiration strikes. This approach takes the pressure off getting it just right the first time, encouraging a more playful, exploratory approach that accumulates options as you go. If none of it works, just remove that “generative layer,” and you’re back to the original. (Undo is always a powerful friend when applying generated content.)

p.span: Photoshop’s generative fill tool (top left) lets you select an image and describe what to insert there. You can keep trying and refining new options, and the possibilities stack up in the layer’s Properties panel.

div.p: VERSION VAULT

p.span: A stash of previous versions for easy comparison and rollback

div.p: The version vault pattern saves multiple versions of work as changes are made, allowing users to review, compare, and revert to previous go-rounds.

list.li: In creative applications where users benefit from lots of experimentation.
When working with machine-generated content that may produce varied results.
When it’s valuable to see or browse the progression of work, including in collaborative environments.
Can be challenging to scale a browsing experience to many versions.

level3.p: Apple’s Image Playground application takes a more faceted approach to sculpting. Instead of relying exclusively on the blank canvas of a free-form text box to collect instructions, Image Playground adds click-n-pick UI elements to control the generated image (Figure 4.12). Choose from preselected image categories—or even a person from your contacts—to kick off the process. The tool draws a picture based on your selections and then launches several tags into orbit around the image, each describing an image attribute. You can remove, edit, or add to those attributes to fine-tune the result. Instead of typing a brand-new description, you can make changes by tweaking its attributes.

p.span: Image Playground supplements text prompts with suggested image attributes that you can add, edit, or remove to change the result.

level3.p: Image Playground is an example of the trait tag UI pattern—a visual system that chunks the attributes of the desired result into interactive tags. Because these tags are presented as controls for further iteration, the trait tag pattern is ideal for sculptor experiences. The user can gradually chip away at the project by selecting, adding, or modifying tags, moving from a coarse to fine result without composing a detailed text description.

div.p: TRAIT TAGS

p.span: Simplified control of generated output by editing attributes

div.p: Trait tags are interactive tags, chips, or menus that represent attributes or characteristics of generated output. Users can add, remove, or modify these tags to refine results, providing an intuitive way to sculpt output without relying on free-form text descriptions.

list.li: To encourage exploration and iteration in content generation.
To make the description of complex and open-ended content generation more approachable.
To overcome “the blank page problem” of helping users get started and understand what the system can do.
To provide guardrails that guide behavior in ways that align with the system’s capability and desired use.
Can limit creativity if users rely solely on predefined tags.
May oversimplify complex concepts into discrete categories.
Doesn’t adapt well to very fine control.

level3.p: This isn’t only about ease. As the designer, you can also use trait tags to constrain actions in useful ways. By presenting only specific options, you can short-circuit the risky open-endedness of a very radically adaptive experience. This does limit the potential scope and power of an unbounded experience, but it’s a way to use familiar UI controls to keep the interaction on the rails.

Trait tags are commonly represented as tag or chip UI elements—that’s what Image Playground does—but they can also be drop-down menus. These menus provide an intuitive way for the system to propose adjacent alternatives. MusicFX is a song-generating app that uses this approach to let you adapt songs based on your text description. The app transforms words and phrases in your text prompt into trait tag menus to make it easy to explore around a theme. Change “heavy metal” to “reggae,” if that’s more your speed. This approach blends trait tags with the Mad Libs UI pattern, with a fill-in-the-blank invitation to playful substitution (Figure 4.13).

p.span: MusicFX offers trait tag menus in a Mad Libs format, inviting exploration in a clear and obvious way. Below, the app offers additional trait tags to add.

div.p: MAD LIBS

p.span: Fill-in-the-blank prompt interface for templated control

div.p: The Mad Libs pattern presents users with a partially completed template or sentence, with blank spaces or drop-down menus for specific types of words or phrases. You fill in the blanks to get personalized outcomes.

list.li: To simplify the creation of structured content like queries, stories, or descriptions.
To make technical or complex inputs more accessible to non-expert users.
To encourage creativity within a constrained framework.
May feel restrictive for those who prefer more fine-grained control.
Can lead to repetitive or formulaic outputs if the templates aren’t diverse enough.
Can trivialize complex topics.

level3.h3: Refine the Result, Not the Prompt
level3.p: All of the sculpting examples so far have gradually adapted the prompt, the description of the desired result. This is a degree away from refining the result itself, and the approach generates a new result with every pass. It’s like creating a sculpture by bringing in a whole new slab of granite every time you want to make a change.

What if you could refine the result more directly to make more narrowly focused changes? “Keep the melody but replace the guitar with saxophone.” Inpainting is a functional pattern that allows for precise, targeted edits, keeping most of the creation intact while chiseling away at specific areas that require more attention. The term comes from a technique used by art restorers for centuries to repair and preserve artworks, filling in gaps and matching colors and textures to make repairs seamlessly. In the digital realm, the term refers to algorithmic edits that target specific portions of the work while leaving the rest untouched. True to the spirit of Sentient Design, inpainting is aware of the context and radically adaptive to desired changes within that context.

div.p: INPAINTING

p.span: Targeted edits that preserve the integrity of the whole

div.p: Inpainting allows you to make selective edits or additions to a larger piece of work, which could be image, audio, video, text, code, etc. The system intelligently blends the changes with the surrounding material, using context-aware smarts to ensure the changes integrate seamlessly.

list.li: To make small tactical changes without starting over or disturbing the rest of the work.
Could lead to overreliance on automated fixes, potentially diminishing people’s skill development or settling for “good enough” patches.

level3.p: Inpainting has been used for decades in digital image editors. Photoshop’s “healing brush” feature, for example, predated machine learning applications but algorithmically applied specific lighting or texture effects to an image in a way that blended naturally into the scene. Image editors are keeping up with the times by using generative models for more creative applications of inpainting.

Previous versions of ChatGPT, for example, allowed you to use a brush tool to “paint” the specific area to refine in a generated image (Figure 4.14). Type a description of the change to make, and ChatGPT changed only that area of the generated image. It’s Photoshop’s generative fill but applied iteratively to the generated content itself: inpainting all the way down.

(Alas, many image generators, including ChatGPT in this example, have retreated from this inpainting pattern, relying on text prompts alone to edit and refine images. This is a step backward; by blending traditional selection with text prompts, inpainting offers more control and specificity than either alone.)

p.span: Previous versions of ChatGPT offered inpainting via a brush tool to highlight a targeted area without disturbing the rest of the image.

level3.p: Despite its painterly name, inpainting isn’t limited only to images. More broadly, it’s a way to collaborate with machine intelligence to precisely target specific areas of any work. For example, “audio inpainting” is a key feature of Udio, a song generator and editor (Figure 4.15). The feature lets you select and rewrite portions of an audio track to alter instruments, smooth transitions, or write bridges between sections of the tune.

p.span: Udio’s “audio inpainting” feature edits specific snippets of a song, editing lyrics on the fly, for example.

level3.p: Sculptors, like all turn-based intelligent interfaces, become collaborative creators, ping-ponging solutions with users in a cycle of progressive refinement. This dynamic is called a feedback loop, a functional pattern for guiding collaboration with machine intelligence. Instead of either the user or the machine doing all the work, the creative process is shared, with the user directing the machine’s work. Mistakes are corrected, the vision is reinforced, and the process leads to steady improvement.

div.p: FEEDBACK LOOP

p.span: User assistance to help the system improve

div.p: A feedback loop invites dialogue between you and the system, where each interaction informs and improves the next. You provide input or make choices, the system responds with results or suggestions, and the cycle continues, allowing for progressive improvement and learning on both sides. This can be as simple as you giving a thumbs up/down to machine-generated results, or more specific controls to guide the output. Whatever the interaction, the system should learn and improve based on the feedback.
