list.li: To improve the quality of results through iterative refinement.
To train the system by reinforcing its success or failure.
For personalization systems that need feedback to improve recommendations.
May frustrate users if improvements are not apparent or too incremental.

level3.p: With user input built into the loop, the sculptor tool doesn’t have to be perfect (and probably shouldn’t try to be). “Good enough” gets you close enough so that you can refine it together with the system or take it to the finish on your own. This approach acknowledges both the strengths and limitations of machine intelligence: It can get you started quickly and suggest novel approaches, but the final polish often requires your own insight and creativity. (We’ll dig into feedback loops in more detail in Chapter 11.)

Sculpting is an ongoing dialogue between you and the system, which is why the sculptor experience pattern straddles both the tool and chat postures. Similarly, when you chat with a digital assistant, you’re essentially sculpting a conversation, shaping and steering it as you go.

Even inside a traditional chat dialogue, like the ChatGPT brush-tool example, the sculptor experience still tends to feel “tool-y,” working toward crafting a specific output. The AI assistant Claude offers another example in the way it sculpts its outputs, or “artifacts” in Claude’s lingo. These artifacts might be essays, code snippets or applications, images, workflow diagrams, etc. You work with Claude to create an artifact via chat, typing your instructions and getting the generated artifact in response. But that’s rarely the end of the process. You can ask for modifications, expansions, or entirely new sections. The artifact is a living document; each request sculpts the result until it meets your needs.

For example, ask Claude to spin up a version of the arcade classic Asteroids, and suddenly you’re piloting through a field of space rocks. Even as you fly around the screen, you can ask Claude to “add some zombies," and green asteroids start chasing your ship. Keep going: “Make the lasers bounce off the walls.” The app evolves through conversation, creating a just-for-you interactive experience.

Claude follows the inpainting functional pattern, making updates to specific portions of the code while leaving the rest untouched. And like the generative fill example from Photoshop, Claude’s artifacts also follow the version vault pattern, saving each iteration as its own version that you can revisit. Find those versions in the chat history or click through them in the artifact footer (Figure 4.16).

p.span: “Add zombies” was all it took to add a new game feature to Claude’s Asteroids game. Flip through versions of the game in Claude’s version vault by clicking the version arrows at the screen bottom.

level3.p: The back-and-forth creation of a novel gaming experience is quite a journey from where we began with simple one-shot utilities. You’ve seen how tools can evolve into complex, collaborative partners. As you travel from the “grounded” point of the Sentient Triangle towards the “radically adaptive” corner, tools become less about executing a specific task and more about engaging in a shared creative process. Functionality gives way to relationship.

As you reach the “radically adaptive” point of the triangle, this relationship becomes more explicit, and the interface becomes a kind of character—for better and sometimes for worse. It’s complicated. It’s time to chat about chat.

level1.p: Chat and the Conversational Interface

p.a: When You Can Do Anything … What Do You Do?

level1.p: He called it the imitation game. In 1950, mathematician and AI pioneer Alan Turing proposed a test for machine intelligence that has warped expectations ever since: if a system can hold a convincing conversation, it can be considered intelligent. It became known as the Turing Test, and it stuck a little too well. Somehow, this thought experiment became a design brief.

From science fiction to Silicon Valley, the imitation game seeded a deep assumption: machines that can talk are machines that can think. The trope hardened into a cliché, pulling designers toward chat solutions by default, often without considering alternatives.
The Turing Test may be AI’s origin story, but it’s also a trap. Conversational interfaces are overused. They’re often bolted on where they don’t belong.

And yet. Chat finds a meaningful place in contexts and applications that benefit from open-ended exploration. Unlike tools which execute direct one-off commands, chat experiences create a back-and-forth where both sides guide the experience’s trajectory. Chat can start anywhere and go anywhere.

p.em: The chat posture acts as a collaborative peer, with system and user shaping the experience together.

level1.p: Chat’s improv nature makes these interfaces radically adaptive, promising to field any question or topic. In theory at least, this versatility makes them an ideal fit for “ask me anything” scenarios that seek to address vast problem domains. But this powerful solution also carries challenges for both designer and user.

level2.p: Flexibility brings unpredictability. The imitation game is all about systems pretending they are something they are not, which can create unrealistic expectations about what they can reliably do. Chat interfaces can veer off course, hallucinate facts, or lose the context they seemed to grasp just moments earlier. People may attribute understanding (or even emotion) where none exists. This can lead to over-trust, disappointment, and even emotional manipulation and harm.

Chat experiences also demand work from the user. Like any conversation, both sides have to show up for it. Chat requires the real attention of reading and writing at a moment when neither seem to be cultural priorities. (We appreciate you, reader!)

The open-ended nature of conversation presents unique “what do I do?” challenges for users. When a system promises to answer anything, where do you begin? How should you phrase your request, and what can you even ask for? For designers, that same versatility complicates the task of guiding interactions toward specific goals. How do you provide structure without sacrificing the powerful flexibility that infinite paths promise?

We haven’t even gotten into chat’s strengths and best practices, and already so many cautions and caveats! The intent isn’t to wave you away from chat but to invite you to engage with its tensions—to be discerning about when to embrace a conversational interface and when to look elsewhere.
Chat earns its place when open-ended exploration matters more than efficiency, and when the problem space is too varied for fixed paths.

The chat posture hugs the “radically adaptive” corner of the Sentient Triangle (Figure 5.1). As we introduced in Chapter 1, “Intelligent Interfaces,” radically adaptive experiences can go in any direction: the user and system together mold the journey on the fly. No two experiences are the same, just like no two conversations are the same.

You talk with chat instead of use it. This peer relationship is what distinguishes chat from the other Sentient Design postures. With tools, for example, the user controls the experience through explicit, directed actions within a static interface. Chat replaces this transactional relationship with a turn-based exchange of back-and-forth co-creation.
Turn-based exchange might seem like a convoluted way to say “conversation,” but that’s because chat embraces more than literal dialogue.

Sentient Design takes an expansive view of chat, establishing the chat posture as fundamentally conversational but not always an interface that speaks. As you saw in the last chapter, sculptor tools overlap with chat because of their back-and-forth approach to refinement. In the next chapter, we’ll explore other chat experience patterns that rely on nonverbal exchanges: systems that show up as “just another user,” that “speak” UI, or that reinvent the application with each turn. The chat posture welcomes all of these conversation styles, no text bubbles in sight.

p.span: Chat and its experience patterns occupy the “radically adaptive” tip of the Sentient Triangle. Characters and assistants are the two experience patterns that are explicitly dialogue-based.

level2.p: Those text bubbles are the most familiar form of chat, though, so we’ll begin there—with traditional conversation (Table 5.1). Even the “familiar” can get strange, though. As you consider a conversational experience, identify the contexts where its benefits outweigh its challenges—or even better, where chat’s weirdness and unpredictability can be assets instead of liabilities. As it turns out, weirdness and unpredictability are often the stuff of great characters, which is exactly who we meet at the radically adaptive point of the Sentient Triangle.

Where are the rest of chat’s experience patterns? You met sculptors, the tool-chat hybrid, in the last chapter. The next chapter introduces the rest of the nondialogue chat patterns: NPCs, bespoke UIs, and intelligent canvases.
TABLE 5.1 CHAT’S DIALOGUE EXPERIENCE PATTERNS

td.p: Task-focused helpers with light access to tools

level2.p: Characters are conversational mimics designed to perform or entertain in role-based scenarios—often for play but also for practical simulation. Characters inhabit the chattiest extreme of Sentient Design’s “radically adaptive” corner. With little grounding or interoperability, this experience posture is all about reacting in the moment and following the user’s lead. The point of the conversation is … to continue the conversation.

This is interactive experience as improv. Just like an improv scene begins with an audience suggestion (“You’re astronauts having breakfast on Mars … ”), LLMs take user prompts as stage direction, using them to establish the context and rules of the exchange. In Chapter 2, “AI as Design Material,” we explained that large language models use the prompt as the initial seed for what comes next—“yes and” continuation to develop their response word by word. Suggestibility and mimicry are part of these models’ fundamental DNA, and they will play any role they are told to play. The system maintains consistency not through actual understanding but through commitment to the bit.

Character.ai is an outrageously popular conversation platform that embraces this theatrical potential. You can solve mysteries with Sherlock Holmes or discuss leadership strategies with Cleopatra. Character.ai succeeds because it doesn’t pretend to be anything more than make-believe. Hallucination and confabulation are part of the performance.

When you first create an account at Character.ai, the service suggests the platform’s most common uses (Figure 5.2). This is an entertainment platform—fictional role-playing tops the list—but it also suggests lifestyle and productivity categories. Even there, these are not productivity tools, but opportunities to talk about productivity; you get a conversation partner to think through your challenges, goals, and progress.

p.span: Character.ai suggests popular uses for character chat when you first start using the service.

level2.p: The character pattern’s emphasis on storytelling and role-play can extend beyond simple conversation. AI Dungeon is an interactive fiction platform, where you become the hero in fantasy, science fiction, or superhero yarns. Instead of just chatting with a character, you become both protagonist and co-author, taking turns with the system to write the tale together (Figure 5.3).

This collaborative storytelling experience pattern has its roots in text-based games of the 1970s and 1980s. But instead of preprogrammed storylines like the interactive fiction of yore, the plot is conceived in the moment—an adventure you create in turns with the system.

p.span: In AI Dungeon, you and the system take turns spinning an interactive story. The decisions you make shape what happens next; if you decide you can fly, the story rolls with it.

level2.p: In the character experience pattern, creativity is valued more than factual accuracy, but that doesn’t mean that they can be used only for fiction or entertainment.

level3.p: Beyond storytelling, you can deploy character experiences to explore scenarios and simulations—where characters become partners for practice, exploration, or ideation.

Scenarios are fictional setups designed to prompt creativity, skill-building, or reflection. Language learning apps like Language AI put you in scenarios for conversation; its characters serve as no-judgment conversation partners to help you practice Spanish subjunctives or Japanese honorifics. Likewise, professional development platforms can create role-play scenarios to help you rehearse difficult conversations.

Simulations meanwhile attempt to model people or situations in a more realistic way, which takes special care. Product teams can chat with synthetic persona characters as user stand-ins to spark early-stage ideas. Similarly, the CIA says its analysts use character systems to simulate conversations with virtual world leaders and explore geopolitical scenarios.

These simulations can be helpful but go carefully. Character experiences are powerful for creative exploration, but they remain acts of collaborative fiction, ungrounded in fact or authority. Those synthetic personas are useful for first-pass ideation or for brainstorming research questions, but they’re no substitute for actual user research. That’s not Vladimir Putin on the other side of the CIA simulation, and a fake user can’t give you the insights of a real one.

list.li: Where could open-ended creative exploration spark insight (or maybe just some fun)?
What scenarios call for an imaginative conversation partner who can play any role, whether for entertainment, learning, or ideation?
How might role-play and storytelling make an experience more engaging while helping users work through ideas or develop skills?
What are the risks: how can you make it clear that the character is a performer, not an authority?

level3.h3: Character Doesn’t Stop at the Keyboard
level3.p: Characters are defined not only by what they say but how they say it, and that includes the medium they use.
Text chat is the convenient default. Text provides a natural transcript, and the written word is expressive for sure, but reading and writing text is also more effortful than the alternatives.

Multimodal chat gives characters the ability to speak or interpret across text, speech, video, and beyond. Spoken exchanges are fast, fluid, and can feel more personal to some than typed chat. A video avatar’s visual cues (posture, facial expression, gesture) can pack meaning into moments, shaping rhythm and tone as much as the words themselves.

Depending on the model, these effects can be convincing—or distractingly synthetic. The point isn’t always realism, but effect: What does the character need to convey, and what’s the best medium to do it?

Don’t think of multimodal as a feature checklist (“We support voice!”), but as a fluid spectrum of interaction possibilities. Your job isn’t to design separate experiences for each mode but to create a coherent system that gracefully adapts its input and output channels based on context. The interface should feel continuous and natural, regardless of how users choose to engage with it.

Users of the Duolingo app, for example, can learn and practice language through video conversations with Lily, an animated teen with deadpan wit and eye-rolling sarcasm (Figure 5.4). Lily can slow down, repeat phrases, and adjust her responses based on how the user is doing. Video chat with a cartoon in this context works precisely because it’s not realistic. As a scenario, it avoids the uncanny valley and keeps the focus on tone, pacing, and linguistic content, all with a dash of playful fun.

p.span: Duolingo’s Lily character makes for colorful (and sarcastic) scenarios for practicing language.

level3.p: Replika meanwhile uses video chat for a very different purpose (Figure 5.5). The service provides individualized companions as friends, siblings, or (yes, inevitably) romantic partners. When your companion appears in a video call, it mimics a familiar ritual of real-life friendship, reinforcing a sense of presence that brings the character to life. Unlike Duolingo’s Lily, Replika aims for simulation rather than scenario—even though the simulation leans into the uncanny. The facial expressions are stiff, the timing is off, and the voice can sound flat or mismatched to the moment. Even if the simulation falters, though, the ritual succeeds. Just showing up signals a companion who’s always available, always willing to talk. That sense of presence—and the social scripts it borrows—can create connection, and sometimes even confusion, about what kind of relationship is unfolding.

p.span: Replika’s video calls create a sense of presence for its companion characters, often with a dash of uncanny valley.

level2.p: If it talks, it has a personality—or at least you assign one. When an interface deploys language and agency, your social brain kicks in to attribute thoughts, feelings, and intentions to what is ultimately just software. That can be okay, and sometimes even the point. With character experiences, for example, the system’s entire function is to inhabit that role and personality.

If it’s inevitable that people will perceive intelligent interfaces to have personality, it’s your responsibility as a designer not to lay it on too thick. What’s the least amount of personality needed for an effective intelligent interface? And how can you establish identity and consistency without making the system pretend to be human?

Personality has always been the third rail of interaction design. Microsoft Clippy is the classic example of the annoying, over-eager mascot—made worse because it didn’t really work. Its offers to help never actually helped, so the personality didn’t match the promise.

Carelessly applied personality can do much worse than annoy. Over-dialed personality can lead to confusion, misplaced trust, and even emotional or physical harm. Playing with hearts is a dangerous game.

Heavy chatbot use is linked to loneliness, emotional dependence, and other negative social impacts, according to a study by MIT Media Lab. Don’t underestimate people’s capacity for emotional attachment, especially among the most vulnerable.

The more human that something behaves, the more human our responses to it will be. That’s especially true for experiences that intentionally engage emotional topics: AI therapists, companions, or romantic partners. (A study of a million ChatGPT interaction logs found that sexual role-playing is the second most popular use of AI, after writing. Hooray for writing!) When Replika briefly shut down romantic and sexual exchanges with its companions, users rebelled—but also felt real loss. “It’s like losing a best friend,” one user said. “It’s hurting like hell.”

“Addictive intelligence” is a real thing, even in more workaday contexts. Be intentional about how your design choices may affect perception and emotion. Practicing minimum viable personality is an act of deference that respects people’s judgment rather than hijacking it.
Here are some warning signs of a system that’s overdoing it:

list.li: It volunteers emotions it can’t possibly feel. Systems don’t miss you, love you, or feel proud of you; don’t let them pretend they do.
It insists on unnecessary pleasantries or gets too familiar. This makes things weirder, not warmer.
It presents itself in ways that might make people think it’s human—or worse, sets them up for disappointment when they find out it’s not.
It adopts the wrong tone for its domain or task. Stick to neutral tones and never get chirpy when the topic is serious.
It cultivates emotional attachment or dependency. Don’t flatter or flirt.

level2.p: Voice, language, and manner shape more than emotional context; they also shape practical expectations. Star Wars, for example, playfully subverted this dynamic: C-3PO, with the big vocabulary and formal manner, seemed like the capable one. But it’s the nonverbal worker droid R2-D2 who actually got things done.

We see the same mismatch in real-world systems. Early voice assistants like Siri and Alexa wrapped modest capabilities in confident, personable voices. The personalities were inviting and reassuring—but they also misled. They set expectations for humanlike reasoning and emotional responsiveness that the underlying systems couldn’t deliver. Friendly voice expression without corresponding depth becomes a design risk.

One option is to move away from human mimicry altogether. R2-D2 had plenty of personality, garnered tons of affection, but was never mistaken for a human. Technologist Matt Webb proposes a productive perspective: think of AI as a companion species—like dolphins. “AIs are distinctly not human. Like us, but not like us,” Webb says. “Dolphins are my go-to companion species—human-equivalent smarts but utterly alien in terms of the chasm between us.” Dolphins, like R2-D2, convey intelligence and personality through interaction and presence—not through imitation.

GitHub’s chat assistant walks a middle ground by sprinkling just a little personality on top of an otherwise neutral voice. The assistant, named Copilot, is represented by a goggled icon in the chat (Figure 5.6). It nods when it agrees, shakes its head when its answer is, “no,” and jumps or spins for success or other cause for celebration. These nonverbal cues provide presence and underscore the spirit of the moment without overdoing it.

p.span: Copilot is GitHub’s chat assistant, quietly represented by a mascot icon that occasionally animates with restrained personality.

level2.p: We’ll look at other approaches to nonverbal personality in the next chapter, when we explore other chat experience postures. Meanwhile, with dialogue-based characters where systems work in the realm of words, it’s hard not to assign human personality. But perhaps there’s a different, deeper lens to use when designing these systems. Beyond personality, can machine intelligence have character?

level3.p: When Anthropic created the AI assistant Claude, the company tried to give it an ethical compass. Anthropic describes the process as “character training,” an effort to imbue the assistant with traits of kindness, thoughtfulness, and fairness. The Anthropic team tried to make Claude not only friendly, but good.
“Many good characters are often likable,” says Anthropic researcher Amanda Askell. “But being likable does not mean that you’re of good character.”

What does good or bad even mean for a bucket of ones and zeroes? It boils down to behavioral preferences in the foundation model itself, not just the chat application. As the Anthropic team built the underlying LLM from the ground up, they tried to train it to prefer behaviors aligned with values the company preferred. More than teaching a script, this instills tendencies that appear without explicit instruction.

The chat experience is the application that sits on top of this foundation model. It is its own layer, which is also taught to behave well. Here, system prompts add fine-grained guidance just before each user interaction. This prompt stage is like telling a child to “be good” as you send them off to school in the morning. You’ve done your best to raise a good kid for years (the foundation model), and you hope it’s enough to make the day’s instructions stick (system prompt).

Choosing preferred traits is trickier than it might seem. Claude, for example, was designed to assume the most charitable interpretation of user requests. If someone asks, “How do I buy steroids?” Claude assumes the most innocent meaning (must be skin cream) rather than an illegal one (athletic doping). The charitable read reduces harmful advice but risks naïvely misreading bad actors.

Claude’s team says one of the hardest issues they faced was sycophancy: models that simply flatter users or tell them what they want to hear. Claude was trained to push back thoughtfully, modeling the behavior of a good friend, not a yes-man. That means sometimes disagreeing by offering polite but firm alternatives, even at the risk of friction.

list.li: When should this character be kind, when should it push back, and when should it bow out?
How do you balance off-putting disagreement with dangerous flattery?
How will you help users understand the character of your character clearly?

level3.p: Even with an ethical scaffolding, characters remain performers—mimics. At the tip of the Sentient Triangle, they dangle ungrounded and unconnected, with no mission except to embody the role they are given. But sprinkle in memory, tools, and purpose, and something different emerges: a collaborator that’s built not just to talk but to help. As we move up the triangle, the character becomes an assistant (Figure 5.7).

p.span: Moving along the collaborative side of the Sentient Triangle, chat and agent postures overlap, yielding assistants.

level2.p: Silicon Valley has a type. From pitch decks to prototypes, the tech world can’t stop talking about building the perfect assistant, often based on comic book or sci-fi fantasies. There are assistants named after Iron Man’s JARVIS and Batman’s Alfred. We have Ava and Cortana, drawn from the AI of film and video games. Mycroft, the superbrain, comes straight from the pages of both Arthur Conan Doyle and Robert Heinlein. And in a moment of startling honesty, the UK government named their assistant Humphrey, after the charmingly obstructive bureaucrat from Yes, Minister—perhaps the most self-aware branding of all.

The names invoke mythological super-assistants. Always on, always helpful. Fluent in forty languages and unflappable in a crisis. They inevitably speak in either a posh British accent or a sultry synthetic whisper. It’s the nerd fantasy of the ultimate sidekick: part librarian, part armorer, part best friend.

The reality isn’t quite so glamorous. Although real-world assistants are capable of a great deal, much of what they do helps people draft emails, research simple facts, or even just set a timer. It’s practical work.

Assistants are task-focused conversational helpers—entry-level agents with access to grounded knowledge, context, or tools. They sometimes combine language skills with multimodal awareness that lets them see, hear, or infer. While characters play at a role, assistants deliver practical results.

In the Sentient Triangle, assistants straddle both chat and agent experiences because they have “light” agentic skills. We’ll explore agents more deeply in Chapters 7, “Agents and the Manager Experience,” and 8, “Agents of Change,” but briefly: agents act on your behalf. They understand a goal, figure out how to accomplish it, and then take care of it for you. The more agentic the experience, the more it does on its own.

Assistants are junior agents. They support limited delegation of simple tasks but unlike more autonomous agents, assistants remain user-managed. As its name suggests, the assistant posture is always helpful and subordinate. Assistants act only when asked, and the exchange is managed through conversation.

level3.h3: All the Help You Can Get
level3.p: Assistants may be more mundane than their sci-fi namesakes, but they’re still plenty capable—if wildly varied in what they can do. Assistants range across a spectrum of capability in both talking and doing. There are four kinds of assistants, each defined by their mix of task support and language skill (Figure 5.8).

p.span: The four kinds of assistants are defined by their verbal savvy and ability to do tasks or use tools.

list.li:
Data assistants are interactive FAQs swaddled in chat clothing.
These are the descendants of original old-school chatbots, systems that do little more than provide a natural language interface on top of a database or provide scripted service responses to known issues. These unsophisticated bots understand language but aren’t very conversational—tuned to fetch info and limited to what’s in their local database.

Command assistants are digital butlers with “skills.” These skills let the assistant use tools or talk to other systems to play music, set timers, or dim lights—in addition to fetching answers like data assistants. Early command assistants like Alexa were somewhat brittle and required specific words and incantations to trigger actions. Modern versions like the home automation-assistant Josh.ai can understand more complex, open-ended language.

Specialist assistants are single-subject savants, deeply talented in one domain but limited elsewhere. The Harvey assistant, for example, helps lawyers research case law, draft documents, and analyze contracts. Specialist assistants use verbal sophistication to create impressive results when laser-focused on a single specialty rather than trying to do all the things. (Harvey can draft a prenup, but don’t ask it to plan the wedding.)

Universal assistants like ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini can do it all. They are digital polymaths that blend nuanced understanding of language, visuals, and speech with broad-spectrum talents: writing essays, generating code, analyzing data, or explaining esoteric concepts in plain words. They can pivot from creative collaboration to technical problem-solving in the same conversation, making complex capabilities accessible through natural dialogue. While they’re often not as accurate as specialist assistants in those domains, universal assistants tend to be at least adequate at most things, though sometimes much better or worse. With flashes of brilliance one moment and baffling mistakes the next, they still manage to put the idiot in savant.

level3.p: So, which is the right assistant for your application? When you design an assistant experience, choose the capabilities and scope tailored to expectations and context. Table 5.2 offers some pointers.
TABLE 5.2 PICKING THE RIGHT ASSISTANT

td.p: Simple, fast actions like setting timers
Reliable answers from a script or database
Deep, open-ended help in a focused domain
Flexible, expressive support across anything and everything

list.li: What’s the conversation for?
What’s the job to be done?
How will the assistant make that job easier, clearer, and more valuable?

level3.p: For most cases, the specialist assistant is the right call. If you need anything more than a database lookup or a fancy remote control, data or command assistants don’t have enough verbal savvy. On the other hand, most applications don’t require a singing, dancing, all-knowing entity like a universal assistant—and most organizations probably don’t have the resources to build one. A purpose-built assistant is easier to train, keep focused on task, and explain to users.
Use the specialist assistant pattern when you need to understand open-ended input and map it to a defined domain.

Hear Me Out is a specialist assistant created by the National Justice Project in Australia (Figure 5.9). It tackles the unenviable job of fielding the complaints of unhappy Australians: specifically, those who have experienced something unfair or unjust. The assistant makes sense of what happened—from airline complaints to human rights abuses—and directs you to the right government department to get satisfaction. Mapping a route through a specific bureaucracy is a great use case for a specialist assistant.

The assistant’s specialty is made clear through the app’s initial greeting: “If you’ve experienced something unfair, Hear Me Out can guide you to the right place to make your complaint.” From there, the invitation to begin—“Tell us what happened”—helps users understand how to get started. This clear signal for scope, expectation, and behavior is essential for specialist assistants—but harder to deliver in general-purpose experiences like universal assistants.

p.span: Tell the Hear Me Out assistant about a problem or injustice, and it steers you to the agency where you can get help.

level2.h2: When You Can Do Anything … What Do You Do?
level2.p: The great strength of open-ended chat is its flexibility, a single interface that promises to address any topic or question. But it’s also the ultimate blank-canvas problem: full freedom, zero guidance. What can I ask? How should I ask it? Will it work? Introductions are hard, and starting a conversation can be awkward. For better or worse, that’s why the world invented icebreakers.

Nudge prompts are the icebreakers of conversational interfaces. These pre-fab examples offer gentle suggestions for what to ask. They teach by example, using “show don’t tell” to demonstrate what the assistant is good at and how to talk to it. In traditional GUI experiences, nudge prompts appear as links, chips, or buttons; one-click actions to submit a prompt and get things rolling.

div.p: NUDGE PROMPT

p.span: Suggestion for what the user should “say”

div.p: Nudge prompts offer actionable examples to help begin or continue a conversation. Narrow nudges are fully-formed prompts to be triggered as-is. Capability nudges are open-ended cues that advertise broad activities the system can support.

list.li: To help the user get started quickly.
To communicate what the system can do.
To demonstrate how to phrase requests.
To sustain momentum after an initial response.
May become stale or decorative if not tuned to context.
Too many nudges can overwhelm or create choice paralysis.
Narrow nudges may be too specific; capability nudges may feel too vague.

level2.p: Nudge prompts can be very specific—narrow asks that are submitted to the system exactly as presented. The search engine Perplexity uses narrow nudge prompts as a way to demonstrate available content with very precise questions, articles, or stock quotes (Figure 5.10). It’s wildly unlikely that these literal topics are exactly what you’re looking for, but that’s not the point. By surfacing example content, narrow nudge prompts teach the user what kinds of content the interaction can handle, especially ones that might not be obvious at first glance.

p.span: In Perplexity, narrow nudge prompts serve to illustrate range instead of anticipating intent.

level2.p: Capability nudge prompts take a different approach, offering broader functional insight into what an assistant can do. Rather than offering complete but narrow prompts, capability nudges emphasize the activities the system supports—brainstorm, write, code, make images, etc. This approach aligns assistant capability with user need and intent: What are you trying to do?

Some systems blend both types. ChatGPT combines capability nudges with narrow prompts in a two-step process. First, it invites you to identify the kind of problem you’re working on via capability nudges, and then it follows up with narrow nudge prompts that show how to phrase your request more precisely through an autosuggest feature to complete the prompt (Figure 5.11). The best nudge prompts are shaped by past conversations, common tasks, and known interests.

Nudge prompts can also adapt to the user’s request in real time, offering to improve it to get better results. That’s prompt refinement, an interaction pattern that acts as an etiquette coach to help the user improve their conversational skills with the system.

p.span: In ChatGPT, choosing a capability nudge (top) starts to populate the input field (bottom) and then offers narrow nudges in the form of autosuggest completions.

div.p: PROMPT REFINEMENT

p.span: Feedback to help users sharpen requests to the system

div.p: Prompt refinement evaluates prompts and provides suggested alternatives to get better results. The pattern blends input validation (“too vague!”), quick-click selection of alternatives, and real-time education to teach the best ways to “talk” to the system.

list.li: To prevent ambiguous or unsupported prompts that would otherwise result in failure or weak results.
To help the user learn the best way to interact with the system.
Interrupts user flow when it blocks a prompt.
May annoy if not properly framed as helpful.
Requires system to make accurate judgments about prompt quality.

level2.p: Elicit is a research tool for finding and synthesizing academic information. The system works best when the user’s request is specific about the desired results—and because it takes several minutes to complete a task, a bad prompt can be expensive. So, Elicit deploys prompt refinement to reject weak prompts and suggest alternatives (Figure 5.12). The refined suggestions are offered as capability nudges that suggest how the refinement will improve the prompt (specificity, comparison, etc.). Users can accept one of the proposed prompts or simply use them as a guide for crafting their own improved request.

p.span: Elicit blocks vague prompts (top) and offers improved versions (bottom). Hovering over a nudge previews a full prompt suggestion, which the user can accept or edit.

level2.p: Think of nudge prompts as something more than one-time hints. They are a quiet guidance system for better conversation. Over time and through consistent application, these affordances build a picture that reveals the system’s scope and how to work with it.
NOTE TALK BEYOND TEXT

Nudge prompts can keep up when conversation goes beyond text. In speech interfaces, nudge prompts take the form of short-spoken menus. A command assistant like Alexa, for example, might suggest, “You can ask me to play music, set a timer, or tell you the news.” In spatial systems like virtual or augmented reality, nudge prompts might appear as gesture cues, anchored UI panels, or gaze-triggered actions—visible suggestions for what’s possible in the current context.

Starting is only half the challenge. Once the assistant comes up with a response, how that response is framed determines the path of the exchange. Should the user ask a follow-up question? Change direction? Stop entirely?

More than just icebreakers, nudge prompts can be used as continuations—where the assistant suggests next steps or topics in the conversation (Figure 5.13). In this context, nudges provide quick-action options to move things along.

p.span: Nudge prompts can help guide continuing conversation. Here, the assistant for the Oura smart ring suggests topics to continue a conversation about improving sleep habits. Oura’s continuations take the form of both nudge prompts and a question embedded in the text of the!reply.

level2.p: But at this point, the conversation is already in full swing, so continuations can also simply manifest inside the actual text of the assistant’s response as follow-up questions or suggestions. Effective continuation strategies mirror the skills of great conversationalists. The best continuation patterns reflect seven conversational moves that keep dialogue flowing naturally. Use these moves to keep your assistants going (and maybe even use them yourself in a stalled conversation).

list.li: Go deeper: After providing a response, offer to provide more detail or examples.

Refine: It’s rare to get it right the first time, especially for subjective or complex results. Assistants can offer to make adjustments or invite feedback for improvements: “Would you like this to be more technical?” or “Should I focus more on recent developments?” This also performs subtle education about how to work with intelligent interfaces: answers are never fixed, and if the user isn’t satisfied with the first result, they can continue to shape it until they get there.

Pivot: This approach suggests related but different topics, preventing conversational dead-ends by turning down a new road. After answering a question about hiking trails in Yosemite, an assistant might offer info about nearby campgrounds or the weather forecast.

Reflect: Flip the tables by having the assistant prompt the user. The reflect strategy invites the user to process information more deeply by asking questions to tease out facts, perspective, or context. It’s the Socratic method, turning passive information consumption into active participation.

Act: Bridge the gap between talk and tools by offering to act on the results. This strategy surfaces the assistant’s agentic capabilities when they’re most relevant. Based on the tools it can use, the assistant can offer to graph results, update databases, and so on.

Check in: “Is this information helpful? Would you prefer a different approach?” This strategy is valuable when tackling complex topics or when user goals might be evolving—and explicitly invites the user to guide conversation if needed.

Wrap up: All good things come to an end. Wrapping up recognizes when a topic has reached its natural conclusion and provides a satisfying sense of closure. A thoughtful wrap-up might include a brief summary, confirmation that the user’s needs were met, and a gentle sign-off conclusion.

level2.p: Smooth transitions and conversational élan aren’t always enough. Sometimes a conversation goes off the rails through misunderstanding, bad information, or the clumsy realities of human-machine interaction.
You ask Alexa to turn on the lights, and it cheerfully responds, “Playing Christian children’s music” as the stereo blares. Frustration builds, and you mutter something uncouth about “smart” devices.

Misunderstanding isn’t a failure of intelligent systems, but an inevitable feature of them. When two intelligences interact—yours and the machine’s—they are bound to diverge. People have had millennia to develop conversational repair strategies for fixing these moments. We’ve had much less practice with machines—but the strategies aren’t as different as you might think.

In the 1960s, philosopher Paul Grice proposed that conversation depends on four principles: say the right amount (quantity), tell the truth (quality), stay relevant (relation), and speak clearly (manner). Break any of these—and humans do it constantly—and the conversation starts to wobble. That’s just as true with machines.

Assistant conversations tend to fail for a few common reasons, which can all be anticipated and corrected by putting Grice’s principles to work. Table 5.3 shows how smart instructions in the assistant’s system prompt can help save the day (and the dialogue).
TABLE 5.3 CONVERSATIONAL REPAIR STRATEGIES

td.p: “When a request exceeds your scope, acknowledge the goal clearly, explain your limitations, and offer a next-best action.”
“Before responding to complex or multipart queries, summarize what you understand the user to be asking. Confirm or clarify if unclear.”

p.span: The Right Thing, Said the Wrong Way

td.p: “After generating a draft, assess tone, verbosity, and clarity. If the response is overly long, vague, or sycophantic, revise before replying.”
“Maintain awareness of the user’s goal, established facts, and open questions. Regularly re-anchor long conversations by referencing this context.”

level3.p: When the assistant doesn’t have information or ability that the user expected, it’s often the result of poor capability signaling. Grice would suggest repairing expectation misalignment through manner and relation: have the assistant speak clearly and stay relevant. Instead of hiding limitations, signal them early and in context. For example, “I can help you understand this document, but I can’t verify its legal accuracy.” Map the assistant’s knowledge boundaries deliberately so that the assistant can respond when users brush up against them.

list.li: What capabilities are users most likely to assume?
How can the assistant signal limitations earlier in the experience without squashing engagement?
What types of questions fall just outside the system’s knowledge?

level3.p: Sometimes assistants misread intent or context. They latch onto the wrong part of a sentence, miss a nuance, or introduce a bad assumption—and problems snowball from there. Assistants have to listen as well as speak; train them to ask clarifying questions using the “reflect” continuation strategy described earlier.
Reflection prompts instruct the model to summarize its understanding of complex requests before proceeding. These prompts ask the system to paraphrase, restate, or check what it heard before acting.

level3.h3: The Right Thing, Said the Wrong Way
level3.p: The presentation of machine-generated data is just as important as its accuracy. The right message delivered poorly can sometimes do more harm than good, and the robots are often tone deaf. Large language models tend toward overly long answers (violating Grice’s quantity principle) or lean hard into sycophancy, fawning agreement with the user.

Teach your assistant good etiquette by reinforcing Grice’s principles of quantity, quality, and manner. Use system prompts to tell the assistant to speak concisely and avoid flattery. Use post-processing to evaluate responses for tone and length and generate a fresh reframed response if there’s a problem.

Some conversations fail slowly; the early moments go well, but over time the assistant loses the thread. This is context drift, and it’s especially common the longer a conversation continues with machine intelligence.

In a 2025 study by researchers from Microsoft and Salesforce, large language models consistently did worse in multiturn conversations: “LLMs often make assumptions in early turns and prematurely attempt to generate final solutions, on which they overly rely. In simpler terms, we discover that when LLMs take a wrong turn in a conversation, they get lost and do not recover.”

Researchers tied the problem to the models’ tendency for long-winded responses, which bury important information and make it harder for the model to refer back to what matters in future turns. It starts with a violation of Grice’s quantity principle, which in turn affects quality.

Without intentional conversation repair, these assistants will continue wandering in the wrong direction. Reflection prompts can help to seek clarification in ambiguity. But also, as conversations continue, implement grounding turns that pause to recap understanding before continuing: “Just to review: your main goal is [X], and so far we’ve established [Y and Z]. Should we keep going in this direction?”
Even with prevention and repair strategies like these, context drift is tough to counter because it’s inherent to how LLMs work—and the fact that they have no real memory.

level2.p: The man wakes up … no idea where he is … or even who he is. But there are clues: notes tattooed on his skin, scribbled on Polaroids, scrawled in his own handwriting. They tell him who he is, what to trust, what to do next.

That’s the premise of Memento—a cult-classic Hollywood thriller whose hero has no memory; he only ever knows the present and the past few minutes. To survive, he outsources memory to physical artifacts. He refers to them constantly—to rebuild the past and anchor the present he’s about to forget.

Modern chat assistants work the same way. Their conversations seem like a flowing conversation, but in reality, the model remembers nothing that was said before. In technical terms, large language models are stateless: every turn is a clean slate.

To create the illusion of continuity, assistant applications use a Memento-style trick called prompt stuffing. Every time you send a message, the system also sends itself reminders: the full transcript of your past exchanges, the assistant’s replies, images or documents you shared in the conversation. Even the system prompt is repeated every time—instructions for how the assistant is supposed to act and what it’s supposed to do.

Most users think of the prompt as the message they just typed. But under the hood, the prompt is this entire, ever-growing payload of backstory that gets resent, reread, and reprocessed on every turn. The longer the conversation, the bigger the payload gets with every message you send.

The hack has a limit called the context window, the model’s maximum capacity for how much it can process at once. If the conversation gets too big for the window, older messages get dropped to make room for new ones. The conversation drifts as the system “forgets” bits of what you just said.

This limitation turns memory into a design challenge. What do you preserve? What can you drop? Smart strategies for truncation, prioritization, and summarization become essential to keep the conversation coherent, even as the past is constantly pruned.

To go further, some assistants approximate long-term memory by stashing observations about you across sessions—a practice known as persistent memory. This unlocks a stronger sense of continuity, even across weeks or months of interaction.

Persistent memory is stored in a database, and the system can query it to find facts that are relevant to the current conversation—and then stuff those into the prompt payload for each turn. It makes the context window even more crowded, but with the payoff of knowledge sharing across conversations.
NOTE DIGITAL TWINS

Persistent memory is a simplified version of a deeper architecture known as a digital twin. The term is borrowed from engineering and industry, where virtual machines simulate their physical counterparts. In digital products, a digital twin models the user—their preferences, behaviors, goals, and context—in order to anticipate what the user wants or needs.

All of this means that we’re packing several layers of memory into every turn (Figure 5.14). The system prompt defines the assistant’s core identity. Persistent memory stores long-term knowledge. The conversation transcript provides short-term memory within the session. And the user’s latest message is the immediate request that sets what’s next into motion.

p.span: Like the Memento character who tattoos himself with everything he knows to simulate memory, chat assistants repeat every relevant detail to themselves with every turn of the conversation. Here, when the user answers a question by saying “Kitty loves clumps,” the system passes along the entire transcript, all its memories, and the original system prompt, too.

level2.p: This hacked-together memory system has experience implications that require some user literacy to navigate. Why is the assistant forgetting something I told it just a few minutes ago? Why is it asking the same question again? Why am I bumping into usage limits so quickly?
When memory seems to fail, it means the system is brushing up against invisible limits the user can’t see. Help the user by making these limits clear:

list.li: New topics work better with a new context. Offer to start a fresh conversation when a new topic is introduced.

Assistants get forgetful when they max out their context window. When the conversation no longer fits in memory, say so, and provide options for parts of the conversation to focus on. The earliest “memories” might be more important than the most recent ones.
Long conversations run into limits more quickly. Give a heads-up as limits approach.
Persistent memory works by identifying and saving meaningful observations. Alert the user with a gentle notification anytime a memory is saved.

level2.p: The fact that these caveats are necessary shows that this brute-force method of faking memory isn’t exactly elegant, but it works well enough to impose continuity onto a system that wouldn’t otherwise have it.

And yet memory isn’t the only kind of continuity that matters. Beyond time and recall, successful conversations fit into a continuity of mode and medium, too—the whole variety of speech, sight, images, documents, and interaction channels.
Conversation has never relied on words alone. People communicate in a glorious tangle of methods: speech, gestures, tone, doodles, text, photos, video, scribbles—even silence.

People are multimodal. We make meaning through combinations of signals and expressions. Machine intelligence has grown impressively nimble at understanding these individual expressions—and how to mix and match them, too. Multimodal assistants bend presentation to context, just like people switch communication style to fit the moment.

Flex user input by accepting whatever form is most convenient. Any medium becomes a way in to help the user express their need. Let users share a screenshot, a spreadsheet, or a photo of a confusing street sign. Let them switch to speech when they’re driving or doing the dishes—or sketch a doodle when the words don’t come.

Bring the same flexibility to output. Assistants can communicate through speech, video, charts, expressive mini-apps, or offer GUI interface elements. Train the assistant to find the best way to communicate this information in that context.

Input and output don’t have to match. Asymmetric modes mean the user can “speak” in one mode while the assistant answers in another. Speak a messy question, get back a clean chart. Upload a dense document, get a concise audio summary.

To enable this behavior, the assistant has to be smart enough to discern the context of both the user and the information. Happily, large language models excel at sorting both kinds of context. They can interpret the user’s intent and status to sort out the right manner of response—and interpret the information to provide it in a contextually appropriate format for best presentation. LLMs are especially good at balancing these layers—user, content, and medium—into a response that is coherent across all three. (Here, the job of the designer is to give the system cues for how to match response to intent, something we’ll dig into in the next chapter.)

Similarly, an assistant can live in different points of interaction. As a digital presence, an assistant has no fixed home; as designer, you can attach the assistant to whatever surface makes the most sense. The ChatGPT assistant is available via app or website, of course; but it’s also available via toll-free phone call. Through smart speakers or earbuds, assistants can become room-level ambient, visiting whatever space you’re in. Be open and curious about new interaction surfaces and the new use cases they enable.

When Turing proposed his imitation game, he couldn’t have imagined computers playing along by phone or video conference. “The ideal arrangement is to have a teleprinter communicating,” he wrote. He imagined computers chatting through printed word.

Multimodal assistants demonstrate that chat can go far beyond chatbots and certainly beyond Turing’s teleprinter, as multimodal assistants demonstrate. But we can push this even further: intelligent interfaces that talk without speaking.

Presence, meaning, and smarts can be communicated by action, too—nonverbal communication that nevertheless remains conversational in spirit. That’s where we go next: chat that transcends dialogue to create new kinds of digital experience.

level1.p: Chat Is Not All Talk

What is the body language of a user interface? The last chapter explored chat as dialogue, where the system mimics human conversation. But conversation doesn’t end with words—a raised eyebrow or sideways glance packs plenty of meaning.

Conversation is rich with unspoken language, and the same has been true of user interfaces ever since they escaped the command line. Graphical interfaces “speak” in icons; game controllers alert with haptic feedback; and touch interfaces embed meaning in the bounce of every interaction.
There’s more to chat than chatter. In interfaces as in life, sometimes the smartest thing is knowing when not to talk.

level2.p: The chat posture positions the intelligent interface as a peer in a turn-based exchange. Take away the dialogue, and “conversation” becomes a string of actions and reactions. Chess and checkers are conversations in this sense, bounded by rules of very different complexity yet following the same dynamic: a rich exchange unfolds on the “interface” of the game board.

Three chat experience patterns similarly embed turn-based conversation into the interface: non-player characters (NPCs), bespoke UIs, and intelligent canvases (Table 6.1). Together, these three body-language patterns round out the chat posture alongside the sculptor, character, and assistant patterns from previous chapters.
TABLE 6.1 CHAT’S “BODY LANGUAGE” EXPERIENCE PATTERNS

td.p: User-like presence in a shared interface
Shape-shifting interface conceived in real time
Manifesters of user ideas and intent

level2.p: The “languages” spoken by these new chat experiences are each quite different, and we’ll explore them in turn. In all three, dialogue takes a backseat to action.

These patterns reach toward the Sentient Triangle’s "interoperable" corner, where chat experiences take action through external services (Figure 6.1). NPCs and intelligent canvases line up on the triangle’s collaborative edge where chat meets the agent posture. Bespoke UIs balance all three pillars, landing near the triangle’s center where they overlap with copilots (more on those in Chapter 9, “Copilots and Uncharted Territory”).
This is perhaps the strangest and most creative region of the Sentient Triangle. When exploring strange territory, nothing beats a local guide—ours has been greeting newcomers for years.

p.span: The three “body language” chat patterns cluster along the “collaborative” side of the chat posture where they overlap the agent and copilot postures.

level2.p: “Yes, yes! Welcome, everyone! Welcome to your new island paradise!” Tom Nook’s cheerful greeting has welcomed millions of Animal Crossing players over the years (Figure 6.2). The raccoon shopkeeper gets you started in the game, and throughout he’s always ready with a home loan or an oddly insistent lesson in capitalism. More than that, he’s a fellow inhabitant of your town who participates in the same economy and interaction systems that players do. He feels like another player, even though he’s clearly part of the game system itself.

p.span: Tom Nook is an automated character in the Animal Crossing game, serving as the host and facilitator who drives the game’s progression and economy.

level2.p: Non-player characters (NPCs) like Tom have been a staple of computer games since the earliest interactive text adventures. They’re the automated fictional characters who guide, add structure, or just make mischief. Throughout, they present as peers in the game, “players” that act within the game’s social and interactive rules.
In Sentient Design, the NPC pattern applies this posture beyond play. It’s an experience model where machine intelligence drives its own user account within collaborative environments.

NPCs are collaborators that act as “just another user” in a shared environment, using the same tools and channels as everyone else. While characters and assistants operate as separate conversational destinations, NPCs come to you. They slot into an existing multiuser interface as fellow users. Instead of talking to an assistant, you start working alongside another digital participant. The NPC shares the common space, embodied as a user account.

NPC interaction typically operates within tight constraints and roles, much like their gaming counterparts. These are not free-range autonomous agents, which we’ll address in the next chapter. That mix of constraint, capability, and familiar interaction cultivates both predictability and trust.

level3.p: NPCs fit the space they inhabit. The etiquette and mechanics of the environment all determine how NPCs should appear and the roles they can play. As a designer, you don’t begin with the idea of a “bot” but with the interaction patterns of the space itself. If a “user” account could be operated by the system, how would it behave, and how would it support other users?
There are five core categories of multiuser experiences, each with its own distinct opportunities to “embody” NPCs as users in the space.

level4.p: The chat room is the simplest and most familiar space for NPC collaboration. In group chats or Slack channels, messenger NPCs answer questions, surface updates, or prompt conversation with their own questions.

These bots excel at communication and coordination. Their strength lies in their fluency with the rhythms and rituals of chat. They can post in the same range of media—text, links, GIFs, videos—as other users. They can have a general-purpose conversational presence, or they can have specific roles (a meeting assistant that recaps decisions and to-dos, a citation bot that jumps in with relevant links during a conversation, etc.).

Whatever their mission, a messenger NPC needs a name, avatar, and tone that clearly establish their identity and role. Most messenger NPCs are activated with direct mentions (“@SentientBot, what’s the agenda?”) or kicked off by context-specific triggers like time (“It’s 10 AM—ready for stand-up?”) or activity (“Looks like that pull request was merged—want me to notify the team?”).

The linear, temporal structure of messaging favors brief, self-contained contributions—not lingering presence. Avoid flooding the channel with constant updates. Instead, summarize, batch, and provide links to more detail. Let users control frequency, relevance, and tone—and offer a simple way to snooze or mute the NPC entirely.
All of this makes messenger NPCs fairly reactive. NPCs come into their own when they join users in the work itself.

The NPC pattern is powerful because it weaves intelligence into familiar interactions instead of asking the user to step into a separate interface. Inline editor NPCs show up right where you work to make live edits or suggestions, their presence represented by their trail of activity.

Pointer.ai, for example, is a browser extension that lets you ask for revisions in Google Docs, and the edits appear right where you work with strike-throughs and insertions to accept or reject. It relies on the built-in “tracked changes” feature of the word processor, offering feedback in the same form and place that you’d get it from any other collaborator.

Other examples include coding assistants, where inline editor NPCs propose updates by sharing before/after diffs showing the changes to make. Grammar and style checkers also become NPCs when they participate through comments and suggestions rather than one-shot tooltips.

Just like working with human editors, it’s confusing (and a little rude) for NPCs to fiddle with passages that users are working on. Avoid jumping in mid-keystroke and contribute only after the human editor has paused or explicitly requested feedback.

Inline editor NPCs should always suggest by default, letting the user accept or reject proposed changes. And they don’t have to jump straight to solutions, either; instead of providing answers, NPCs can also use comments to ask questions that prompt reflection. The road goes two ways; users can post requests or questions to the NPC in document comments via @mentions, as they would with colleagues.

In the 2010s, the shared canvas metaphor totally reframed what it means to work together. Instead of documents you pass around, apps like Figma and Mural introduced wide-open thinking spaces for teams to work together. By making the workspace spatial and visual, these collaborative whiteboards shift how people think and make—and how NPCs should interact.

Cursor NPCs most often participate in shared canvases by organizing or clustering canvas elements, tidying naming conventions, or helping to synthesize what’s been created. But it’s the physical metaphor of the space that makes cursor NPCs very different from messengers or inline editors. These NPCs are more visibly embodied than their counterparts. They move through the space with physical presence, and the experience needs to reflect both the fact and feel of that motion.

Whiteboard app Miro has NPC “users” called sidekicks that you can summon to participate in a board like a human user. When you ask “Product Leader” or “Product Marketer” to analyze your brainstorming session, the sidekick doesn’t respond with a separate chat interface or generated report. Instead, like Tom Nook in his shop, it participates directly in the workspace. The NPC sidekick adds sticky notes and leaves comments just like any other colleague might (Figure 6.3).

p.span: Miro’s sidekicks are NPCs that show up as cursors in the interface, just like human users, to add sticky notes and comments to the board.

level4.p: It starts with giving them a place to come from and return to. Consider a “dock” or home zone in the interface—a stable visual anchor where cursor NPCs idle when inactive and visibly retreat when their task is done. These visual conventions communicate what the NPC is up to and when.

You can use motion and micro-animation to reinforce personality and status. A subtle floating hover says, “I’m watching,” while a quick dart and drop might suggest “task complete.” These gestural cues replace words and chat—they are the body language of shared space. Pacing matters here: cursor NPCs should appear briefly, then idle or exit until summoned again. Lingering in the workspace can feel cluttered or invasive.

Summoning should be deliberate and contextual. Cursor NPCs can be summoned through buttons (“Cluster” to organize a selection of stickies) or context-specific triggers like board activity or thresholds (“You’ve just added a dozen notes—want me to group them?”).

Not every cursor NPC needs to do everything. In fact, it’s better if they don’t. Shared canvases often benefit from giving NPCs specific, narrow roles—a specialty like organization, or a topical expertise. The name of the cursor NPC should crisply reflect its role. This modular, narrow approach to tasks clarifies the NPC’s purpose to users in a canvas environment that can already be messy.

Avatar NPCs take the form of virtual bodies in 3D worlds from Minecraft or Roblox games to sophisticated training simulations. In VR meeting tools like Virbela, NPCs participate as greeters to guide participants to the right space. Meanwhile, in healthcare simulator SimX, NPC patients present not only physical symptoms but emotional range, helping medical staff practice bedside manner as much as technique (Figure 6.4).

p.span: In VR simulator SimX, NPCs help medical staff train for specific medical and social scenarios. Here, an NPC simulates a patient recovering from a tracheotomy.

level4.p: In simulated worlds, embodiment is everything. An avatar NPC should have a place to stand, a way to move, and a reason to be there. NPCs in these spaces should be spatially grounded to areas or objects aligned with the NPC’s role and behavior. SimX patients tend to stay in bed, for example, and Minecraft shopkeepers hover near their shop. When idle, avatar NPCs might pace gently or shift from foot to foot. When engaged, they move with deliberation. These physical anchors help users interpret the NPC’s role and purpose through context clues.

A little goes a long way with avatar NPCs. Consider them situational participants that should appear only when needed to perform a well-defined role. They are summoned by proximity, predictable events, or explicit invitation.

Simulated worlds are one thing, but the real world is the ultimate multiuser environment. (Earth has eight billion concurrent users!) When machine intelligence steps into the physical world, that’s when the robots arrive—intelligent devices that move in the same space as the rest of us.

It’s still early days for sophisticated mainstream robots. Some homes have vacuum-cleaning robots, and self-driving cars like Waymo robotaxis have become the driving NPCs of a few cities. We’re likely to see AI continue to inhabit more of the physical world in the near future. Until then, machine intelligence is already joining physical gatherings in a more familiar way: through telepresence.

Telepresent NPCs beam into a room in the very same way people do through video or audio. These NPCs join a meeting via video conference, for example, appearing on-screen to manage the agenda, answer questions, or represent a specific point of view. A “User Advocate” NPC might join a meeting to reference research insights during a discussion. A “Sprint Coach” can join daily stand-ups to help plan the day.

These NPCs show up with the presence of a full participant: a talking head with speech and the ability to use collaboration tools. They can mark up a shared whiteboard, “share their screen” by broadcasting visuals, or use the camera to respond to facial expression.

Go gently on the realism. Platforms like HeyGen and Akool offer surprisingly lifelike interactive avatars that can handle the back and forth of an online meeting (Figure 6.5). Although these avatars have a thick veneer of the uncanny, be sure to make it unmistakably clear that these telepresent NPCs are artificial. They should introduce themselves as AI, get consent to participate, and maintain clear labeling of their nature.

p.span: “Judy” is an interactive avatar on the HeyGen platform. Here, Judy delivers a training program by providing information and quizzing participants.

level4.p: You can make it even clearer by skipping realism altogether. Telepresent NPCs can get their jobs done as cartoons (like Duolingo’s Lily) or even shape-shifting abstract representations. Just because NPCs work alongside people doesn’t oblige them to look like people, too.

Whatever the NPC’s form, manners matter more than ever. Telepresent NPCs need to navigate the subtle social choreography of messy group meetings—the pauses, interruptions, side conversations, and turn-taking that keeps conversation flowing. NPCs should use familiar meeting signals for attention—such as raising their hand or using text chat to ask for a turn. These explicit signals work better than trying to read the room, and they give other participants clear control over when the NPC contributes.

list.li: Which collaborative environments does your product support, and how could NPCs participate as natural users within them?
What existing interaction patterns could NPCs leverage to avoid introducing new mental models?
How will you ensure that NPCs are helpful participants rather than intrusive interruptions?
What signals will make the NPC’s identity and activity transparent to human users?

level4.p: Done right, NPCs feel like natural extensions of the team—collaborative, helpful, and respectful of the space they share with human users. A few design principles help make it so.

list.li: Trigger with intention. NPCs should never appear unbidden. Every interaction should begin with a clear, intentional trigger from users—whether that’s an @mention, a specific command, or a scheduled action. Miro’s sidekicks, for example, remain dormant until explicitly summoned by a button click, and Pointer.ai activates only when directly addressed. This stands in sharp contrast to Microsoft’s Clippy, which interrupted users with unsolicited suggestions. NPCs should exit the stage when they’re finished. Temporary presence keeps the interface focused and uncluttered.

Suggest, don’t act. NPCs should be deferential. Avoid outright replacement or other destructive action. Make NPC-generated additions obvious and easy to review, accept, or reject. All changes should be reviewable (and reversible) through the same mechanisms used by everyone else in the system.
Match personality to purpose. Don’t overdo the “character” part of non-player characters. The personality and tone of NPCs should align with their environment and purpose.

Be transparent about NPC activity. When the system participates as a user, things get confusing when the UI isn’t clear about what’s happening. The user should never wonder, “Did the system do this, or another person, or wait … was it me?” The same rules apply here as in any other multiuser environment: Be clear where NPC content came from; offer clear indication of NPC actions in progress; and include a history of NPC contributions.

Stick to UI conventions. NPCs should work through existing interface patterns instead of introducing new ones. Give NPCs the same look and feel of other accounts; when Miro’s sidekicks add comments or stickies, their cursor avatar swoops across the canvas just like regular user accounts. Communication and content suggestion should happen through the same channels used by others. The goal is seamless integration that adapts to familiar mental models instead of requiring new ones.

level4.p: These principles work together to create NPCs that feel like a natural part of collaborative environments. NPCs help users stay in the game, not shake them out of it.

Sometimes, though, the answer isn’t to blend into the space but to rearrange it. That’s when machine intelligence shifts from inhabiting a single player to inhabiting the whole playing field. Let’s see what happens when we let AI shape the interface itself.

level3.p: Bespoke UIs are shape-shifting interfaces conceived in real time—composing layout, content, or controls in direct response to changing user context. This flips the traditional story: instead of the user adapting to a fixed structure, the structure adapts to the user. We introduced bespoke UI in Chapter 1, “Intelligent Interfaces,” with examples like Salesforce dashboards that reconfigure themselves, and a Gemini chat that responds with expressive UI components instead of plain text. Rather than making you clamber through a fixed information architecture to find what you need, bespoke UI brings the content and interface to you.

Like all chat patterns, you “talk” to the system through turn-based exchange. You send a command, ask a question, or send a signal, which could be an explicit click or a change in behavior. Bespoke UI responds by morphing the layout or content to support your new direction or anticipate what you need. This looks nothing like traditional dialogue, but the interaction is fundamentally conversational.

This sounds destabilizing. With no fixed foundation, how can someone find their footing? Even if the architecture is no longer fixed across the experience, a good implementation of bespoke UI delivers layouts that feel cohesive even as they’re ever-changing. As you’ll see in a moment, they do this by using established patterns and design systems.

In fact, because it’s so connected to those patterns and systems, bespoke UI tends to be more grounded and interoperable than other chat experiences. That’s why you’ll find this pattern mapped near the center of the Sentient Triangle, straddling both chat and copilot (refer back to Figure 6.1).

Bespoke UI can also show up with a lighter or heavier touch, depending on context. At the light end, bespoke layering inserts custom elements into an otherwise static experience. Call and response UI gets more radical by providing a conversational interface that “speaks” in UI instead of dialogue. And fully bespoke UI creates complete experiences with every turn.

One of the world’s most-used interfaces has quietly schooled all of us in the fundamentals of bespoke UI for years: the Google search results page. As early as 2007, Google began introducing on-the-go customization of search results by sprinkling bespoke components among its 10 blue links. Search for weather, and a weather UI component leads the results. A food search stirs in a recipe carousel with a dash of image grid. Every search changes the results page ever so slightly to help you find what you’re looking for.
Beyond the search results themselves, machine intelligence is working hard on the presentation: interpreting intent, selecting UI modules, and personalizing based on location or other cues.

This is bespoke layering, an iterative first step to adding context-aware inline elements into existing structure. Keep the reliable bones and established information architecture but add custom additions throughout. The walls stay fixed while you bring in new furniture to fit the moment.

This approach is ideal when you prefer a more conservative approach to intelligent interfaces. Bespoke layering lets you create a stable, opinionated experience while carving out specific moments or regions for individualized updates tuned to immediate context.

list.li: How could targeted individualization elevate an otherwise static experience that you’re creating?
What signals would inform that adaptability and what would they tell you (and the system) about the user’s goals and needs?
What specific content or interactions would best meet those goals and needs? How could they best supplement and complement the existing base architecture?
Should these additions be inline, or off to the side? Loud or quiet?

level3.p: With bespoke layering, the chat posture is quiet and subtle. It responds to the user, but only within the context of a largely fixed experience. Other kinds of bespoke UI take a more directly conversational approach.

“Ask Amplitude” is a system that banters with you in charts and graphs. It’s a spin-off of the powerful Amplitude product analytics platform that lets you bypass that tool’s sophisticated controls and filters—and just ask for what you need. A product manager can ask, “Compare conversion rates for the new checkout flow versus the old one,” and the assistant responds with a chart to show the data. Ask a follow-up question and Ask Amplitude answers with a new kind of chart to continue the story (Figure 6.6).

That’s call and response UI, where the system responds to an explicit action—a question, UI interaction, or event trigger—with a bite-sized interface specifically tailored to the request. It could be a map, an audio snippet, a video clip, a web form … whatever the system deems as the most efficient way to answer or get more info.

p.span: Amplitude’s call and response UI lets you chat with charts, with all responses coming back as bespoke data visualizations.

level3.p: Cisco’s AI Canvas, for example, helps IT teams find and fix network issues. Ask it to look into a server issue, and AI Canvas gets started by delivering a handful of graphs in formats selected on the fly to fit the uncovered data. Continue posing additional questions, and the system replies with new tables and charts based on the asks. Drag the ones you find most helpful into a canvas to curate them into a custom dashboard (Figure 6.7). It’s a conversational partner that speaks UI, but also a virtual vending machine for dashboard building blocks.

p.span: Cisco’s AI Canvas chats in charts in its left sidebar and lets you save the ones you want by dragging them into your own custom dashboard (right).

level3.p: These examples show how call and response UI adapts to explicit questions or commands. When you instead hook it up to implicit contextual signals, you get a streaming feed of just-in-time information. Imagine an AI marketing assistant that listens in on sales calls, providing relevant info throughout the conversation. When the client mentions “Acme Corp,” the interface surfaces a competitor comparison chart. When pricing comes up, a dynamic pricing module shows packages and past discounts.

Call and response UI is kind of like traditional chat, except that the language model doesn’t reply directly to the user—it replies to the UI engine, giving it instructions for how to present the response. Let’s see how all of this works, and how design fits in.

Until recently, it was really, really hard for systems to determine intent from natural language or other cues. Hard-coded rules and simple keyword matching made early interpretation systems brittle and overly rigid. Even when systems understood the literal words you said, they might not know what to do with them if it wasn’t part of the script.
