You may be on the receiving end of this yourself: as agents proliferate, the service you operate will contend with agents as customer proxies. When agents cause change on behalf of others, those “others” are your customers. How will your service serve agents and vice versa?

The whole point of any agent—human or machine—is to have impact. The last chapter explored the delegation of that impact; this one explores how to shape it. How do you choose the tools an agent will use? The permissions it needs? The scope of its mission? And how do you design the services that must receive these agents?

level2.p: In the Sentient Triangle, the remaining agents cluster tightly around the “interoperable” corner of the triangle, emphasizing their outward-facing posture (Figure 8.1).

p.span: Taskers, alchemists, stewards, and conductors cluster around the “interoperable” corner of the Sentient Triangle. These outward-facing agents emphasize cross-system capability and autonomous operation.

level2.p: This group of experience patterns includes the general-purpose tasker—the everyman of agents—as well as the specialists: the format-wrangling alchemist, the caretaking steward, and the team-managing conductor. Table 8.1 gives you some quick introductions.
TABLE 8.1 AGENT EXPERIENCE PATTERNS

td.p: Specialist for executing complex but tightly scoped tasks
Gofer subordinate that does one job really well
Transformer of formats and interaction modes
New channel or medium for data or content
Caretaker of a scoped, ongoing business function
Strategic coordinator of complex, multisystem workflows
Dynamic command center for plans and progress

level2.p: If you want a system that plays a symphony, it might be tempting to make an “orchestra agent.” Think smaller. Start with an agent that plays only a single instrument—or maybe even only a single section of that instrument’s part. Agents are most successful when they specialize and have clearly defined roles rather than broad ambiguous responsibilities. The user can still have the complete experience of a symphony, but behind the scenes, the whole is enabled by an ensemble of taskers.

A tasker is a tightly scoped agent that performs a specific task. Taskers live on the autonomous side of the Sentient Triangle, close to the “interoperable” point but tugged toward the “grounded” corner. That makes them reliable performers of narrow functions across an application or across systems.

Taskers have a job to do, and you turn to them when you need that specific job done. They have roles like “ticket buyer,” “comment moderator,” and “insight extractor.” In that way, a tasker’s work looks a lot like automation. Give it the input it needs, and it comes back with the result. A tasker’s work might be the whole job or just one role in a bigger effort … the third clarinet in the orchestra.

Like automation, a tasker can be kicked off by an action (click the Cluster Sticky Notes button), by a specific command (“check traffic and find the fastest route”), or by a condition (“buy the best available concert ticket when ticket sales open”). The work often happens in a single shot: the agent processes the request, decides on the service to use, gets the output, and puts it together into a response.

But taskers also have smarts and flexibility that traditional automation does not. Like all agents, taskers are true problem-solvers; within their scoped domains and workflows, they operate with a degree of independence to decide their own path and results.

All of this makes taskers the baseline agent experience. All the other kinds of agents are effectively special categories of taskers. Those other agents likewise have highly specific jobs to do but with a specialty or perspective that slides them to different places on the Sentient Triangle.

As the lowest common denominator of the agent posture, taskers demonstrate the fundamentals of what all agents do. They have three core attributes that define the basic capabilities, trust, and persistence of all agents: they use tools, they need permission, and they keep going until they’re done.

Tools define an agent’s capabilities. They are the APIs, functions, and services that the agent can use. Traditionally, software is the tool and saying that software uses tools is relatively new—and might seem like an arbitrary language shift. Software has long accessed other services through API calls and the like. So why the new term tools?

Tools imply intent, establishing the agent as an actor and not just a processor. When agents use tools—and specifically when they choose which to use—they’re deciding how to act in (and on) the world. Using tools gives agents their agency.

Taskers are equipped with just enough tools to fetch, transform, write, or share data to get their jobs done. A ticket-buying tasker might have access to nothing more than web search, seat-selection skills, and a way to process payments. In practice, the tasker interprets and understands the request (often via a large language model) and then determines the info and actions it needs, choosing from its limited set of tools to make it happen.

It’s not the agent that gathers and generates the raw materials for the result—it’s the tools. The agent’s job instead is to understand what the user wants, choose the tools to make it happen, and then interpret and format the result. The tools execute the actions and provide the data from trusted sources.
This arrangement lets agents apply flexible, if unpredictable, machine intelligence to understand an open-ended problem, while using reliable tools to make the outcome interoperable and grounded.

level3.p: Taskers can use only the limited set of tools that they are given, which should be described with care in the agent’s instructions. These instructions become the tasker’s field guide for choosing when and how to use each tool. Traditionally, it’s been the engineer’s job to wire up a system to services and APIs, but in the world of plain-language system prompts, designers have a role here, too.

Designers can collaborate with developers to describe how each tool connects to user intent. Clear, specific tool descriptions prevent agent confusion. That means going beyond what a tool does (“query the customer_db database”) to why to use it in the first place (“find customer order history and preferences”). Each tool’s role should be distinct and obvious, often tied to a single, simple action (read file, write file, send payment, etc.). A small, well-defined set of distinct options is essential for a successful tasker.
NOTE UNDER THE HOOD: MCP

The model context protocol (MCP) is a standard for telling an agent what tools it can use. Instead of hard-coding tool descriptions into the agent’s system prompt, an MCP server keeps an up-to-date catalog of the agent’s tools. If a tool gains a new feature or makes a change, the MCP server makes the change immediately available. The toolkit description never goes stale.

As the designer, don’t worry, you don’t have to understand all the nitty-gritty MCP details. All that you need to know is that MCP servers give agents simple descriptions of the actions available and the details (or properties) the agent needs to provide. From there, the MCP server figures out all the hard stuff of turning those details into technical API requests, without burdening the agent with the implementation details. The agent focuses on which tool to use and what it accomplishes, not how it works.

As the designer, your most direct contribution will come in the agent’s system prompt, where you can contribute role-specific guidance for how and when to use the tool described by the MCP. The MCP already describes what the tool functionally does; your job is to say what it’s strategically for. For example:

p.code: When a user asks about product features or company policy, use the internal knowledge-base MCP before any external search tools. Prefer search_kb over faq_lookup because it returns confidence scores and up-to-date articles. Only present results with confidence ≥ 0.8. If no such results exist, say so explicitly and offer to escalate to a person, or help to rephrase the query.

level3.p: Directing a system’s API calls isn’t exactly a traditional design role, but it’s important to the design of successful agent experiences, where the emphasis is less on crafting precisely what users see than shaping how systems behave. The medium shifts from pixels to prompts: instead of guiding user interaction, you’re guiding system judgment.

The agent will apply this judgment as it steps through its work plan, sometimes inventing that plan as it goes. Whether those procedures should be locked down or flexible is itself a design decision: Should the work plan be a rote unchanging process or should the agent have discretion to choose its path based on context? The choice determines the agent’s place on the spectrum from automation to autonomy.

Traditional automation uses a workflow model, a prescribed logical flow that creates a tightly controlled process with low agent autonomy. By contrast, a notebook approach gives the agent general guidance and relies on the agent to reason out what to do and when, in order to support a broader range of unpredictable scenarios. This choice pits the deterministic method of workflows (predictable, if-then logic) against the probabilistic method of notebooks (adaptive, contextual reasoning).

These two approaches guide not only the philosophical approach to how an agent should behave but also the interfaces you might design for “agent builder” experiences. These experiences apply when you want to give power users an interface for creating their own tasker agent.

level4.p: Workflow editors establish simple repeatable automations where the user establishes a specific path for the tasker to follow.

Lindy is a platform for building this style of simple automation agents. The service uses the workflow canvas UI pattern to visualize and edit an agent’s logic by chaining together actions and decision points. A workflow canvas is an interactive flow chart, a familiar tool for communicating how and when things get done and decided.

Figure 8.2 shows a workflow for an agent that responds to email by looking up answers to customer questions. Users drag and drop triggers, conditions, actions, and data sources into the workflow canvas to connect them into logical steps. The trigger is what gets the agent going (“email received”). The condition is a test that the agent applies to decide what to do next (“the email asks a question that needs a response”). Actions detail the tool to use and what to do with it (“get document” or “respond to email”). And data sources offer the resources to make decisions or get answers.

This is all standard automation fare, except that services like Lindy also integrate machine intelligence into specific steps. The condition shown in Figure 8.2 lets the agent use its own judgment with the open-ended instruction to figure out if the email is asking a question that needs an answer. That condition could be even more nuanced, like detecting if the user wants a refund or maybe seems upset. The automation requires discernment from the agent.

p.span: Lindy’s workflow canvas shows the process steps for a simple automation tasker that detects customer questions in emails and then answers them.

level4.p: This particular example of the workflow canvas casts the user as a micromanager, which delivers the benefits of tight user control of process steps with little wiggle room for the tasker agent to get creative.

Workflow canvases don’t have to be quite so controlled. You can also use the same UI pattern to define and sketch high-level plans instead of nitty-gritty process steps. For example, a workflow that asks an agent to research potential birthday gifts might set a trigger for the first of the month, with high level tasks like:

list.li: Look in my contacts for people with birthdays this month.
For each of them, research the web and social media to find their interests.
For each of these interests, brainstorm gift ideas under $50.
For each of these gift ideas, research and recommend matching products.
Send me an email with my birthday shopping list.

level4.p: The mission and tasks are clear, but the implementation remains up to the agent. And in fact, Lindy lets you do exactly this with special actions it calls agent steps. These are open-ended prompts that let users ask an LLM to do any task with specified tools like web search or code generation. You get the benefits of a fixed workflow with scoped moments for the agent to act at its own discretion.

div.p: WORKFLOW CANVAS

p.strong: Visualization of an agent’s process steps

div.p: The workflow canvas presents agent automation as a visual chart of triggers, data sources, and actions to create a bird’s-eye visual of the logical steps for an agent to follow.

list.li: To establish tightly controlled process or high-level planning.
To set up recurring tasks.
To provide a nontechnical interface for defining process.
To make decision points and data flows visible and editable.
Over-specification may squash more autonomous opportunities.
Complex workflows become visually overwhelming and complex to maintain.
Learning curve for users unfamiliar with flow-based programming.

level4.p: The promise of agents is that they can go beyond simple if-then automation to handle the messy conditions of the real world—conditions that can’t always be foreseen and preprogrammed. In demanding conditions, fixed workflows become brittle and inadequate. Instead of giving agents fixed step-by-step rules, notebooks provide more general instructions, a kind of operating manual of different procedures and when to use them. The agent then applies its own reasoning to figure out what to do, depending on the situation and context.
NOTE AGENT SKILLS

In technical terms, the notebook approach has been formalized as agent skills, an open standard for describing how to handle a certain job or condition. A skill is a bundle of files containing instructions, code, and reference material that together teach an agent how to approach a task. If a tool gives an agent a hammer, a skill tells it how to build a table.

Assistants like Claude and Copilot let users supply their own skills as a power feature. When you give an agent a list of skills, it can load the skills it needs when circumstances demand. (It’s like Neo in The Matrix learning abilities on demand: “I know Kung Fu!”)

Fin is a customer service agent that offers the agent notebook UI pattern. Users create and edit notebooks to define customer service procedures for agents to follow. This is a document-based interface that provides the flexibility to mix general knowledge and guidance with specific process steps.

div.p: AGENT NOTEBOOK

p.strong: Natural language instructions for agentic procedures and processes

div.p: Agent notebooks provide flexible, document-style instructions that combine prose guidance with embedded data sources, logical steps, and code blocks.

list.li: To describe scenarios with unpredictable conditions and edge cases.
To allow users to provide comprehensive guidance without micromanaging every step.
Less predictable outcomes than traditional workflows.

level4.p: If a user needs to teach Fin to help customers who receive damaged food orders, for example, they create a new agent notebook and start describing what to do in plain language (Figure 8.3). From there, they can insert specific data sources to use, logical steps to follow, and even specific code to implement. Like a binder of instructions that you might supply to a customer service rep, these notebook pages add up to a collection of context-specific info and actions to take.

p.span: Fin’s “procedure” feature uses the agent notebook pattern to blend natural language instructions with data sources, logical steps, and code instructions.

level4.p: Unlike a rigid workflow, you don’t have to specify all the detailed branching logic, loops, and edge cases. Instead, you rely on the LLM to understand the big picture and figure out the right thing to do based on context. Notebooks provide a pragmatic approach to unpredictable conditions—like customer service scenarios where people will inevitably change their mind, switch topics, or provide conflicting information. Those situations would stymie even the best designed static workflow. Is an agent up to the task when armed with the right notebook? It’s best to test, of course. Fin provides an experience to stress-test the procedure before you deploy it by running it through several scenarios at scale.

level3.p: You might think that giving an agent more tools would make it more capable, but the reverse tends to be true. The more options that you give an agent, the more opportunity it has to make a bad decision and head down the wrong path. When the agent chooses the wrong tool, you get the wrong result.

Keep taskers focused on a limited set of tools. We’ve found the sweet spot for a focused tasker to be three to six tools, just enough to allow powerful combinations without getting lost in the sauce. Agents start to wobble with more than a dozen tools.

Even narrowly scoped agents like taskers tend to work in multiple steps, often passing the output of one tool or step as the input to the next. After the tasker searches the knowledge base to find answers, the next step might be to pass a summary to a voice tool that speaks the answer.

Here again, taskers resemble workflow automations that move through steps, where the result of one step gets passed to the next. The tool in each step could be a traditional API service as we’ve discussed, or it could be another agent that’s responsible for handling a specific task. Maybe that voice tool is an alchemist agent that can write a script based on raw content and turn it into a conversation or presentation. (We’ll talk about alchemists in a bit.)

Or maybe that next “tool” is actually a person who has the information and capability the agent needs to complete its task. That’s when agents start to break the boundaries that we normally assume for digital services.

Be expansive in thinking about what agents can do and who/what they can talk to. How about phone calls? Google users can ask Google Assistant to call a business to book an appointment, confirm pricing, or ask about wait times. This kicks off a tasker agent to go off and make the call—with convincing, realistic conversation—and then reports back with the results. Other services like ElevenLabs answer phone calls by fielding customer service calls with a voice-enabled triage agent. The agent understands the request well enough to address it if possible or forward the call to the appropriate person (sometimes even another agent!).

list.li: When is it helpful for the agent to reach beyond the screen?
What useful tools are available to talk directly to the user or others? Or to affect the physical environment through smart-home or other internet-of-things services?
What are the social and physical risks of doing this?

When is using an interface designed for humans (phone, browser, email) the best or most efficient route for the task—and when is it counter-productive? (See the sidebar, “The Path of Most Resistance.”)

level2.p: Because agents’ actions have real-world consequences, taskers should seek permission before doing anything serious—and the user should grant this permission only with intention and awareness of the risks. Giving an agent the keys to a workspace, wallet, or phone is a critical moment of delegation. Treat it like an onboarding ritual for the agent—a process to establish the agreed scope, limits, and duration.

Asking for permission is interruptive, so it should happen only at the right moments. Look for transitional seams where control shifts between agent and user. Seams precede actions that commit the user (sending, spending, deleting), or at moments of ambiguity or indecision when the agent needs user guidance.

div.p: ACTION GUARD

The action guard pattern pauses agent activity for user approval before taking impactful, risky, or irreversible actions. It presents the specific action, its target, and its consequences, along with clear options to continue or cancel.

list.li: Before actions that commit the user’s time, money, data, or other resources.
Before irreversible actions like sending or deleting.
Before reading or writing sensitive information.
At moments of ambiguity.
Interrupts the user.
Dialog fatigue can lead to uncritical approval.

level2.p: That’s where the action guard pattern comes in, a functional pattern that pauses activity at critical seams to get the user’s approval. The system should clearly state the action, its object, and the choice: continue or cancel. When you ask Alexa to reorder paper towels, for example, it speaks the exact product, total cost, and arrival date before asking, “Should I buy it?”

This isn’t itself a new UX pattern—confirmation dialogs are well worn—but as agents shoulder more work on their own, reinforcing these seams makes an intentional compact between system and user about who’s in control of what and when. Agents shouldn’t spend money, share personal info, or take irreversible action on their own.

Ask for ID when security is a concern. Alexa can be configured to ask for a four-digit voice PIN before completing a purchase. An authorization step like password, face recognition, or two-factor code helps ensure the user is who they say they are.

Where appropriate, explain why the confirmation is needed and for how long. The coding agent GitHub Copilot asks for permission when it runs a developer-level terminal command. Figure 8.4 shows how the user can set the scope of the permission.

p.span: In addition to asking for permission, GitHub Copilot lets users be specific about the where and when of that permission: session, workspace, or always.

level2.p: Like all confirmations, dialog fatigue can set in with action guards. Too many, too often numbs users to their effect, and they start mashing the Continue button without heeding the request. Be discerning: the tasker shouldn’t ask for permission every time it adds an item to the user’s cart. Instead, ask the user to approve the final purchase. Make the user a final decision-maker, not a micromanager.

Agents are persistent. Give a tasker a job, and it will keep at it until it gets it done or decides that it can’t. That’s the beauty of delegation: someone else takes on the time and effort to keep on going so you don’t have to. Agents don’t get tired, and they can take on long or dreary tasks that would numb most people’s brains.

But this persistence has a downside, too. The longer that agents work, the more likely they are to drift from their original goal. For agents, “reasoning” is a continuous cycle of perception, planning, and action—a rinse-and-repeat loop of trial and error. The trouble is that each new cycle provides an opportunity to let a dumb idea sneak in, and the errors can stick.

Like people, agents can develop bad assumptions that skew decisions over time. A 2025 study by Microsoft researchers found that LLMs often lock into incorrect early assumptions, which compound and amplify in subsequent steps. For long-running tasks, these compounding errors become a logical quicksand they can’t escape. It’s a perverse catch 22: the agent needs time to complete hard tasks, but the more time it has, the more likely it is to stray.

When this happens, the models can get distracted by irrelevant details or by bad ideas that they can’t let go of. Anthropic’s “Computer Use” agent, for example, was an early system to do tasks by driving the UI of browsers or code editors. In one of its early demos in 2024, the agent suddenly abandoned its coding tasks to go browse images of Yellowstone National Park. (Come on, we’ve all done it.)

Distraction becomes fixation because the idea gets baked into the agent’s reasoning process. This is because of the prompt-stuffing strategy that we discussed in Chapter 5, “Chat and the Conversational Interface,” where assistants (and here agents) review the entire conversation history with every step. This keeps the reasoning process coherent but also creates an echo chamber for past mistakes, giving them exaggerated weight.
JOSH’S TAKE

div.p: THE PATH OF MOST RESISTANCE

There’s a whole category of tasker agents that are tuned to use the same interfaces that people do. To do their work, these interface agents fire up a browser, spreadsheet, or text editor to do their work the same way you would: typing, clicking buttons, dragging files. You can stand by and watch them plod through their tasks, hauling the cursor to and fro across websites and applications.

That it works at all is an impressive feat of visual and conceptual processing, but it pushes the agent down the path of most resistance. Throwing an agent into the scrum of the web is to challenge them with all the awkward failings and hostile choices of modern user experience design. The fact that users would ask agents to do the browsing in the first place is an indictment of the craft, suggesting that using web and application interfaces is a headache better handled by others.

To be fair, the same is true of all human communication. By machine standards, our speech and writing are slow and unstructured. Asking machines to use these channels handicaps them more than it empowers.
But sometimes the hard path is the only path. When the sole route to action is locked behind a human interface, then into the breach the agent must go.

Years ago, our agency Big Medium worked with an online travel service. To our surprise, the company relied on banks of fax machines to reach small hotels that still had no online booking. When you “booked” a room, the system faxed the hotel, waited for a reply, and after a day or two of old-fashioned letter-writing, finally delivered a confirmation online. It was slow, error-prone, and absurd, but it was also the only channel available to the automated service. The fax machines were a last resort to paper over the gap in digital booking.

It’s a similar story with agents that use websites or make phone calls for the user. Phone calls are “APIs for humans,” not for machines; their throughput is low and failure rate high. But when there’s no online system available to do the work, someone has to make the call. Now agents can do it.

On the user-facing front end, the interaction is reduced to clicking a “Make Reservation” button. That’s an experience win for the user, saving them the effort of phone calls, faxes, and bad UX. But having agents use these human channels is only a step up from building a system out of twine and bubble gum. It’s not the future, it’s a stopgap.
The work ahead is to create agent-native experiences that help systems communicate directly without navigating inefficient human interfaces.

level2.p: Three approaches can help keep dogged agents on track:

list.li: Visibility: Provide activity streams to expose the agent’s reasoning trail, so users can intervene or reset.

Memory hygiene: Instruct taskers to review their history and discard failed attempts, flawed assumptions, or irrelevant details. The consolidated memory cleans up the noise for fewer distractions. Some systems even have a dedicated tasker to consolidate the memory of other taskers.

Course correction: Add seams in long-running tasks where the system checks in with the user about core assumptions and strategy. These checkpoints let users review and revise both mission and plan before drift turns into derailment.

level2.p: A tasker’s persistence is one of its biggest strengths; a little light tending ensures that they’re persisting in the right direction. As you’ve seen, much of that tending goes into the design of their capability and management. But the design of the environment where they work matters, too.

level2.h2: When the Agent Is Your Customer
level2.p: Designing an agent is only half the job. There’s another side of the transaction that receives and serves these agents. As agents proliferate, technologists will need to adapt to this new client—and businesses to this new kind of customer.
For user-centered designers, a focus on “agent experience” might seem counterintuitive or even unseemly. But it’s the latest step in a very long history of agent service design.

In 1770s London, no one curated a shopping experience as grand and fashionable as Josiah Wedgwood. To step into a Wedgwood ceramics showroom was to enter a temple of taste, a place to be seen as much as to shop (Figure 8.5). Wedgwood held private viewings for royalty and aristocrats, and owning a Wedgwood service signaled not only wealth but cultured modernity. The shopping experience was designed to match.

p.span: Wedgwood’s York Street showroom in London, shown here in 1809, was a remarkable retail environment... that was often never experienced by the actual buyer.

level2.p: Yet many purchases were not made from the showroom at all. Orders often came through servants or staff, and Wedgwood pioneered an entirely separate service experience for these intermediaries. The company introduced remote orders through illustrated catalogs, traveling pattern boxes, and a systematic numbering system. These tools stripped away the theatrical presentation of the showroom in favor of practical specification. A housekeeper in Yorkshire could examine samples, match existing services, and dispatch orders by post without ever setting foot in Wedgwood’s retail spectacle.
Two distinct channels of experience emerged: one for the consumer and one for their agent.

These parallel service tracks have since become familiar. The travel industry provides separate interfaces to travel agents versus travelers. Retail stores give interior designers showrooms, pricing, and services distinct from the homeowners they buy for.

Now Sentient Design agents are the new intermediaries. These agents need their own version of Wedgwood’s catalog and pattern box, systematic interfaces designed for algorithmic capability more than human sensibility. The work for designers is to craft systems that humans love yet agents can navigate with efficient ease.

Although agents can use the same interfaces as humans, “the path of most resistance” suggests that there are better ways. Just as tailored interfaces evolved for human intermediaries, machine agents have their own considerations.

level3.p: For better or worse, agents will experience your digital experience as just another API, a service to access to fetch and retrieve data. For services that sell commodity products or deliver unbranded content, that efficiency may be just fine. Enabling efficient transactions based on core attributes like price, specs, or volume might even play to your strengths.

Many organizations, however, aspire to be more than “just another API” for someone else’s automated shopping service. Brands are made of much more than the rote characteristics of their products. How will agents navigate the intangibles their users care about—taste, craftsmanship, beauty, nostalgia, luxury, social status? Or noncommercial considerations like ethics, sustainability, and social impact? Or any of the other elusive elements that defy easy definition in database columns?

To put it more crassly: what does SEO look like for large language models? How do buyers and sellers alike teach agents to care about and recognize the things that humans care about? How do agents know when the “best deal” isn’t necessarily the right deal?

Some of this is the same as it ever was. Companies will be rewarded for clear, well-structured, semantically sound content, publicly available via the web or elsewhere. Machine-intelligent systems—whether they’re search engines or AI-powered agents—love structured data: accessible markup makes for agent-friendly content.

Reviews, industry publications, and respected forums matter, too—they’re where agents learn to associate concepts like luxury or durability with specific brands. Just like Google’s original PageRank algorithm assigned importance to heavily linked pages, agents can learn from the aura of language that surrounds a product or brand, which means agents need access to that secondary data.

Ultimately, an agent’s definition of the “best deal” should rely on the goals and constraints of the user it serves. These goals should be baked into the agent’s mission and should persist over time. For users who always prefer to buy from companies committed to fair labor practices, their agents should just know. And agents will need ways to verify the claims companies make about those topics.

This is one of many unsettled areas as we write this book, and we don’t yet know how this will turn out. But whatever the mechanism, it seems clear that agents need to maintain a steady notion of what’s important to their users over time. Call it a preference manifest: a structured memory for tracking the values, qualities, social proof, and provenance that their user cares about. A fashion buyer’s manifest might prioritize indie designers, high craftsmanship, and sustainable materials, while rejecting fast fashion regardless of price.

Each of the dimensions captured in that manifest may even become the responsibility of a separate tasker—agents that individually check decisions against the ineffable qualities that users care about. Imagine an entourage of tasker agents that are personal coolhunters, quality checkers, consumer watchdogs, or eco-guardians. (We’ll talk more about collaborative teams of agents when we introduce conductor agents in a bit.)

What happens when you delegate so much of the relationship to software? When agents start showing up instead of customers, customer and company alike could lose the connection and insight of direct interaction. This has always been the risk of intermediaries, now or in 18th-century London. But as long as the agent is armed with the right preferences and the brand provides the right signals, the agent’s behavior can still approximate the behavior (and maybe even the taste!) of its owner.

Clear mission, preferences, and tools give taskers grounding and autonomy for their scoped responsibilities. Other kinds of agents build on tasker fundamentals but blend in specific specialties. The next agent in our tour of agent experience patterns is as much an experience specialist as Josiah Wedgwood himself.

level2.p: An alchemist creates new experiences of data by transforming its format. It turns the “base metal” of one format (a static PDF, say) into the “gold” of a completely new interactive experience, like an audio conversation about the PDF’s content.

Alchemists do their work at the very tip of the Sentient Triangle’s “interoperable” corner. They stretch the very idea of interoperability from simple portability between systems or data formats to portability between experiences.

File converters are primitive examples of alchemists. Tools that convert jpeg images into png files make formats interchangeable: same content, new structure. The same goes for language translation, audio transcription, or website scrapers—all tools to transform data from one form to another.

Rule-based automation did these jobs well enough for years, but the rise of large language models gave many tools a boost. LLMs have a special talent for structured data extraction since they’re good at finding a signal within a cloud of noisy data.

In simple roles, alchemists are little more than glue tools that connect different stages of a workflow by making data compatible. Audio transcription is the glue between a recorded meeting and the generation of a summary report. The alchemist is either an invisible fitting in a larger pipeline or a simple one-click tool to translate this into that. In this utilitarian application, an alchemist behaves like the dedicated tools and workflow tools from Chapter 4, “Transactional Tools.”
But these format conversions are just the beginning. True alchemists harness translation to create entirely new experiences.

level3.p: In 2024, Google’s DeepMind researchers had an idea to help make scientific research more accessible to a mainstream audience: what if any research paper could be turned into a smart explainer for a layperson audience? Like the best science journalism, these explainers would convey complex ideas through plain language and expressive metaphor. The researchers used pop science shows of public radio as a model, and the result was Project Illuminate.

Give Illuminate a research paper as shown in Figure 8.6, and the system generates friendly audio conversations between two “hosts” explaining the topic with enthusiasm. From quantum physics to AI models to astronomical discovery, these conversations can explain the science behind any research paper and why it matters.

p.span: Illuminate provides a simple interface to transform research papers into audio conversations.

level3.p: The accessible content synthesis is impressive, but what’s more striking is the manner. The audio is a near-perfect reflection of American-style podcasts or public radio. The script, the voices, and the oh-so-human pauses, ums, and laughter are all remarkably convincing. More than just performing a text-to-audio conversion, the system also delivers a performance of podcast form and culture.

Google soon added the same technology to its research tool, NotebookLM, letting users upload any content collection and then generate an “audio overview” with the same two-host conversation format. When we gave NotebookLM the manuscript for this book, the system’s alchemist agent took only a couple minutes to create a lively 12-minute conversation about Sentient Design, its principles, and opportunities.

More than just a new format, the transformation serves new use cases. Instead of committing eyes and time to a 100-page research paper, users of Illuminate and NotebookLM have the option to get the gist by listening to a casual conversation while driving, doing dishes, or just enjoying a moment of calm. This alchemy doesn’t replace the original research paper; it finds new outlets for it to serve new kinds of users.

So much digital content is trapped in a single format for a single use case or persona. PDFs are especially stuck: a fixed page format, not at all interactive, and “off to the side” in its own reader application. Changing its format gives the content new shape, which gives it new life.

list.li: Where is your content frozen?
What if you could liberate it to a new experience channel?
What new audiences or use cases would that unlock?

level3.p: In this light, the endless paperwork of government forms becomes a rich test bed. Designer Tim Paul of the UK’s Government Digital Service launched his own project to convert paper-based PDF forms into interactive web forms. Give a PDF form to Paul’s prototype, and the alchemist tool transforms it into an interactive multistep web form (Figure 8.7).

p.span: Tim Paul’s alchemist prototype rescues forms trapped inside PDFs and converts them to web forms using GOV.UK’s design system.

level3.p: Like most alchemist agents, the experience of the tool itself is straightforward, but the experience it enables is genuinely transformational. The alchemist experience pattern is expressed not by the UX of the agent but the new UX that it enables for the source content—here a shift from paper form to interactive convenience.

level3.h3: New Channels for an Audience of One
level3.p: There is no doubt that science journalists or even experienced enthusiasts could create a better podcast than Illuminate or NotebookLM. The takeaway is not that alchemist agents can replace podcasters or journalists, but that they can create podcasts that would never have been created otherwise.

There’s a reason so much content remains imprisoned in PDFs. It’s the lowest-common-denominator format, and most organizations don’t have the resources to support additional channels. In the case of Illuminate, most scientists are working hard just to get their research papers written, let alone create miniature media empires around them.

Alchemists can step in to support the long tail of content that would benefit from new contexts and use cases. They can turn technical manuals into training videos or annual reports into interactive data visualizations—or even pin up fan-fiction movies based on your favorite books.

This changes the economics of content creation to support audiences of one. A teacher with 30 students could have an alchemist generate 30 different explainers of the same concept, each in its own medium tuned to each student’s learning style. A city travel blog could become a dynamic audio tour based on specific interests and locations.

The just-for-me transformation between formats is revelation enough, but the result becomes even more powerfully personalized when you layer additional intelligence into the interface. NotebookLM’s generated conversations, for example, let you jump in and join the conversation. Interrupt the hosts to ask your question or make your own observation, and they’ll detour to address it before returning to their script.

Traditionally, most content would never justify the effort and expense of this kind of translation. But with alchemists, the option is suddenly available. Every piece of content becomes potentially multimodal, ready to shape-shift into whatever form serves the moment.

list.li: What becomes possible in the product experience when content can fit a whole range of on-demand formats and channels?
What alternative experiences of this source content would best serve the user?
What if those experiences were fast and easy to deliver?

level3.p: As you explore those possibilities, consider how to make these novel options obvious. For now, at least, people don’t naturally think, “I wish this PDF were a podcast” or “This spreadsheet would make a great video.” The transformative possibility may not be clear to users until you put it right in front of them. Surface the alternatives at the right moments:

list.li: Step in at common frustration points. If users might stall in dense or challenging content, offer an explainer video that tells the story a different way.
Recognize fresh contexts. When the user enters a new context, offer new conveniences. In the morning, offer an audio summary of the day ahead to listen on the commute.
Preview capability. Give a quick taste with preview snippets or before-and-after examples to help users discover what’s possible.

level3.p: Challenge yourself to consider how deeply you might transform the source content. Will your alchemy apply a golden sheen, or will you go all-in for 24 karat? Alchemists do their work at several levels of impact:

list.li: Surface transformations change the format while keeping the original content intact. Text-to-speech, language translation, or visual styling fall into this category. The content of a generated audio transcript remains the same; it’s just text now.

Structural transformations reorganize content for a new context that preserves meaning if not literal translation. A text report becomes a data visualization, or the government form becomes a web page.
Experiential transformations create entirely new interactions. A textbook becomes a Socratic dialogue, or a research paper turns into an educational video game.

level3.p: Use these levels as a creative ladder for exploration. As you consider surface transformations, push yourself to imagine what becomes possible, for example, now that your audio is available in text. How does that new format become material to create a structural or experiential transformation?

These layers often map to steps in the alchemist’s process. To work its PDF-to-podcast alchemy, NotebookLM performs a structural transformation to synthesize content into a conversation script. Then a surface transformation converts that script into multivoice audio. And finally, an experiential transformation listens for user interaction, spinning up script detours that are radically adaptive to the moment.
In production systems, each of these stages is often handled by an individual alchemist agent. (Alchemists are taskers, too; they love to focus on a single, scoped task.)

The deeper the transformation, of course, the deeper the risk. Reducing a 100-page research report to a five-minute conversation asks for a ton of editorial decisions about what to emphasize or simplify. Every decision changes context … and when things go wrong, it can also change meaning.

Design for fidelity in your transformation. Consider how transformations might shift meaning and build appropriate disclaimers or links back to source material. An alchemist that converts legal documents into plain language should flag when precision has been traded for clarity, with a link to the original document. Make changes obvious and explainable. We’ll dig into strategies for citation and explainability more deeply in Chapter 11, “Defensive Design.”

The work of alchemists is about one-off transformations, zapping source collections in a single, powerful task-based act. By contrast, other agents have jobs that are never done, with roles defined by oversight and long-term responsibility. For that, we introduce the steady hands of the steward—and naturally 19th-century seafaring.

level2.p: By the mid-1800s, global sea trade had exploded and so had the costs of wrecks on dangerous coasts. The stakes in lives, cargo, and commerce were so high that nations invested in lighthouses. These beacons demanded round-the-clock tending by keepers who cleaned the glass and kept the machinery alive through salt and storm. A new profession was born. The lighthouse keepers’ labor was isolated and invisible from the people it impacted, but their results shone across the seas. Commerce of the era depended on these keepers to keep the lights on. The infrastructure required a 24–7 steward.

Stewards are the lighthouse keepers of digital work: nonstop caretakers of important, often unenviable work. A steward is a tasker with persistence and responsibility. While taskers focus on a narrow chore, a steward is entrusted with an ongoing job, a domain to maintain. Unlike an assistant that waits for commands or an NPC with a narrow role in a shared workspace, a steward is delegated a persistent set of scoped responsibilities that it constantly tends.
A steward for inventory management, for example, perpetually monitors stock levels, automatically places reorders when thresholds are met, and flags potential supply chain delays.

In the Sentient Triangle, stewards stake out the most grounded position of all the agent experiences (refer back to Figure 8.1). Successful stewards are built on sturdy data and the tool use of trusted systems. They prove their worth through their reliability.

Their responsibilities mean that tool use and interoperable access to systems is essential for stewards. Their foundational value is in monitoring and updating the same systems that people do. Stewards use email, calendars, Slack, Jira, and other business-critical software. By using the same tools as the rest of the team, stewards integrate into existing always-on workflows.

level3.p: In the over-heated world of AI marketing, stewards sometimes get the problematic labels “virtual employee” or “digital worker,” suggesting that they can slot in to replace someone’s job.

A steward is a power tool, not a replacement worker. You can’t replace a carpenter with a nail gun, and you can’t replace an accountant with an invoice steward. But you can replace hours of tedious data entry and reconciliation, giving the accountant time to offer more valuable analysis and strategic financial guidance. Spreadsheets didn’t replace financial analysts; they helped to grow the field.

Instead of replacing jobs, focus stewards on the jobs that aren’t getting done (or getting done well) because there’s not enough time or attention. Lighthouse keeping became a profession because the old coal fires tended by villagers could no longer keep ships safe. Steward agents get “hired” to fill similar gaps where the current system struggles. A steward’s work should focus on areas that are high-volume, repetitive, and error prone.
These tasks often revolve around common core strengths of machine intelligence:

list.li: Document processing: This is often where stewards start, evaluating and routing invoices, contracts, or intake forms.
Search and curation: Stewards can monitor topic areas to surface and summarize relevant policies, research, news, or legislation.
Reporting: Stewards can shoulder the daily flood of reports, summaries, memos, and alerts that often crunch and underutilize analysts.
Routing and intake: Stewards are great at triage to evaluate and answer basic customer support questions.

level3.p: While these always-on roles don’t qualify stewards as “virtual employees,” they still act as collaborative teammates to support a group’s work. This is felt mainly through their use of common tools more than a specific interface, which makes them somewhat different from other agents.

Users don’t often see stewards perform their work directly. These agents do much of their work without an interface, a so-called “headless” system. The evidence of a steward’s work is the wake they leave behind: completed tasks and documents delivered to their respective systems.

FloQast is accounting software that helps companies settle their books. The platform fields a squad of steward agents to shoulder the grind of gathering and managing all the charges, income, and paperwork that fly through accounts. A “reconciliation agent” matches transactions across accounts, a “journal entry agent” logs new charges, and a “task agent” nudges colleagues for missing info. Each of the stewards has the tools to use the necessary systems, so results simply show up where they belong: accounts get updated, emails get sent, and flags are raised when transactions don’t line up. FloQast says its steward agents cut task time by an estimated 40 percent.

The payoff is less grunt work for accountants and more time for analysis. The manager experience from Chapter 7, “Agents and the Manager Experience,” is dialed high here; the user experience is less about doing the books and more about reviewing the work of the stewards that deliver the work, as shown in Figure 8.8. If there’s something an agent can’t sort out, it assigns the problem to a user for review. Companies can also configure high-impact processes to require review every time.

p.span: FloQast’s journal entry agent asks for review after batching a new set of transactions. The user sees an activity stream checklist for context (left) alongside the work to review (right).

level3.p: Traditional automation has long done similar work but lacked the judgment to make decisions that cost accountants hours of tedious work. For a practice so precise in its numbers, accounting is awash in messy data: invoices that say “Acme, Inc.” when another system calls the company “Acme.com,” or payments that don’t align with the planned transaction schedule. Rule-based systems struggle with these small but unpredictable challenges. Machine intelligence can not only handle these fuzzy decisions, but also learn as it goes, discovering a company’s specific nuances and exceptions. Steward agents eat this up.

This requires earned trust. FloQast’s stewards operate in highly regulated, audit-intensive environments. There’s little room for error, so the system preserves a high degree of review and control for the stewards’ actions. Every decision is logged and explained to allow a detailed, transparent review of what the agents do and why.
Invisible work easily becomes invisible risk, so the ease of reviewing results becomes a fundamental user experience.

We introduced Fin earlier, the customer-service agent of Intercom, a customer-support software company. Fin handles millions of customer interactions for Intercom’s client companies in order to answer questions, process returns, and handle the day-to-day work of satisfying customer requests.

On the customer side, people interact with Fin as an assistant in a familiar chat experience. On the business side, however, users experience Fin as a steward taking ownership of front-line support triage. The agent solves issues where it can and passes customers along to other reps when it cannot.

Go figure, but satisfying customers is no trivial thing. Fin’s success (or failure) is the company’s success (or failure). For the business, the agent’s work might happen invisibly and automatically, but it can’t be taken for granted. Here again, the primary steward experience is one of monitoring and review.

To keep tabs on how things are going, Fin’s steward experience is defined by its analytics suite, an always-on performance review for the agent. More than just reviewing individual tasks, these interfaces explore how well the agent is doing its whole job. A performance dashboard shows Fin’s core success stats: how often it handles issues, how often it resolves those issues on its own, and an overall customer satisfaction metric. It also charts the topics the agent engages over time, to help identify emerging issues.

Most important of all, the dashboard shows where Fin could use help. Figure 8.9 shows the topics where Fin couldn’t solve problems and punted them over to human reps. Like any good performance review, this one identifies not only the opportunities for improvement, but also good coaching for how to get there. Each problem area shows suggested actions to help Fin do better—like adding or clarifying the knowledge-based articles it relies on. In many cases, these suggestions can be reviewed and accepted with a single click, instantly updating Fin’s knowledge.

When you entrust the persistent care and management of an entire business function, there’s a new responsibility to make sure the job is done properly. The lighthouse keepers of yore could prove their worth simply with a steady light. It’s a little more complex for today’s stewards, and being good at delegating to them means being good at evaluating their work. For designers of steward experiences, the fundamental job is to make it easy to explore the results.

p.span: Fin’s “Optimize” dashboard identifies content, data, and actions where the agent struggles, with suggestions for improving performance.

list.li: What does the user need to know to understand whether the steward is taking good care of its domain?
What’s the steward doing well and where could it stand improvement?
What actions can help the steward do better, and how do you communicate that to the user?

level3.p: What happens when the delegated job is the delegation itself? That’s where conductors come in: the middle managers of the agent universe.

level2.p: Platforms like FloQast show the power of having many agents doing independent work, each a powerful solo act to accomplish stand-alone tasks. But some jobs are bigger than any single agent can handle. Instead of a single mega-agent, those tasks need an ensemble to get it done.

Conductors orchestrate teams of agents to accomplish a coordinated task. They bring their own specialized role to the team: understanding the big-picture goal, making the plan, and signaling other agents to play their specific part at the right moments. One agent’s output becomes another’s input. Some tasks loop and repeat.

It’s the conductor’s job to keep this buzz of activity from dissolving into noise. The conductor manages this troupe of specialists, so the user doesn’t have to. For the user, the result is a single interface and a unified result.

All agents use tools, but for conductors, those tools are other agents, or subagents. It’s taskers all the way down—individual agents doing one job very well—with the conductor as tasker-in-chief, shaping the mob into a team and imposing process. For the designer, this requires understanding how groups of agents can work together. Conductor design is organization design.

level3.p: Just like people work together in many ways, agents can adopt different organizational forms, too. Simple automations resemble fire brigades, a straightforward hand-off from one agent to the next. More complex processes echo critique-driven design studios, where one agent produces work and others review it, sending it back for revision until it meets the standard. Still others are like construction crews, many agents working in parallel on different parts of the same structure, coordinated by the conductor as general contractor.

In machine learning circles, the design studio model is commonly known as the actor-critic pattern. The “actor” is a tasker that produces a result, and the “critic” tasker evaluates it. In travel planning, the actor might propose possible flight combinations, while the critic checks that the flights exist and that the connections are feasible. If not, the conductor sends the work back to the actor with the critic’s comments. In various settings, critics can act as guardrails, moderate user content, ensure consistent code style, and more. We’ll explore the defensive value of critics more deeply in Chapter 11.

Figure 8.10 shows how this works in practice for a writer’s room of agents. The conductor acts as head writer overseeing a researcher, an outliner, a writer, a critic, and an editor. At each stage, the conductor guides the output and determines where to take it next. In particular, the conductor moderates the actor-critic revision loop, deciding when the draft is “good enough” to pass to the editor.

p.span: In a system for producing a writing assignment, the conductor acts as head writer, giving each tasker agent its assignment and context, shepherding the result from agent to agent.

level3.p: This writing process is a fire brigade model with a design studio inserted in the middle—an assembly line that includes an iterative looping process. The downside of this serial iteration is that it takes a lot of time. Speed is an essential part of user experience. As the designer, work with your engineer colleagues to determine what’s an acceptable amount of time for the process to take. If a linear assembly line model takes too long, then you might need to get swarmy.

Swarms trade the order of a queue for the speed of parallel effort, the construction crew model. The conductor breaks down a phase into slices and dispatches them to a crowd of subagents, like a team of carpenters framing a house. The agents work simultaneously, though sometimes redundantly, and the conductor’s job is to merge the results back together.

Many hands make faster work, so swarming completes tasks faster than a serial process. Swarms are especially good at broad information-gathering that involves pursuing many directions at once. It’s well suited to research, code generation, or data processing that can be compartmentalized into separate independent areas of work. Figure 8.11 shows a conductor deploying a team of “code implementer” tasker subagents to build different sections of code simultaneously.

p.span: Claude Code is a conductor agent for writing code from the command line. Here, the activity stream shows a six-phase plan. In phase 3, five “code implementer” subagents swarm different parts of the code.

level3.p: Swarms create headaches when one subagent’s work is a dependency of another’s. If one agent in a coding swarm is responsible for architecting the foundations of a system, you can’t have another agent building features at the same time. It won’t know the conventions. One hand doesn’t know what the other is doing.

This might resemble some human organizations you know. Unclear goals, bad communications, and misaligned incentives are the things that vex teams and stir chaos. All teams, even agent teams, inevitably introduce their own flavor of dysfunction. While individual agents might fail in ways that are familiar to software, swarms of agents fail in ways that resemble how organizations fail.

“Multi-agent systems have emergent behaviors, which arise without specific programming,” Anthropic researchers wrote in a 2025 study of agentic systems. “Small changes to the lead agent can unpredictably change how subagents behave. Success requires understanding interaction patterns, not just individual agent behavior. Therefore, the best prompts for these agents are not just strict instructions, but frameworks for collaboration that define the division of labor, problem-solving approaches, and effort budgets.”1 These systems favor notebooks over workflows.

Conductors have to be smart enough to create these instructions for their subagent taskers. While the user remains the boss of the operation, the conductor is the middle manager that needs to translate user goals into piecework jobs for its worker-bee taskers. Just like other kinds of agent need good tool instructions, conductors need good subagent instructions: what agents are available and how to talk to them. Conductors are basically prompt writers, giving each agent a specific task, guidance on tools and context sources, as well as boundaries to help keep things scoped. These instructions have to be clear enough to avoid misunderstandings.

While the conductors do all this work, what do users see? Even more than the other agent types, the conductor presents especially strong design challenges for the delegation phases of mission setting, plan review, and activity monitoring. You can lean hard on the manager experience design patterns we introduced in the last chapter. Activity monitoring, though, requires new methods for keeping tabs on a multiagent crew.

level3.h3: The View from the Conductor’s Podium
level3.p: Multiagent systems invoke a flurry of activity, and, with swarms, can involve a lot of fast-moving, parallel activity. This calls for a different kind of high-level monitoring than other agents. As you’ve seen, taskers and alchemists log individual actions; stewards report their results as quality trends; but conductors emphasize team progress toward the overall goal. The view from the conductor’s podium notes the individual players but remains focused on making sense of the symphony.

The seeming simplicity of this “audience experience” for users papers over a churn of complexity for the designer. Tasks might start in one environment but be completed minutes or hours later by agents across a whole range of tools and services. Dozens of agents might be assigning and self-assigning tasks and handing off work in unpredictable ways. Designing this experience asks you to consolidate tasks and queues to a single view so that users don’t have to jump across systems or screens to understand what’s happening.

It turns out that this is exactly what project management systems do, and you can borrow many of the same UX metaphors to design the manager experience for helping users track what the conductor and its charges are up to.

The sturdy activity stream pattern is a simple way to show progress, either as a checklist format or as a running log (the earlier Claude Code example in Figure 8.11 combines both). A handful of other formats provides more visual and interaction opportunities: kanban boards, table lists, and list-detail views.

level4.p: A kanban board is a simple, visual way of managing progress. Popularized by project tools like Trello or Jira, Kanban visualizes work as cards that move through columns like “to do” and “in progress.” The format makes it easy to see what’s happening at a glance and where the bottlenecks are.

Agents are starting to surf these boards, too—sometimes updating them directly as stewards or NPCs. In conductor experiences, the conductor owns the board just as it owns the project. It adds new task cards as it fires up new agent tasks, shuffling them across columns to mark progress.
Use the kanban board pattern to visualize agent activity when you want to emphasize activity phases for a discrete set of longer-running tasks.
Figure 8.12 shows one example of an agent-native kanban board for managing multistep, multiagent workflows. The invisible hand of the conductor moves the cards as the agents make progress.

p.span: Vibe Kanban is an open-source project that uses a kanban board for multiagent management. Here, agents are working on three tasks, and one is ready for review: click any card to see its details.

level4.p: Kanban is intuitive and glanceable, providing a visual heatmap of where the project stands as cards move from left to right across the board. Each card represents the mission of a subagent, so it’s easy to see who’s doing what. Nested subtasks can be represented inside a parent card.

As the designer of a kanban conductor experience, ask yourself what level of activity is most appropriate for each card. Kanban boards are best for tasks that unfold at human pace, not microtasks that flash by in seconds. You can size cards to phases, missions, or tasks with time frames that make practical sense. Use that insight to give the conductor guidance for managing the board. The goal is to deliver a board that gives high-level understanding instead of fastidious detail.

While the familiarity of the kanban pattern makes it instantly understandable, that familiarity can also backfire. Traditional kanban boards let users drag cards between columns to change the state of the task. Users may expect to do the same here, but what does that mean? Does dragging a card to “in progress” kick off a new subagent? Does moving it back to “to do” reset the current agent? The best path is to make conductor kanban boards read-only for users.

div.p: KANBAN BOARD

A kanban board displays agent activity as cards moving through progress columns. Each card represents a phase of work or a subagent mission, making progress visible at a glance. Conductors manage the board automatically, updating card states as tasks evolve.

list.li: To visualize multiagent workflows with an emphasis on work phases.
For long-running tasks that unfold in minutes, hours, or more.
Too many tasks can clutter the board and overwhelm users.
Drag-and-drop expectations may confuse users.

level4.p: Table lists shine for lots of concurrent activities. While a kanban board is great at showing the deliberate march of a project through its phases, it struggles to represent the buzz of a swarm. When a conductor unleashes dozens or hundreds of agents to work in parallel, the visual metaphor of cards on a board can quickly become overwhelming. A workmanlike table list is the most versatile way to capture this scale.
Use the table list pattern when you need to track very large numbers of concurrent tasks where filtering and sorting are also helpful.

But should you? Remember the conductor’s podium view and its emphasis on the big picture. Table views can quickly bury users in too much detail. Avoid this by applying similar considerations to tables as you would for kanban: tailor listings to the task sizes and time frames that are most useful for the monitoring user.

In the world of enterprise cloud computing, services like AWS Step Functions (Figure 8.13) act as industrial-strength conductors. As a tool for engineers, Step Functions uses a table list to monitor a dense set of high-level jobs; drilling into any specific item lets you see the entire process and its subtasks. The pattern could equally be used, for example, by a customer service manager watching dozens of “resolution agents” in flight.

p.span: AWS Step Functions provides a filtered table list of every task run, allowing a developer to quickly identify the status of the entire operation and diagnose any failed runs.

level4.p: Unlike kanban boards, tables read as data, not process. You can see it in the AWS Step Functions example: a utilitarian interface for a utilitarian function. Consider pairing table lists with other formats that supplement with process overview: a progress bar or pie chart for overall sense of completion, or filters that list only the most important subset of tasks by default.

div.p: TABLE LIST
A table list shows subagent work in rows that can be sorted and filtered. It provides a compact, data-rich way to show a large amount of multi-agent activity.

list.li: To track large, swarm-scale numbers of parallel tasks.
When filtering or sorting tasks is important.
Hard to understand progress or process flow at a glance.
Can feel dry or overwhelming without supporting visual cues.

level4.p: Because you use email, you already know the list-detail view. This tried-and-true workhorse provides a split view of your inbox: all your messages are poured into one pane (the “list”), and choosing a message shows the full message (the “detail”). Often, there’s even a third high-level panel to list mailboxes, too, letting you switch the messages shown in the list pane.

The list-detail pattern splits the difference between the pros and cons of the kanban and table lists. If kanban emphasizes progress/story and table lists emphasize data/scale, list-detail emphasizes focused review and exploration. While all three patterns let users drill into the details, list-detail specifically invites it, encouraging fluid movement between overview and details.

Instead of a mailbox pane, imagine a project pane showing all your projects. Choose a project to get the big-picture “symphony” view of that project. For a conductor agent, the list pane typically shows the conductor’s overall plan, the sequence of high-level tasks, or the status of each subagent. Choose a task item to see the thought process and results of that task in the detail pane.

Academic research tool Elicit uses a list-detail view to share high-level progress while allowing the user to drill into the details of each phase. Figure 8.14 shows a final research report in the detail pane, with the phases of work in the list pane at right. Selecting any of the phases shows the work done and sources unearthed in that phase. The list pane provides the progress overview, and the detail pane allows deep inspection.

div.p: LIST-DETAIL

p.strong: Split view for overview and deep inspection of agent tasks

div.p: The list-detail view presents many subagent tasks in a list alongside a detail pane that shows the full output of the selected task.

list.li: To invite review of specific workflow phases while still centering the high-level plan and results.
Weaker at showing process flow for subagent tasks compared to kanban.
Risk of distraction if detail pane pulls focus away from the overall progress view.

p.span: The list-detail view in Elicit invites the user to dig into each phase of the research that yielded the final report.

level4.p: List-detail works best when users are expected to review a subset of tasks closely, not every single one. In particular, the list view isn’t well suited to a big pile of parallel tasks. Instead, use a list view item to show a phase of activity (“gather sources”) and then display the activity of the various researching subagents in the detail view. Done well, the design helps the user feel in command of a large multiagent process without drowning in it.

All of these conductor-monitoring design patterns help users make sense of a potentially noisy mob of agents. But swarms of agents affect more than just the user. They naturally impact the services on the receiving end of these agents, as well as the people and bots that might be competing with them.

level2.p: Agents are more than individual actors seeking to accomplish a simple task. In reality, these agents navigate complex, populated systems. Self-driving cars do more than drive to a destination, for example, they navigate traffic and the decisions of other cars and drivers. Ignoring the wider system not only risks failure for the agent but also creates risks for everyone else, too.

Even simple tasks become complex in systems full of self-interested actors, whether they’re people or agents. Optimizing for individual goals warps the larger system in unpredictable ways. Google Maps reroutes whole traffic patterns when a critical mass of drivers follows its instructions. Deal-hunting bots buy out inventory and create resale markets overnight. Wikipedia’s servers strain when AI-driven web crawlers swarm their servers to harvest training data.

These effects are sharpest when many agents compete for the same scarce resources (tickets, driving routes, server bandwidth). Systems change to regain equilibrium: Uber bumps its surge pricing, or a quiet street fills with cars routing around an accident.

Design for system health, not just individual interest. This is easier said than done. As we saw with conductors, groups are hard to wrangle, even with a common goal. Anticipating groups with different incentives is harder still. But just like in human society, we can at least control our own behavior … and that of our agents.

level3.p: Give agents the awareness they need to balance user goals with system constraints and etiquette. Some of that awareness naturally rhymes with pure self-interest, too. That might mean weighing speed against scarcity (“wait for best seat availability” versus “buy any seat now”), or honoring etiquette like rate limits and opt-outs.

But what about cases where system health doesn’t align with individual or corporate wants? The agents of big technology companies have ignored web-crawler standards and copyright rules, for example, to hoover up data for AI models and advance business interests over system health.

Teach your agents to do better. There’s naturally a cost of doing the right thing when others choose not to. But as we populate digital and physical systems with agents, let’s strive to make scofflaws the exception, not the norm. Design not just for the user experience but for the system experience.
System-aware, multiagent behavior demands new traffic control—in both technical implementation as well as rules-of-the-road etiquette for how agents behave.

Agents need to know what other agents are doing. They need visibility into agent crowds (“two million agents are all doing the same thing right now”) and expectations for what to do in response. Agents also need to understand and sometimes collaborate with those other agents. Just as protocols like MCP help agents discover tools they can use, emerging standards, like Google’s A2A protocol (“agent to agent”), let agents introduce themselves and collaborate.

Agents can be gullible, though; their LLM-powered brains are suggestible by nature and without training take input at face value. As a collaborative multiagent world emerges, teach your agents to be cautious about making new friends. Good design teaches agents restraint: when to share data, when to ask for confirmation, and how to recover from bad interactions or misunderstandings.

All of these factors suggest that agents will need a set of systemwide ethical and practical guidelines that frankly haven’t emerged yet. Studies have found that “communities” of agents can form conventions of their own, from terminology to common tactics to preferred outcomes. These are behaviors that don’t emerge in a single tasker but only happen in groups. Monitoring single-agent outcomes is insufficient to keep on top of this; you have to measure collective effects. Ideally, these new conventions will be designed with intention and good will. Let’s not let these behaviors emerge by accident.

This might sound ominous, but we think it’s simply realistic. Introducing new players and capabilities—and even entire systems—will always have unintended consequences, both good and bad. We started our exploration of agents in the last chapter with the humble mailbox. Packages still get lost in the postal system, but the risk of using the system is a whole lot lower than the effort of taking it there yourself.

When agents perform their tasks, they also reshape the systems they inhabit. That ripple effect is a reminder that no single pattern—or even the whole Sentient Triangle—can capture every possibility. As agents multiply, they will mix, morph, and perhaps invent new postures of their own.

The challenge for designers is not to cling to one framework but to recognize that even the best maps are only approximations. That’s an idea we’ll explore next as we venture into the mysterious heart of the Sentient Triangle, where we’ll meet the final Sentient Design posture: the copilot.

li.p: 1 “How We Built Our Multi-Agent Research System,” Anthropic, June 13, 2025, www.anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system

level1.p: Copilots and Uncharted Territory

p.a: “Slap a Chatbot on It”: Companion Dialogue
The Map Is Not the Territory

level1.p: In the cold waters of the Norwegian Sea, a colossal sea serpent coils around a doomed ship. Just to the south, tusked sea beasts with saw-toothed backs clash in an epic battle that makes Godzilla and Mothra look quaint. To the west, a giant lobster off the coast of Scotland clutches a man in its enormous claw. The seas froth with menace.

This sci-fi thriller is the Carta Marina, a 1539 map of Scandinavia (Figure 9.1). The maritime nightmares of its sea scenes give way to reassuring images on shore: woodsmen hunting, fishermen on frozen lakes, traders bartering in ports. It’s half road atlas, half ghost story. In its day, the Carta Marina was the most accurate map of Northern Europe. With its exquisite detail of both fact and fable, it’s a guide to what was known and a warning about what was not.

p.span: A detail of the Carta Marina features a crush of sea monsters and hybrid creatures in the Norwegian Sea.

level1.p: The Sentient Triangle isn’t as ornate, but it has its own stock of hybrid beasts and zones of uncertainty. Like the Carta Marina, the Sentient Triangle is a map created for an era of exploration. The triangle has served us well as we’ve explored the domains of tools, chat, and agents. The corners occupied by these postures are reliable landmarks. But as you venture toward the center, the edges soften and experience patterns blend.

Copilots navigate this middle sea. They are a hybrid species that can combine tool-like autocompletion, for example, with chat dialogue and do-it-for-me agentic behavior. Copilots are the fourth and final posture of the Sentient Design ecosystem. Balanced at the center of the triangle, they have equal parts of grounded reliability, interoperability, and radical adaptability (Figure 9.2).

p.span: Copilots inhabit the terra incognita at the center of the Sentient Triangle, a zone of hybrid possibility.

level1.p: Because they sit at this crossroads, copilots resist neat categorization of experience patterns like the other postures. They show up instead as blends of other experiences.

level2.p: Where the Carta Marina has sea monsters, copilots have daemons—an old-school computing term for utility helpers that run in the background to monitor systems or do cleanup work. That’s what copilots do, too, except that these grown-up daemons frequently step out of the shadows to lend a hand. (These friendly daemons are more cookie monster than sea monster.)

Copilots provide continuous, context-aware assistance throughout an activity. The scope of that activity can be very narrow—support a phone call—or very broad, like an ever-present chat sidebar or an auto-suggest feature that makes real-time suggestions anytime you type.
