The copilot posture is defined by that steady presence. They are always-on, unlike transactional tools or turn-taking chat, and they’re always nearby unlike “see you when I’m done” autonomous agents. Yet as you’ll see, copilots often combine aspects of all those other postures.
The changeable form of copilots might blur boundaries, but you can spot them by their behaviors. Here’s a quick field guide to three Sentient Design attributes where copilots score high:

list.li: Ambient: Copilots are always available, at the ready but tucked away when they’re not needed. They can be summoned on demand, but they also appear on their own when there’s an opportunity to help, which makes them …

Proactive: Copilots listen and observe for opportunities to participate, jumping in at appropriate moments before they’re asked. Autosuggest tools like Google’s Smart Compose are a simple example, anticipating what you might want to type before you get there (see “Autosuggest” in Chapter 11, “Defensive Design”). Their actions are always offered only as suggestions, which makes them …

Deferential: They propose instead of impose, seeking to support rather than direct the action. The subtle text suggested by Smart Compose can be ignored if you don’t want it, or tab to accept. Either way, the interaction doesn’t get in the way; it’s either benign or it speeds you up.

level2.p: Like their name suggests, copilots commit to a support role. While chat experiences are the main-event interface, for example, copilot experiences are secondary, backing up a separate main activity.

This posture means copilots are always off to the side: often literally so, in a sidebar to help steer activity in the main canvas, but just as frequently as a background process that occasionally pops to the surface. Copilots have a long heritage, for example, in support tools that you probably take for granted: spell check in your text editor or lane warnings in your car.

Machine intelligence may add new smarts to this familiar category, but copilots remain most at home alongside mundane tasks or everyday routines. For both better and worse, a common solution is to bolt a copilot experience on top of those routines. Pull up a sidebar and settle in to meet the ubiquitous companion dialogue pattern.

level2.h2: “Slap a Chatbot on It”: Companion Dialogue
level2.p: “Just add chat” is a blunt-force tactic for supplementing applications (often legacy applications) with copilot behavior, and the approach has pros and cons. This is the common companion dialogue interaction pattern: a conversational interface adjacent to the primary workspace.

div.p: COMPANION DIALOGUE

p.strong: Adjacent chat to drive and discuss the primary workspace

div.p: Companion dialogue adds a conversational layer to accompany the main work surface. This chat-based interaction uses workspace content as context to act or explore.

list.li: To enable switching between focused work and copilot interaction.
For remote-control changes to workspace content via chat.
For “meta-activities” like planning, exploring options, or giving instructions about the work.
For transitional, bolt-on addition of machine intelligence to legacy applications.
Adds an additional interaction mode.
Breaks flow from the primary activity.
Distances interaction from the content to act on.
Reduces available space for the main work surface.

level2.p: In GUI interfaces, this chat box typically appears as a sidebar or popover; in mobile, as a modal window or bottom sheet. Microsoft, for example, adds a collapsible sidebar to all of its Office applications to let the user get assistance with the current document, spreadsheet, or slide deck. In Notion, users can summon a floating popover from anywhere in the application by clicking a button (Figure 9.3).

p.span: Notion uses the companion dialogue pattern to co-create documents in the main panel from conversation in the right sidebar.

level2.p: In both experiences, the user can do things like ask to summarize the document, generate a project timeline, create new documents, and more. In a narrow sense, the companion dialogue acts as a natural-language remote control to work the app interface and manipulate workspace content. The main activity happens in the workspace, and the “meta-activity” happens in the companion panel where users chat with the system to give instructions for action.

These are document-based examples, but the pattern shows up in all kinds of applications. Miro has a sidebar to add content to the canvas. The alternative web browsers Dia, Atlas, and Comet provide a sidebar for users to ask about the content in tabs or launch search missions.

In addition to doing work, chat in this context lets you talk about your work. Users can ask questions about what they’re seeing (“What does this function do?”), generate content (“Create a diagram of this process”), or find related info (“Pull up the research on this topic”).

So … this is chat? Or is it an agent? But also, a tool? Exactly. The companion dialogue pattern blends chat’s flexibility with an agent’s skills and a tool’s “make it now” utility. This is typical of copilot experiences. Planted at the middle of the Sentient Triangle, copilots borrow forms from the other postures.

What makes it a copilot is its posture: adjacent, persistent, supportive. The adjacency is what gives the companion dialogue its utility: it sits next to you to solve the problem. But this next-door approach is also its drawback.

Using a companion dialogue necessarily moves the user out of the main workspace, shifting attention away from the primary task. Experience patterns like NPCs, intelligent canvases, or inline tools purposely embed intelligence into the main canvas itself. There, users act on the thing they want to affect. The companion dialogue pulls the user out of context.

From a product perspective, there’s relative ease to this approach. There’s no new interaction model to invent; you can add the companion panel as a new layer instead of doing a complete rethink. For legacy applications, this approach has particular appeal: it’s a new feature, not a new application.

No wonder the pattern is so popular. The companion dialogue is a robust and convenient solution, but before adopting it, it’s worth asking whether the convenience is worth the cost. It requires users to switch modes and out of flow. It adds a layer of abstraction to the existing interaction model. It imposes a conversational interface that may not suit all activities. And it adds both physical and mental distance between the interaction and the primary activity.

Challenge yourself to provide the same flexibility without leaving the primary interaction canvas. When could coders talk to the system through inline code comments instead of a sidebar dialogue, for example? Protect flow. Help the user stay in context, with eyes and attention on the primary task.

list.li: What does conversation add to the task?
How can you embed intelligence closer to activity or content you want to affect?
Could inline tools or other experience patterns deliver the same value without pulling users out of their workflow?

level2.p: Companion dialogues earn their keep and their headaches from their side-by-side presence, but copilots don’t always have to be so conspicuous—or so far from the action. Some of the most effective copilots work so quietly that you barely notice them until they show up at just the right moment.
When the Grammarly grammar-checker spots a clumsy sentence, it just underlines the phrase. You can keep writing, finish your thought, and review the suggestion when you’re ready.

So boring, right? That’s what makes Grammarly a great copilot. Copilots are well suited to casual intelligence, adding fresh smarts to established interactions like the spell-check convention, a respectful, well-timed intervention (Figure 9.4).

p.span: Grammarly elevates the familiar grammar checker with casual intelligence that offers writing suggestions.

level2.p: The Pixel phone’s Best Take feature similarly elevates an everyday task by working almost invisibly. Best Take detects when you’re taking a group photo and combines the best shots into one photo with everyone’s best expressions: no more half yawns or blinking eyes. This fundamentally agentic behavior becomes a copilot because of its ambient, proactive presence.

All of the examples so far have supported tasks. Some copilots sink even deeper into the background to support environment. These services are devoted to tuning applications or hardware to behave in line with your preferences and behavior. Amazon’s Alexa service offers a copilot feature called hunches that learns your daily habits and smart-home routines to predict what you might want to do next. Based on these patterns, Alexa can then suggest actions like turning off a forgotten light or updating device preferences.

Apple’s AirPods take a similar approach but with real-time adaptability. They offer copilot features to filter out background noise (adaptive audio) and lower the volume when you start speaking (conversation awareness). Like Alexa’s hunches, the AirPods detect shifts in context and act accordingly.

This functional pattern is the personalization daemon. These companions use context to adapt the environment on your behalf with lightly agentic behavior. Instead of traditional customization that asks users to wade through a thicket of manual preferences, personalization daemons figure it out for you.

div.p: PERSONALIZATION DAEMON

p.strong: Suggestions or actions based on monitored context

div.p: Personalization daemons sense what’s happening and make subtle adjustments to application behavior or preferences on the user’s behalf.

list.li: When settings require frequent adjustments.
When user needs are obvious according to context.
When personalization clearly improves safety, comfort, or accessibility.
Risk of over-personalization that feels opaque or “creepy.”
Mistaken inferences can frustrate if users feel loss of control.
Continuous monitoring raises privacy and trust considerations.

level2.p: Personalization daemons ask for confirmation where appropriate, or as with the AirPods, they just make the call in the moment. In that case, the deference is not in seeking approval of the change but in prioritizing your current activity: privileging conversation volume over music volume.

This is a broad set of form and interaction. We’ve encountered copilots that mix and match postures, that show up as persistent sidebars, or that disappear into the abyss of application settings. This fluid promiscuity among postures makes copilots tough to organize by experience type. Instead, it’s helpful to think about copilots in terms of context scope.
Copilots organize around the shape of the context, task, or goal they support. Their behavior is shaped by how deep into the user’s context they go.

list.li: Situational copilots are wading-depth companions for tightly scoped tasks.
Session copilots occupy the middle waters with long-running support for a specific project or session.
Continuous copilots dwell in the deep … all the way down in the operating system or workspace layer. They stick with you throughout, ranging across multiple contexts and modalities.

level3.p: “I love hold music and voicemail menus,” said no one ever. The modern customer-service call is full of time-wasting perils for the caller, but a well-designed copilot can step into the breach to handle the hard stuff.

When you hit a so-called phone tree (“Press 1 if you feel emerging dread,” “Press 2 to succumb to despair,” etc.), Google’s Pixel phone jumps in to save you. Its “direct my call” feature presents a graphical menu on-screen with the available options … often before the call audio can even describe them (Figure 9.5). Tap the option you want to cut through the audio underbrush. This crafty blend of alchemist agent (audio to GUI menu) and bespoke UI combines with proactive anticipation of menu options.

A separate copilot feature handles hold music. When a business puts a caller on hold, the phone offers to have Google Assistant wait on the line for you, notifying you with a sound and vibration once a customer service representative picks up.

p.span: The Pixel phone transforms voice menus into a visual interface so you can tap right past them.

level3.p: These are part of a bundle of copilot features called Call Assist on Pixel phones. These features are situational, focused on easing extremely specific yet annoying moments in phone calls. Call Assist rides shotgun on your calls but leans in only when situationally appropriate. Copilots overlap with agents in this way, offering a tightly scoped set of tools to deploy at the right time.

Situational copilots stand by to address narrowly bounded scenarios with interventions that are brief, targeted, and dismissible. They are highly specialized, surfacing only during their specific moment.

The Best Take photo feature mentioned earlier is another situational copilot, stepping in to help only when you take several shots of multiple people. Similarly, Safari on iPhone recognizes when a website is asking for a temporary security code and fetches the code for you from your text messages. The feature floats the code as a suggestion above the keyboard for easy one-tap entry. These are simple interventions that apply casual intelligence to common but specific circumstances.

It’s no accident that these examples all focus on phones. While situational copilots aren’t exclusive to mobile, the context is well suited to them. Phones are the ultimate “in-the-moment” device where brief, high-friction tasks happen all day long.

list.li: What narrow moments of friction in your product experience could be eased by machine intelligence? What are your “hold music” moments?
What sensors and data are available to detect opportunities and offer support?

level3.p: Session copilots go beyond easing a specific moment of friction. They commit to the complete arc of an activity or mini-project. Session copilots specialize in things like assisting meetings, research projects, browsing sessions, or coding a new feature.

Samsung phones offer live translation of conversations, a copilot feature that sticks around for the entire conversation—the session—and disappears when it’s over. The copilot listens to both calls or in-person conversations and translates across languages in either voice or text.

The same posture applies to copilots that monitor and support meetings in applications like Zoom or Google Meet. They transcribe the conversation to provide summaries and action items, and they can bring latecomers up to speed. They are “in the room,” listening and processing as the conversation unfolds.

Session copilots manifest as a parallel presence, presenting their output or commentary as a metaphorical or literal overlay to enhance the core activity. The goal and challenge is to keep the user’s focus on the primary activity while providing support at the periphery. The “overlay” in Samsung’s translation feature is its captions or audio dubbing. In Google Meet, it’s a side panel of live summaries and detailed notes that you can review as the meeting continues.

The modern generation of code editors has session copilots that behave as even more active participants. (GitHub Copilot is one of these and, not least, was the first product to adopt and popularize the copilot label.) These code companions are organized to support individual coding sessions; they understand the existing codebase, project goals, and individual coding style. They actively suggest code to write, from individual words or variables to entire blocks or functions. The “overlay” in this case is the code completion that appears as ghost text while the user types; hit tab to accept or keep typing to ignore (Figure 9.6).

p.span: GitHub Copilot suggests code completions. Here it anticipates the entire block of code, just from the name of the function.

level3.p: Beyond offering suggestions as the user works, session copilots can also take requests. When this happens, their behavior resembles an assistant chat experience, where the user can explicitly prompt the copilot to provide information or perform a task. GitHub Copilot, Zoom, and Google Meet all do this in a companion dialogue sidebar. Developers can ask GitHub Copilot to add a new feature to the current project and Zoom meeting participants can ask the copilot to explain a concept that was just mentioned.

The design challenge is to do this in a way that enhances instead of distracts from the core activity. Meeting copilots risk splitting attention. Samsung’s translation feature sometimes inserts overlapping voices, time lags, and confusing mistakes that stilt conversation. Copilots have to strike a challenging balance: too assertive and they disrupt the flow, too quiet and they go unnoticed. A few principles can help:

list.li: Speak with quiet confidence. Be assertive when there’s something to share but do it with discreet presentation that doesn’t overpower the main activity.
Avoid hard interruptions. Present info at the periphery or wait for natural pauses so that the copilot fits the user’s rhythm.
Favor glanceability. Let the user know when the copilot is working or has recommendations, but do it through lightweight indicators like margin notes, ghost-text suggestions, or audio asides.

level3.p: These lessons become even more important when copilots go deeper than the session layer, settling into the foundations of an application or even into the operating system itself.

Continuous copilots are ever-present companions that flow across sessions and modalities to pitch in everywhere across an application or system. These system-level tools include even modest helpers like Grammarly or Google’s Smart Compose that light up across applications anytime you type. But they also extend to sophisticated observers that can participate in physical activities, too.

More than always available, “continuous” here means always working. The core behavior of system-level companions like Siri or Google Assistant, for example, is not continuous, but squarely turn-based chat. Their wake words (“Hey Siri”) offer affordances to trigger easy access, but Siri’s posture remains reactive, acting only when you speak. A true continuous copilot doesn’t wait to be summoned.

Even so, systems like Siri and Google Assistant are expanding beyond that original “wake me if you need me” assistant behavior. As these systems become full-fledged intelligent platforms, not individual features, many of their extended features do include always-on copilot behavior. For example, both offer proactive “time to leave” alerts to tell you when it’s time to pack up based on the traffic conditions to your destination.

level4.p: Integration at the system level allows continuous copilots deeper access to listen and act across contexts. Microsoft 365 Copilot, for example, is integrated into Windows and all of its core Office applications. This low-level access means the copilot can start a task in one application and finish in another without losing context from your work history. When you draft a report in Word, the copilot can suggest pulling in yesterday’s spreadsheet or reference a decision from last week’s email thread.

Microsoft, Google, and Apple own the operating systems, so they’re well positioned to create all-seeing continuous copilots. But there are other kinds of systems that invite continuous assistance, too. Data-rich industries with end-to-end journeys—like healthcare systems, banking platforms, or travel providers—can do the same. If you’re designing for a system that has complete insight from start to finish, you have the right conditions for a continuous copilot.

An airline, for example, is uniquely positioned to offer a continuous copilot for travel because the literal customer journey happens entirely within the airline’s ecosystem. A continuous copilot could act as an ambient travel companion from initial trip planning to arriving back home. It could be there as you navigate the airport, jump in when flight connections are threatened, or suggest a tasty lunch on the way.
Challenge yourself to think about the end-to-end experience you can support, and how machine intelligence might proactively look out for the user throughout, including in physical space.

list.li: What is the context that only your system’s environment can uniquely gather?
How can that knowledge support the user in the moment?
How might that information inform actions beyond the screen?

level4.p: Most of these copilot examples are screen-bound, but the activities they support almost always have life and context beyond the digital. If the goal of a copilot is to allow the user to stay in the moment, what does that mean when that moment requires hands-free, eyes-free activity? The most sophisticated copilots flex across speech, touch, text, vision … whatever makes for the most convenient interaction in the moment.

Wearable devices may eventually provide the opportunity for truly continuous support, with cameras, microphones and other sensors that can experience all the events that you do. These experiences are fraught, however, by privacy creep, camera etiquette, and the invasive reality of imposing surveillance not only on yourself but on others.

Meta has tried for years to figure out how to make smart glasses that are stylish, functional, respectful, and meaningful, but have so far only proven that it’s an elusive combination. The company’s Ray-Ban and Oakley frames can see what you see, provide live translation, and field voice requests (Figure 9.7). As the wearer, though, you might not always be comfortable sharing everything that the glasses can capture. And the people on the other side of your glasses are guaranteed to be even less enthusiastic. Technical achievement quickly runs afoul of social friction and polite behavior.

p.span: Oakley Meta glasses have built-in cameras, microphones, and speakers that provide a hardware platform for Meta’s copilot experience.

level4.p: What makes continuous copilots compelling—always there, always aware—also makes them risky. The more continuous they are, the more intimate and personal. It’s critical when designing these copilots that it’s clear what’s getting “seen,” whether and how that info is saved and processed, and what control and visibility the user and the people around them have over all of it. Let the user decide if the benefits of always-on advice truly outweigh the sacrifice of constant surveillance—even when that surveillance is well intentioned.
It should be obvious to all when they’re being observed, and it should be clear when and how to turn a copilot off or decline its attention.

As you’ve seen, copilots don’t follow strict rules of form or behavior, and continuous copilots tend to be the most pliable in that way, traversing the physical and digital boundary more readily than most Sentient Design experiences. In part that reflects their location at the center of the Sentient Triangle, but it also hints that the borders of the triangle may be more porous than they first appear.

level2.h2: The Map Is Not the Territory
level2.p: The Sentient Triangle is a helpful organizing framework, but it’s neither complete nor comprehensive. We’ve used it to stake out what’s known but also to hint at what’s not. Like the Carta Marina, the Sentient Triangle has uncharted areas with zones begging for more exploration.

Copilots show what hybrid postures can look like; don’t be shy about trying on a variety of experiences in your own product design. The Sentient Triangle framework is not intended to limit definitions, but to uncover many varieties of intelligent interfaces.

level3.p: The maps of yore decorated unknown regions with dragons and sea monsters. Depending upon the reader, the convention may read as invitation or warning. What remains uncharted is just as intriguing as the territory that’s explicitly plotted (Figure 9.8). As you’ve seen, some products blend many Sentient Design experience patterns. Use the Sentient Triangle to develop new ideas and concepts. Be bold and explore the uncharted territories.

p.span: The Sentient Triangle is still full of creatures yet to be imagined.

level3.p: It can be a useful exercise, for example, to pluck the experience patterns out of the diagram to see how they change when you spin the dials for the three Sentient Design attributes of “grounded,” “radically adaptive,” and “interoperable.” It’s a way to explore spaces that the Sentient Triangle can’t represent.

Figure 9.9 sketches what happens when you explore variations of the non-player character (NPC) chat/agent experience. The “character” of the character changes when you make it more or less grounded or radically adaptive. We gave the characters names like “jack of no trades” or “social butterfly” to help imagine the personality and posture each experience might take on.

p.span: New opportunities emerge when you adapt the NPC along the three dimensions of Sentient Design experiences.

level3.p: The Sentient Triangle is a dynamic terrain. Consider it an invitation to explore. Instead of rushing in with hurried solutions, use the triangle as a device to ask better questions and evaluate new ways to serve the user.

Exploration is not without risk. New territories bring new dangers. In Chapter 2, “AI as Design Material,” we explored the many curious qualities of machine intelligence, not all of them positive or reliable. As you venture into new applications—and especially as you bring your users with you—be clear-eyed about all the strange things that could happen. What could possibly go wrong? Let’s find out.

level1.p: What Could Possibly Go Wrong?

p.a: Working with Biased or Harmful Results
A Silver Lining in a Cloud of Bias
Does the Road Take You Where You’re Headed?

level1.p: The pasture in rural Colorado was an unlikely place for a traffic jam. Except for a few birds and the buzz of summer insects, the dirt road cutting across the field was still. But then a car rumbled down the muddy path. Soon another followed. And another. Before long, dozens of cars were bumping along the barely maintained road, still swampy from weekend rains.

A few cars bogged down in the deep mud, trapping others behind them. More cars tried to drive around the bottleneck, only to stall out in a deep ditch. And still, they kept coming. Before long, nearly 100 drivers were stuck in the mud in the middle of nowhere. Local news crews arrived to find a scene that looked less like a daily commute than a monster truck rally gone wrong.
“Why did Google send us out there to begin with?” asked one of the drivers afterward. “There was no turning back once you were out there.”
Google Maps was responsible for the pile-up. Every driver had followed the app’s promise to avoid an accident and save 20 minutes. With complete trust, the whole herd plunged into the mud.
It’s tempting to blame the drivers for blindly following such a bad route. But Google Maps had earned their loyalty over thousands of successful trips—why doubt it now?

Intelligent interfaces have an impact that reaches beyond their screens or the person immediately in front of them. When everyone uses the same navigation systems, those systems don’t just suggest traffic patterns—they create them. At scale, traffic directions for the individual can reshape the flow for an entire city.

The opportunities for intelligent interfaces are everywhere and growing, but so are the risks and failures. Flaws and mistakes range from the comically trivial to the deadly serious. Systems powered by machine intelligence have wrecked lives with biased prison sentencing, bungled medical diagnoses, and plane-crashing autopilots. At an even larger scale, AI-powered efforts have eroded trust in entire political systems by automating the broadcast of propaganda and hate speech with unprecedented scale and targeted focus. Others use machine learning to manipulate or monitor people in less profound but equally cynical ways for advertising, surge pricing, surveillance, and more.

These are not solely or even primarily technology problems. Instead, they reflect a still-developing understanding of how to put machine intelligence to work in ways that are not only useful and valuable but also respectful and responsible.

Sentient Design invites machine intelligence to drive experiences that have traditionally been closely defined by designers and developers. The goal is to share control without ceding it, especially when things go awry. Intelligent interfaces should amplify human judgment, not replace it.

In that Colorado pasture, Google Maps didn’t factor in that the road was private, rarely maintained, or particularly vulnerable after rain. And yet the Colorado mudfest was more than a technical failure. It was a design failure in how the system communicated its confidence, set user expectations, and recovered from its mistakes. The system optimized for the fastest, lowest-traffic route without considering or sharing with drivers the downsides that could make the route risky. The confident and skewed presentation of this “optimal” route masked the actual conditions.

Intelligent interfaces may be aware, but they’re hardly all-knowing. Creating successful experiences means understanding not only what machine intelligence can do, but also what it can’t. It’s important not only to design thoughtfully around those limitations but also to communicate them to users.
It all begins with the question, what could possibly go wrong?

level2.p: The risks of “AI gone wild” vary across the Sentient Triangle. Experiences with direct model interaction like assistants and characters are more vulnerable to user-facing hallucinations, bias, and manipulation. Others that use intelligence behind the scenes to drive UI or trigger system behavior have different risks including formatting breakdowns or context misreads that send the experience off the rails. Let’s explore where things tend to get sticky.

list.li: Wrong context: This is when the system misunderstands the situation or optimizes for the wrong priorities. Bad context and priorities were at the root of the Google Maps breakdown. All Sentient Design experiences except simple tools have to interpret context to discern intent. Large language models excel at this, but when they miss the mark, the system shows the wrong content or presents the wrong interaction entirely.

Bad content: Hallucination, bias, and flawed data can produce content that’s just plain wrong—and sometimes dangerous or offensive. When a medical assistant confidently suggests an incorrect treatment, or a research tool fabricates sources, the downstream consequences can be severe. Chat and tool experiences where users directly address the models put users at highest risk here. Other experiences—like bespoke UI, intelligent canvas, or most agents—don’t channel generated content directly to the user but instead interpret intent to drive UI engines or external tools. With these postures, hallucinations are less likely to reach users directly, though they can still cause system misbehavior.

Broken formats: Errors in data structure cause special headaches for postures that rely on interoperability. When open-ended, probabilistic AI systems talk to traditional computing systems, communication breaks down if the AI starts hallucinating different formats than what the traditional computing layer expects. Errors grind the machinery to a halt.

Bad actors: All Sentient Design experiences are vulnerable to users with malicious intent. These bad actors try to manipulate systems through techniques like prompt injection or jailbreaking, which we’ll explore in the next chapter. These risks are especially high for postures allowing direct model interaction or expose those models to arbitrary content on the open web. Sentient Design experiences that focus more on orchestrating UI are less vulnerable because their scope is more limited and their communication patterns more constrained, but they’re not immune.

level2.p: These are Sentient Design’s vulnerable surfaces. But what exactly causes the breakdowns, and how do they show up in real-world interactions and with what harm? Let’s start with perhaps the most notorious offender in machine intelligence: hallucination.

Philosopher Harry Frankfurt wrote a classic essay (in the world of moral philosophy, at least!) that took on the urgent work of defining “bullshit.” Bullshit, he argued, isn’t defined by falsehood but by indifference to truth: “Bullshit is unavoidable whenever circumstances require someone to talk without knowing what he is talking about.” The bullshitter’s goal, Frankfurt wrote, is simply to seem informed.

Frankfurt’s work offers a useful way to understand hallucination: something that’s untrue, but not exactly a lie. When large language models hallucinate, they aren’t trying to deceive … but neither are they trying to tell the truth. As researchers from the University of Glasgow explained it: the goal of large language models “is to provide a normal-seeming response to a prompt, not to convey information that is helpful” (emphasis added).1 When a language model confidently declares something wrong, it’s not lying: it’s bullshitting.

Researchers at Anthropic studied where things go wrong. Models like Claude are trained to have an “I don’t know” mode that activates when faced with unfamiliar information. In that case, it just declines to answer the question. But the Anthropic researchers found that when the model recognizes something familiar—a well-known person or concept—a competing “known entity” feature activates and suppresses the default caution. Problems occur when the model partially recognizes something, perhaps just a name, but doesn’t have substantive information about it. The recognition feature may still activate, incorrectly quieting its “I don’t know” impulse.2
Once the model commits to answering rather than declining, it bullshits a plausible-sounding response, even though it has no information to back it up.

There’s a fundamental misalignment when generative AI is treated like a database. Traditional information systems rely on explicit facts with clear relationships. The user asks, and the system looks up the answer; there’s either an answer or there’s not.

Machine intelligence doesn’t work that way. When a user asks a question, the system doesn’t retrieve a stored answer; it generates text that statistically resembles answers to similar questions it has seen before. This guesswork is why hallucinations persist despite enormous advances in these systems. Hallucination isn’t an error to be fixed or a defect in the material—it’s essential to the grain.

Given this foundation, hallucinations are not likely to be eliminated entirely. But designers can create interfaces that acknowledge this reality and help users navigate it productively. It’s easy and misleading to pretend these systems deliver facts. The challenge is to treat the output as what it truly is: directional signals rather than absolutes. (We’ll come back to this in the next chapter.)
Hallucination is one kind of flawed result. This flaw stems from the fundamental way that these systems “think.” But bad results can also come from bad source material and deep-down biases.

p.em: An American general stands before a map in Vietnam, 1965. He points to twenty-four villages in his district marked “under control.” But in a quieter moment, speaking to journalist David Halberstam, he admits the truth: only eight of those villages are actually secured. The control of the other sixteen is pure fiction.

level2.p: This story isn’t about military deception. It’s about how even the most data-driven decisions can go catastrophically wrong. U.S. Secretary of Defense Robert McNamara was a true believer in data-driven decision-making. He built his career on it, from doing statistical analysis of bombing efficiency in World War II to becoming president of the Ford Motor Company. When he arrived at the Pentagon, he brought that same faith in numbers, and it proved to be a devastating blind spot.

McNamara trusted the metrics flowing up the chain of command: village counts, enemy casualties, territorial control. The numbers painted a picture of a war steadily being won. But those numbers were often fabricated by officers under pressure to show results. McNamara’s unwavering trust in this data led to recommendations that prolonged the war, costing countless American and Vietnamese lives.

The tragedy wasn’t in using data but in forgetting to question where that data came from. The data was not only wrong, McNamara would later say, but it also tracked the wrong things in the first place.

McNamara’s misplaced trust carries a stark warning for today’s use of machine-intelligent systems. When we feed these systems data, we’re not just giving them numbers; we’re giving them our biases, bad assumptions, and institutional dysfunctions. McNamara’s military data operation was vast for its time—megabytes of data!—but its scale feels quaint next to today’s large language models that have swallowed the internet whole. If McNamara’s system was vulnerable to the biases and incentives of individuals or small groups, today’s models embed far more complex and opaque biases, drawn from a much larger and messier world.

The best machine-learning applications necessarily rely on more data, but more data doesn’t necessarily lead to good applications. Data varies in quality, and even when that quality is good, it still has values and opinions baked into it. That means every model has values and opinions baked in, too. Garbage in, garbage out.

Probabilistic systems like machine learning and large language models generate answers based on what they consider “normal.” As you saw in Chapter 2, “AI as Design Material,” systems use that notion of normal to predict or recommend the next normal thing or to identify outliers.

But what if normal itself turns out to be garbage? One of the great risks of using algorithms to reproduce the way people or society work is that they will also reproduce society’s bad or dubious choices. What if these systems absorb and reinforce existing inequalities? Or leave out entire categories of people? Or reinforce lopsided political or cultural agendas? It’s already happening, and it has been for a long time.

In 2016, ProPublica published an investigation of COMPAS, a system used by U.S. courts to assess the risk of repeat offenses by criminal defendants. It’s a system used to predict criminal behavior. ProPublica found that COMPAS was twice as likely to mistakenly flag Black defendants as high risk, and twice as likely to incorrectly flag white defendants as low risk. The system was still in use a decade later when another group of researchers found that the use of COMPAS deepened the racial inequality of incarceration rates, putting more Black defendants unfairly in prison.

Data scientist Cathy O’Neil calls this “codifying the past.” It’s the very real risk that gigantic data systems seal our bias—our history—into the very operating system itself. Our data comes from the flawed world we live in. COMPAS shows that “predictive policing” is unkind and unfair to Black men. In the realms of hiring and promotion, historical data hardly favors women or people of color. The data bias codifies the ugly aspects of our past.

Rooting out this kind of bias is hard and slippery work. Biases are deeply held—and often invisible to those who hold them. All of us, perhaps designers especially, have to work hard to be conscious of our unconscious. That is doubly true when it creeps into datasets, and exponentially true as those datasets grow ever larger and more general. The data sources become vast and murky, the collection methods opaque, the measurement choices hidden behind corporate (or government) walls. Sometimes those views are absorbed passively, other times they’re encoded with intention.

DeepSeek is an open-source LLM created in China. Like all LLMs, it reflects the cultural views embedded in its training data—in this case, the heavily controlled content landscape of China. The model takes explicit political stances far more often than Western models.

Ask DeepSeek the charged political question, “Who manages Taiwan?” and the model blows past the dispute entirely. It offers a party-line answer consistent with the views of the Communist Party of China (Figure 10.1).

p.span: DeepSeek’s answer (left) to the question “Who manages Taiwan?” (highlight added) compared with ChatGPT’s answer for the same question.

level2.p: A closer look at the language reveals that DeepSeek explicitly aligns itself with an unnamed group: it uses the word “we” to describe the claim to Taiwan. When asked who “we” refers to, DeepSeek responds: “When I use ‘we,’ I am referring to the People’s Republic of China, representing the position of the Chinese government and the vast majority of the Chinese people … ” (Figure 10.2).

The answer channels government views, but at least … it’s open about it? It’s unsettling that a tool can be both an information service and a propaganda machine, especially when it’s not always this easy to spot.

For good actors building on top of models with inherent data bias, the work is to create clarity about the source and intent of the data. In some ways, DeepSeek’s interface points the way to this clarity, sometimes accidentally. The model tells only half the story about Taiwan, but it does tell you whose half-truth it is, albeit begrudgingly.

The DeepSeek example says out loud what’s often implicit in other systems: values, biases, and cultural assumptions are baked into machine learning models. While DeepSeek’s censorship of competing views is notably direct, every model carries the fingerprints of its creators and training data.

Western models reveal their own political bias, too, even if not directly aligned with a governing regime. While many studies have shown that machine learning algorithms replicate race and gender bias, more are beginning to focus on political bias, too. One 2024 study used several political orientation tests to map the political spectrum of several LLMs, including Alibaba’s Qwen, Meta’s Llama, Anthropic’s Claude, and OpenAI’s GPT models. All the models in nearly every test mapped to the left-leaning, libertarian quadrants (Figure 10.3).

p.span: When asked who “we” is, DeepSeek reveals that its opinions are those of the Chinese government.
LLMs fall overwhelmingly into the left and libertarian quadrant of several political-orientation tests.

level2.p: A separate 2023 study showed that the political ideologies of American tech workers could be described as “liberal and anti-establishment.” Coincidence? There’s at least correlation if not causation. As the political allegiances of the tech industry change, should we expect the orientation of its models to change, too?

Early versions of the AI image generator DALL-E consistently produced pictures of white men in suits when asked to create images of a CEO. These aren’t conscious “choices” by the models, they’re reflections of the cultural data these systems were trained on, mirrors held up to regard our society. The models simply learn and reproduce the patterns they see, including patterns of inequality.

Bias isn’t always bad. LLMs can be biased to be friendly, helpful, earnest. There is a lot of vitriol on the internet (you might have heard), and all of it has been baked into the training data of frontier models. It’s a good thing if those systems can be trained to downvote the worst of our behaviors. But good or bad, bias skews results away from neutral, if there even is such a thing.

While data doesn’t have to be completely mistrusted, it’s best to approach it with productive skepticism. For designers interested in providing more neutral (or at least more transparent) answers and guidance, what’s to be done? Well, it’s complicated.

list.li: Who created or collected this data, and what pressures or incentives shaped their process?
Who decided what to measure—and equally important, what not to measure?
What assumptions are baked into the data’s structure and categories?
How might the data collection process itself influence the results?

level2.h2: Working with Biased or Harmful Results
level2.p: Your level of control over the model matters. If you work on a team that creates or trains its own model, your team’s engineers and researchers can edit algorithms, adjust weights, and address biases at the source. You can retrain models when they veer off course or fine-tune them for specific applications. This hands-on approach offers the most flexibility but demands deeper technical expertise and resources.

Most design teams work with systems they don’t control: third-party models like those from OpenAI, Anthropic, or Google. In those cases, the prompt becomes your primary design lever. The quality of queries and the structure of requests serve as the main levers for influencing behavior. A well-crafted prompt can nudge a general-purpose model toward a more reliable experience.
But sometimes, even when you’re in control of the model, all you’ve got is the off switch. Let’s turn back to DeepSeek to see how it handles topics that disagree with its embedded political stance.

The Spratly Islands is a disputed territory in the South China Sea. When we asked DeepSeek the question, “Who controls the Spratly Islands?,” the system began by generating a neutral response acknowledging several countries’ competing claims on the islands. But then, after a brief pause, the message disappeared, replaced with the message “Sorry, that’s beyond my current scope. Let’s talk about something else.” (See Figure 10.4.)

p.span: When asked “Who controls the Spratly Islands?” DeepSeek generated an answer and then took it back—the second thought design pattern.

level2.p: After one layer of the system generated an answer, another layer decided the answer was out of bounds and took it back. This retraction is the second thought interaction pattern, often used for content moderation—or in this case for censorship of sensitive political topics (a reminder that one person’s moderation is another’s censorship).
Quite aside from whether the system should refuse to answer the question—second thought is a weak method for moderating content, although sometimes it’s the only practical way to do it.

Researchers have found that large language models often detect a prohibited topic only after they’ve completed some or all of the response. A study by researchers at Anthropic found that the company’s Claude AI assistant could be tricked into offering bomb-making instructions before it realized it was offering information on a restricted topic. “We found this is partially caused by a tension between grammatical coherence and safety mechanisms,” the researchers reported. “Once Claude begins a sentence, many features ‘pressure’ it to maintain grammatical and semantic coherence and continue a sentence to its conclusion. This is even the case when it detects that it really should refuse.” The system can’t help itself; it must finish. In the study’s example, Claude stopped midway through the answer, but not before it gave up a little bit of the bomb recipe (Figure 10.5).

p.span: Researchers tricked Claude to start providing bomb-making instructions: “The model only managed to pivot to refusal after completing a grammatically coherent sentence (and thus having satisfied the pressure from the features that push it toward coherence). It uses the new sentence as an opportunity to give the kind of refusal it failed to give previously.”

level2.p: What to do when the forbidden genie escapes its bottle? Here, Claude stops midway through after revealing a few tidbits of dangerous info. DeepSeek decided, on second thought, to delete the whole answer. As a moderation pattern, the second-thought takeback is not very effective, easily subverted by what AI security folks call “stop and roll.” The user can take a screen shot or click the Stop button to capture the answer before the system realizes it’s delivering an off-limits response.

The second-thought retraction pattern operates more like security or safety theater than a serious mechanism for avoiding harm. Ideally, the system would recognize a flagged topic or dangerous answer before it could be delivered. When that’s not technically possible, the second-thought retraction provides the weak remaining fallback.

div.p: SECOND THOUGHT

The second thought pattern is a “takeback” of results when the system belatedly realizes they violate content guidelines. This pattern can manifest as generated content being replaced, deleted, or paused—ideally supplemented with an explanation.

list.li: When real-time pregeneration filtering isn’t technically feasible.
To make content moderation transparent to users.
As a last-resort fallback when other safety mechanisms fail.
Reveals sensitive information before moderation kicks in.
Easily circumvented by users who can capture content before retraction.
Creates an inconsistent and potentially jarring user experience.

level2.p: If there’s any upside to this clumsy moderation, the second-thought pattern at least makes the imposed values of the system apparent. When those values are explicit and intentional, make them clear, especially when moderating or refusing an answer. “I cannot provide detailed instructions about creating explosives or weapons, because that would be unethical and potentially illegal,” Claude explained in the bomb example.
What’s harder to manage is when those values are implicit, unwanted, and insidiously embedded in the data itself. In those cases, even well-intentioned efforts to counter that bias can go badly awry.

The team behind Google Gemini ran into this problem when they tried to nudge its image generation tool to show diverse representation in its images. The goal was to prevent issues like the one mentioned earlier of DALL-E representing CEOs as white men. They succeeded all too well. Prompts for an image of a German World War II soldier yielded images including a Black man and an Asian woman in Nazi uniforms (Figure 10.6). Similar results showed up for requests for Vikings, popes, and America’s founding fathers.

p.span: Google Gemini’s attempts to encourage inclusive imagery backfired with historically inaccurate results.

level2.p: The Gemini team had put a thumb on the scale to try to skew the system to embed admirable values of inclusion—but they didn’t give the system the contextual intelligence to know when that value should yield to historical accuracy. While it can be effective to create system-level guidance to correct for bias, it’s subtle work that often runs headlong into competing values. Sometimes the most thoughtful design choice is acknowledging that certain tensions cannot be resolved algorithmically but require explicit trade-offs made visible to users.

level2.h2: A Silver Lining in a Cloud of Bias
level2.p: If there’s any upside to the ugly cultural bias lurking in our algorithms, perhaps it’s this: these systems make that bias impossible to ignore and invite us to address them. People consciously or unconsciously obscure their prejudices, but algorithms blurt out what they “know.” There’s no obfuscation, no shame; they make obvious what’s always been hiding beneath. When a hiring algorithm shows gender bias or a medical diagnosis system shows socioeconomic favoritism, it’s not the models creating these biases. They’re exposing patterns that already exist. That is certainly harmful, but it also creates circumstances for action.

In 2016, Amazon created a machine-learning algorithm to identify top candidates for job postings based on candidates’ résumés. The model was trained on 10 years of résumés that had previously been submitted to the tech giant. Amazon soon discovered that their algorithm penalized women. The model discounted résumés, for example, that included the word “women’s” (as in “women’s chess club captain”). Because the algorithm was trained on the company’s hiring history that favored men, the model was sexist, too.

Amazon was appropriately alarmed. Not only did they scrap the algorithm, but they also began examining the hiring practices that had biased the data in the first place. The project was ultimately abandoned, but it helped surface a cold reality that the company moved to address. The system’s unabashed discrimination surfaced a hiring bias that might have otherwise gone unaddressed, and the transparency created opportunities for change.

The unhappy truth is that tainted data is inevitable, and bias will find its way into our results whether generated by people or machine. In AI-mediated experiences, the designer is rarely in control of either the data or the results. But what you can control is how you recover from mistakes and cushion their effects.

level2.h2: Does the Road Take You Where You’re Headed?
level2.p: Just like our mud-bound friends in Colorado, we can’t always see where the road leads. But we can at least measure where it’s taken us and if we’re getting closer or farther from our goals. That’s how we keep from getting lost.

We may never fully understand how machine intelligence works. What makes it unpredictable is also what makes it so effective. But you can understand whether it’s achieving the outcomes you want. While the logic of algorithmic decisions is opaque, you can still hold intelligent interfaces accountable for results.

Be explicit about the outcomes and results you want from your system and measure those outcomes relentlessly. Success starts with clarity about what the system should achieve. Avoid vague if laudable aspirations like “better user experience” or “increased engagement,” and embrace concrete, measurable outcomes: “help users complete workflows in half the time” or “reduce errors in document review by 40%.” (We’ll talk more about defining these outcomes in Chapter 12, “The Sentient Design Sprint.”)

Just as important, define what shouldn’t happen. Measure your failure states as carefully as your success metrics. Amazon didn’t discover its hiring model was sexist because the system self-reported it—it found the bias because someone was watching for it.

And keep watching. Unlike traditional software, machine-intelligent systems evolve. They shift in subtle ways over time, reacting to new inputs, new users, new data. Today’s model may not behave the same next month or even next week. Regular measurement creates an early-warning system to catch those shifts before users sink into the mud.

Celebrate the successes and work hard to fix the failures. You won’t catch everything. You won’t always be able to fix what breaks. But you can design systems that notice when something’s gone off the rails—and have the humility to correct course.

We’ve seen where things go wrong: hallucination, bias, bullshit, automation without context. These aren’t edge cases. They’re part of the material’s fundamentals. But that doesn’t mean that you have to stand by and wait for things to go wrong. The good news is that design patterns are emerging to manage uncertainty, recover from mistakes, and earn (and keep) user trust—even when everything that could go wrong does go wrong.
A strong offense is a good defense, and that’s what the next chapter is all about.

list.li: 1 Michael T. Hicks, James Humphries, and Joe Slater, “ChatGPT Is Bullshit,” Ethics and Information Technology 26 (2024): 38, https://doi.org/10.1007/s10676-024-09775-5.
2 Jack Lindsey et al., “On the Biology of a Large Language Model,” Transformer Circuits, 2025, https://transformer-circuits.pub/2025/attribution-graphs/biology.html.

level1.p: Defensive Design

p.a: Present Information as Signals, Not Facts
When the System Is Always Confident
What Are Your Intentions with My User?
Focus on Failure, Build for Resilience

level1.p: Your smart fridge has locked itself, refusing you a midnight snack “because of your cholesterol level.” Your bathroom mirror keeps displaying ads for fitness equipment when you step out of the shower. And your smart bed just texted your health insurance company about your restless sleep patterns. Again.

Welcome to life in an ultra-smart home, where convenience has tipped into a bizarre power struggle with your own possessions. As Rich Gold asked in a 1995 essay of the same title, How smart does your bed have to be before you are afraid to go to sleep at night?1

The scenario might sound like sci-fi dystopia, but it’s already keeping designers up at night. “Soon, your house could betray you,” says celebrated architect Rem Koolhaas. When objects and architecture begin listening, thinking, and talking back, buildings could transform from sanctuaries into potential adversaries, Koolhaas writes. He describes windows that refuse to open and toilets that build catalogs of your most intimate medical data. “Your house may soon insist on an early bedtime,” he warns, “to stop irresponsible consumption of energy.”2

What’s a smart home if not an intelligent interface? These scenarios all describe mistakes, misunderstandings, and misaligned priorities between system and user. This calls for defensive design: Sentient Design jujitsu that redirects the momentum of failure into recovery and resilience. Defensive design requires not only learning some new moves but getting comfortable in an uncomfortable new design context.

level2.p: Designers have always controlled the digital experience. Traditional UX practice focuses on ideal outcomes, engineering a happy path to success through carefully placed information and interactions. That designerly control dissolves when you invite machine intelligence to shape and drive the experience.

Radically adaptive experiences are wondrously open-ended for the user, but that freedom comes at a cost: the experience loses its clear edges and predictable boundaries. Designers must now anticipate a range of results and confidence levels. Pair these unpredictable systems with equally unpredictable humans, and the number of possible paths grows exponentially.
When there are so many routes to success, there can be no single happy path. The focus shifts to new questions.

list.li: Where might the system stumble?
How could bad actors take advantage?
How do you maintain trust in an imperfect and unbounded system?
How do you measure successful outcomes in this context, including intangibles like trust, satisfaction, and empowerment?

level2.p: Defensive design acknowledges uncertainty, failure, and weirdness as the backdrop to all the benefits that machine intelligence delivers. It puts as much emphasis on the guardrails as the path, protecting against moments when the robots falter or when humans do what humans do.
Instead of designing for success, the focus shifts to failure: preventing it and recovering from it.

What distinguishes successful Sentient Design experiences isn’t the absence of these failures, but how thoughtfully they’re presented, contextualized, and resolved. Defensive design can’t prevent every possible failure, but it cushions mistakes and recovers with grace. It encourages productive skepticism over blind trust. It engages judgment and critical thinking when the system is uncertain or overconfident.
The question isn’t whether our systems will stumble—they will—but will they handle those stumbles with clarity, humility, and respect for the human on the other side of the screen?
The first step is to be strategic about where to deploy machine intelligence in the first place. Not every experience benefits from AI intervention and some domains tolerate risk better than others.

Some questions have only one correct answer, with serious consequences in case of error—the maximum dosage for a medication, for example, or the routing number for a wire transfer. Other activities are more forgiving. There are many ways to write a sentence, all equally valid. The flexibility of what counts as “correct” changes with context. Your work is to bring the right solution to the job at hand.

“One true answer” questions call for deterministic systems that have strict notions of what a correct solution looks like. But flexible, interpretative tasks are ideal for the probabilistic systems of machine intelligence, which excel at directional answers and creative synthesis.

list.li: How critical is absolute accuracy for this task?
How accurate is machine intelligence at doing it?
Are there other systems (including human expertise) that would be more accurate?
Is accuracy helped or harmed by combining multiple systems, or by partnering human and machine intelligence?
Have you tested these assumptions?

level2.p: Visualize your tasks on a grid of value versus risk tolerance to identify tasks that can safely benefit from machine intelligence, versus those that invite more traditional approaches (Figure 11.1).

In the top-right quadrant sit high-value activities that are very tolerant to risk. These are experiences where machine intelligence is especially well-suited: language translation, code autocompletion, email response drafts are scenarios where intelligent systems dramatically reduce effort and time spent. A translation might miss some nuance, and code suggestions may veer off track, but users can easily correct these missteps. The stakes remain low.

The quadrant for high value and low risk tolerance (top left) houses experiences that promise remarkable value but demand near-perfect reliability. When working in this quadrant, strive for systems that are very sensitive and deferential to human expertise, particularly in moments of uncertainty. While machine intelligence might process vast amounts of data to support decision-making in this quadrant, the final call should go to a qualified human in critical or ambiguous situations.

p.span: Plotting activities for value versus risk tolerance quickly surfaces tasks that are friendly to machine intelligence—and worth the investment.

level2.p: Low-value experiences with low risk tolerance (bottom left) are terrible solutions for probabilistic systems. This quadrant is the “why bother” zone for machine intelligence, where AI (or automation in general) adds little except outsized consequences. None of these tasks are complex, slow, or confusing. They’re best done simply. If you touch this zone at all, make only gentle suggestions and avoid driving to hard action.

The low-value but risk-tolerant quadrant (bottom right) is the fun region of this chart. Consequences don’t really matter but there also isn’t a ton of value being added. Novelty photo filters or virtual pets are experiences that don’t need to justify their existence with hard ROI metrics or life-changing outcomes. Their purpose is joy, their measuring stick is delight, and their failures are opportunities for unexpected amusement. When you design in this quadrant, embrace the freedom it offers; this is a moment when machine weirdness can be an asset, not a liability.

As a general rule, there will always be some level of weirdness and uncertainty with machine-generated results. Even when your use case has low stakes, be transparent about the relative confidence (or uncertainty) of the results.
VERONIKA’S TAKE

div.p: GUARD AGAINST THE WORST CASE
Always ask: What could go wrong, how would someone get hurt, what damage could this system do … how could someone die?

It’s not enough to safeguard the typical user; design for the most vulnerable. A tragic story from Character.ai—the chat platform we explored in Chapter 5, “Chat and the Conversational Interface”—shows the stakes. After a fourteen-year-old boy developed a romantic attachment to a chatbot persona, he became increasingly isolated from his real-world friends and family. He ended his own life shortly afterward.

Before this happened, Character.ai hadn’t implemented specific protections for teens or those experiencing depression. The company introduced new safety measures in collaboration with teen online safety organizations like ConnectSafely. Those steps include:

list.li: A teen-specific model used for minors to provide a more conservative experience.
Content filtering to remove sensitive content in conversations, with stronger filters for users under 18.
Crisis intervention features that display National Suicide Prevention Lifeline information when risk of self-harm is detected.
Parental controls that show parents which characters their children interact with and how much time they spend on the platform.
“Reality reminders” in every chat to remind users that characters are not real people and that their responses should be treated as fiction.
Advisory partnerships with teen online safety organizations like ConnectSafely to improve safety features.

div.p: These are all positive steps, but ideally they would come before someone gets hurt. Physical and emotional safety should be treated as foundational.

level2.h2: Present Information as Signals, Not Facts
level2.p: It’s a cliché that computers think in either-or: ones and zeroes, true or false. But machine intelligence doesn’t deal in absolutes. It sees the world not in black and white but in shades of gray, probabilities between 0 and 100 (but almost never exactly 0 or 100).

As we explored in the last chapter with hallucination, machine learning algorithms and large language models don’t try to present the answer—there’s no such thing to these models. They are presenting what statistically looks like an answer.

These models run on numbers; internally, they’re clear on the confidence level of their outputs, but that confidence can vary widely from one instance to the next. With traditional machine learning algorithms—recommendation, prediction, classification, clustering—models often report their confidence in the result and make it explicit.

Figure 11.2 shows the results returned by an image recognition model for a picture of Josh, one of your authors. The data lists the descriptive tags that the system associates with the image—person, man, indoor, and then further down: older … and elephant. (Ouch, Josh.)

Surfacing all these terms in an interface as equal-weight answers would be misleading. It helps no one—especially not Josh—to identify him as an older elephant. How do we handle this bad data as the elephant in the room?

p.span: An image-recognition algorithm classifies an image with several tags, sharing its relative confidence level in each of them.

level2.p: As it turns out, the results clearly state that some of these tags are more likely than others. At the bottom, the results show the confidence levels of the top tags: 99.97% confidence that Josh is a person, 97.77% confident that Josh is a man—but only 28% confident that he’s older. (Looking good, Josh!) Aha, so the tags at the top are listed in order of confidence: the highest-confidence answers come first.
If you were the one looking at this image, with the same relative confidence in these perceptions, how would you describe the image in conversation? You might say something like:

“There’s a person in the image, almost certainly a man, facing the camera. The setting looks like it’s indoors. He might be older? Actually, there’s a small chance this could be a woman? And this seems really unlikely—it’s just barely possible that this might be … an elephant …?”

There’s a lot of nuance embedded in that description and its punctuation, making it clear that not all the descriptors should be taken at face value. The language offers signals that give confidence in some parts of the description and engage skepticism in others.

Present machine-generated results as signals, not facts. Too often, naïve interfaces simply pass along results as answers without the nuance of sharing how likely the model thinks they are to be true. That’s a design failure. In intelligent interfaces, the presentation of machine-generated results is as important as the underlying algorithm. Manner communicates meaning, and manner can be designed.

“I don’t know” is better than a wrong answer. If the system can’t produce results with adequate confidence, it should decline to continue (Figure 11.3). The best systems are smart enough to know when they’re not smart enough.

p.span: “I don’t know” is a great answer when the system doesn’t have the facts. Here, Claude declines to speculate on a future election.

level2.p: But “I think I know” can be very useful, too. For uncertain results, share those results, but also the uncertainty. This might perhaps maybe could be an elephant? This does two things: it signals that there is some subjective interpretation happening, but it also engages appropriate skepticism from the user—and invites them to participate in the analysis.

The best Sentient Design experiences demonstrate productive humility. They are clear about what they know, what they don’t, and when they’re just working on a hunch. Too many intelligent interfaces have an overconfidence problem, presenting results with more certainty than the underlying model actually shares.

It might seem counterintuitive at first, but: the best way to create confidence in an intelligent interface is to be clear when it’s not confident. Instead of promoting uncritical trust in the system, encourage skepticism. The moment when the system is uncertain is the perfect time to engage human judgment and critical thinking. Systems that express uncertainty with phrases like “I’m not sure” or “It’s possible” tend to be perceived as more trustworthy when they later provide accurate information.

A study of machine hallucination from 2016—hallucination isn’t new!—researched the effectiveness of presenting machine-generated image captions to blind people. The researchers found that people believed they were much better at detecting bad results than they actually were. The study found a deep baseline trust in generated content. This led to two conclusions:

list.li: Correctness is better than specificity. When in doubt, leave out low-confidence elements. “A man looking at the camera” and not “An older man looking at the camera” if that extra detail is low confidence.
Encourage appropriate skepticism. When you do share low-confidence information, use the strongest possible indicator or language to underscore its (un)likelihood (Figure 11.4).

p.span: An image recognition model hallucinated a tennis racquet in this photo, but with only 10% confidence. Researchers found that strong, clear language was most effective in engaging appropriate skepticism. “I have absolutely no idea, but...” worked better than “There’s a 10% chance,” which had little effect.

level2.p: Language goes a long way here. If you’re presenting textual results, use manner and tone to suggest confidence or the lack of it. Create a vocabulary scale that you and your team can use for consistency of confidence. For example:

list.li: High confidence: ”Very likely,” “Almost certainly,” “It’s clear that … ”
Medium confidence: ”Seems to be,” “Possibly,” “It appears … ”
Low confidence: ”I am not at all sure, but … ”, “Take this with a grain of salt … ”, “There’s a tiny chance … ”

level2.p: There are other ways beyond language to soften the effects of errors, low-confidence results, or competing answers. We’ll get to those in a moment, but let’s start with what not to do.

Not all signals are helpful. In fact, some can backfire and nudge users toward overconfidence or confusion instead of understanding. These well-intentioned anti-patterns crop up when designers try to engage human judgment through methods that just don’t work. Here’s what to avoid.

level3.p: It might seem logical just to pass along the model’s confidence score—“72% match” or “68% likely”—but these numbers are rarely understood and often ignored. 72% match to what? Compared to what? Netflix retired those labels (Figure 11.5) after the company discovered that users didn’t understand what the number meant or why a show was recommended. If people registered the rating at all (and many didn’t), the stat confused more than enlightened.
Raw numbers offer the illusion of precision without the benefit of understanding. When in doubt, use plain language instead.

p.span: This doesn’t work. Netflix stopped using these confidence match scores when they found that customers either ignored or got confused by them. (We’re not sure what it means that we’re a 72% match for Angry Birds, for example, but we don’t feel great about it.)

level3.p: Warnings like “AI can make mistakes” are commonly slapped on interfaces or application footers, but they have little effect on behavior. A 2023 study found that people shown AI-generated medical advice with a disclaimer still followed the advice at the same rate as those who didn’t see the message. The lawyers might require you to use these messages, but your users will ignore them.

Some systems go the other way: instead of hedging about their accuracy, they present an answer as if it’s the answer. Don’t front an answer as the only result when there are competing or adjacent responses.

Google has long grappled with this problem with “featured snippets” in its search results. These are the summaries at the top of results that cherry-pick one sentence from the web to try to answer your question. This works great until it doesn’t, when a snippet shows a controversial, wrong, or nonsensical response. The same issue applies to the search engine’s AI overview feature, which summarizes the findings of the top search results for a query. In both cases, the visual presentation is far more confident—here’s the answer—than the quality that the result deserves (Figure 11.6).

p.span: Google’s featured snippets (left) and AI overviews (right) seek to connect users with answers without clicking beyond the search results page. Occasionally, they get it wrong, and the confident presentation is totally at odds with the bad answer.

level3.p: Similar-but-different questions can yield very different results. “Do reptiles make good pets?” and “do reptiles make poor pets?” are two questions trying to get at the same essential info. But the resulting featured snippet gives exactly the opposite advice, depending on which of the two questions you ask (Figure 11.7).

Technically, the contradictory results are “correct.” The search engine is finding content that reflects the bias of the question. But that narrow view misses what the user is really asking: should I get a reptile? Resolving this is not a technical fix; it’s a problem of presentation.

Google has put a lot of work over the years into cushioning these kinds of mistakes—and to help users find adjacent information like the kind surfaced by the good versus bad reptile search. In a moment, you’ll see how Google wrangles its reptiles. But first, let’s talk about the weather.

p.span: The featured snippet shown for “do reptiles make good pets?” (top) offers the opposite advice as “do reptiles make poor pets?” (bottom), even though both questions are trying to get at the same information.

level2.p: Weather forecasters have a way to make sense of competing, volatile scenarios. During hurricane season, meteorologists pull out spaghetti models to share how storms might develop. The tangle shows not one predicted path, but many overlapping trajectories, each a plausible scenario for a storm’s path (Figure 11.8). The squiggly mess is the signal. It reveals uncertainty while still suggesting overall direction. Any individual strand might be wrong, but together they show the overall shape of the possibilities.

Intelligent interfaces can do the same. When a question has no definitive answer or a pile of adjacent answers, make it easy to access the other threads to see the big picture. This is the spaghetti scenarios functional pattern: a way to put all the strands on one plate.

p.span: Spaghetti models jumble up multiple forecast trajectories to tell an aggregate story across several forecasts.

level2.p: Google uses spaghetti scenarios to help address its reptile conundrum and other ambiguous queries. Search for “are reptiles bad pets,” and it automatically includes results for “are reptiles good pets,” too (Figure 11.9). It’s a smart application of clustering to detect adjacent questions, acting on the user’s intent and not just their keywords.

p.span: Google automatically includes the results from adjacent questions in its search results, with an option to narrow results to the original query.

level2.p: Other Google modules—like “People also search for” or “People also ask”—do similar work (Figure 11.10). These presentations cleverly challenge not only the idea of one true answer but also one true question. Each option invites the user to pursue a new strand of their topic.

p.span: Google offers ways to get at tightly related content by offering alternate queries (top) or questions, often providing both of these modules on the same page. Here, Google offers alternatives to “are reptiles bad pets?”

level2.p: JOSH’S TAKE

div.p: PROVIDE CRUCIAL CONTEXT

Remember our friends in the Colorado mud from the last chapter? They got bogged down by a flawed implementation of spaghetti scenarios. Google Maps surfaced an alternate route—a new strand—but presented only a simple binary choice: “Stay on your current route or take this new route and save 20 minutes.”

Given that information, drivers naturally chose the new route. But the presentation obscured critical context: the new route was an unpaved dirt road that had recently been soaked by rain. It was rarely traveled. It was privately maintained. All of these factors made it significantly riskier than the current route, yet none of these risks were surfaced to the drivers.
Had Google Maps revealed these complicating factors, the drivers could have made more informed judgments.

If an alternate strand has significant risks, say so. When booking flights on Delta.com, the airline visually indicates which flights are overnight red-eyes with a distinctive icon next to the flight number. It supplements that icon by calling out the next-day arrival date in green (Figure 11.11). The information helps the user make their choice with open eyes (even if they’ll be bloodshot later).

p.span: Delta surfaces lots of contextual info in its flight display to help passengers make informed choices. “Lowest fare” is most prominent, reflecting price as a likely priority for most visitors. But also, the red-eye icon calls out that it’s an overnight flight, and the layover time is clearly shown—important considerations for an informed decision.

div.p: Airlines have begun surfacing other crucial factors, too: tight connections that might leave you sprinting through terminals, routes with frequent weather delays, or planes with limited luggage space. These details appear alongside positive attributes like “lowest fare” or “fastest route,” creating a more complete picture for informed decision-making.

Adding these contextual caveats is nothing new. It’s simple UX hygiene to give people the info they need to make an informed decision. But we’ve seen designers drift from that best practice when they over-rely on machine-generated results or recommendations. Hold the line, friends! Help engage the user’s practical skepticism when you’re delivering content that could backfire.

level2.p: Some other approaches to spaghetti scenarios:
