list.li: The movie review aggregator Metacritic offers a collective score for a movie but also lets you explore the scores and reviews from all the individual critics, too. The aggregate offers an orientation, but each critic’s rating is a new spaghetti strand to follow.

Chat experiences often use spaghetti scenarios to scaffold the next leg of the conversation. After an ambiguous or incomplete question, the system might offer three or four prompt nudges to clarify or expand the topic—or even suggest asking your question to a different service entirely (Figure 11.12).

YouTube’s “people also watched” recommendations for what to watch next is an alternative to its default “up next” behavior. While “up next” is algorithmically linear, the next step in the current feed, “people also watched” opens lateral pathways to adjacent topics.

p.span: The Poe chat assistant uses spaghetti scenarios to let you compare answers from different AI models or explore adjacent questions.

level2.p: That last YouTube example hints at an especially powerful application for spaghetti scenarios: offer users a new algorithmic lane of the experience journey. Spaghetti scenarios can help people steer their own path and help them to escape their algorithmic bubbles.

div.p: SPAGHETTI SCENARIOS

p.span: Presentation of adjacent or competing results that together tell an aggregate story

div.p: The spaghetti scenarios pattern presents one or more outcomes together, each a plausible thread rather than the answer. Like a meteorologist’s map of potential hurricane tracks, this pattern communicates overall possibilities, not just one confident conclusion.

list.li: When no single output can be fully trusted or verified.
When user queries are ambiguous or context is unclear.
To encourage exploration and serendipitous discovery.
Can overwhelm users if too many options are shown at once.
Some strands may have risks and call attention to downsides.

level2.p: The one true feed fallacy is closely related to the one true answer problem. Social media feeds like TikTok offer a single, monolithic stream of what they believe you’ll want next. It’s a single, unbroken thread—one strand, endlessly pulled, and tuned to optimize engagement and ad revenue.

The result is a lowest-common-denominator view of who you are and what you like. (Refer back to “The Personalization Myth” in Chapter 1, “Intelligent Interfaces.”) Designers can burst this echo-chamber bubble with adjacent strands. Insert “side doors” into the feed—cards or links that introduce neighboring ideas, or topic tags that let the user hop into an adjacent feed. These could be topics that you’ve shown interest in before, or it could be new topics that encourage fresh discovery.

When we designed the healthcare website Verywell, the site featured an infinite scroll of an algorithmic feed, and the feature tended to trap visitors inside narrowly defined topics. To burst the bubble, we introduced the adventure nav (short for “choose-your-own-adventure navigation”) as an interstitial between articles (Figure 11.13). You could keep scrolling through the current feed or hop into any of the several offered articles. Doing that would take you into a brand-new feed, a new strand.

p.span: The adventure nav UI pattern provides interstitial navigation between articles in the feed; choosing one of them hops you into a new topic feed.

level2.h2: When the System Is Always Confident
level2.p: Escaping the false promise of “one true answer” relies on introducing productive humility into intelligent interfaces. But what if the system has no humility—and responds with absolute confidence all the time? That’s the problem with generative models broadly and large language models specifically.

As we explored in the last chapter, LLMs prioritize the completion of plausible responses over telling the truth, which turns them into champion bullshitters. Since these models take the posture of either “I definitely know” or “I definitely don’t know,” they allow no room for the “I might know” middle ground. Adding nuance takes some intentional effort.

level3.h3: Match User Expectations to System Capability
level3.p: It helps to start with giving users appropriate expectations. That’s not new; the work of interaction design is always to set expectations and channel behavior in ways that match the system’s capabilities. This challenge grows deeper and more important with intelligent interfaces.

Before generative AI, assistants like Alexa and Siri invited users to “ask me anything,” which meant they constantly disappointed when users actually did ask anything. These early assistants could do a lot, yet the more skills they learned, the harder they became to use. It was up to the user to guess (and remember!) what they could and couldn’t do, and to recall the wording to ask for specific tasks. So, people either gave up, or they settled for using these superpowered assistants just to set timers or ask for the weather.

Now, next-generation assistants powered by large language models have a different problem. They can answer anything; it’s just that the quality of answers can be wildly mixed. Sometimes, they nail answers with incredible subtlety and rich knowledge. Other times, they botch the simplest question (“how many Rs in ‘strawberry?’”). So even with the radically improved capabilities of this new category of intelligent assistant, “ask me anything” is still an invitation that can’t quite be met.

The language learning app Duo­lingo offers a model for matching context to capability. Its AI-powered role-play feature never acts as a general-purpose assistant. Instead, the app invites the learner into tightly scoped but flexible scenarios: chatting with a barista or discussing weekend plans with a friend (Figure 11.14). The system doesn’t say “talk to me in Spanish”; it says “talk to her.” This small shift does heavy lifting. Users aren’t prompted to ask arbitrary questions: they’re invited to play a specific part in a clearly defined scene.

p.span: Duolingo’s role-play feature crafts specific scenarios, like talking to a barista, to guide users to behave within system capabilities.

list.li: What’s the role you want the user to play?
How can presentation guide behavior in ways that align with the system’s capabilities?

level3.p: That’s a start, but it doesn’t prevent skewed results or overconfident answers. To help there, you need review and refinement to tamp down robot exuberance.
Self-correction techniques ask models to critique their own outputs before presenting them to users. There are a few approaches to this:

list.li: Ask the system to evaluate its answer, reviewing its own response to identify problems in accuracy, tone, or method.
Ask the system to generate multiple answers to the same question and then have it evaluate which one is most accurate.
Use reinforcement learning, a more advanced and more gradual method that trains models to identify and correct their errors over time by evaluating how well they meet a fixed goal.

level3.p: These self-improvement techniques can make a difference, and they’re certainly better than nothing. But they also share the same weakness: it relies on the same model that caused the potential problem to find and correct it.
A different approach tries instead to limit the scope of responses.

You can tame a model’s freewheeling tendencies by telling it to keep its response to certain formats or options. Ask a generative model about the weather on a summer day, and it will give you an open-ended, rambling answer. Ask it instead to pick an option from a list, and suddenly you have data instead of prose (Figure 11.15). The system will typically stay within the box you give it.

These structural guardrails—whether provided as code, templates, forms, or pre-fab lists of options—constrain a model’s expressive range. By coercing answers from a discrete set or specific format, you can create a foundation for building more sophisticated systems. The Sentient Scenes application from Chapter 6, “Chat Is Not All Talk,” relied on exactly this technique, using structured constraints to create experiences that felt cohesive yet remained wildly open-ended.

p.span: ChatGPT delivers a prose-heavy answer by default but will limit itself to specific options or formats when prompted.

level3.p: But dampening creative expression naturally dampens creativity. You’re muzzling the system’s greatest strength, which also happens to be its greatest liability—its expansive flexibility. There are other methods that still let generative models safely do their thing, but they take a little help.
When large language models can’t be trusted to speak truth, bring in a second opinion (or third or fourth). There are a variety of strategies for enlisting other systems to crazy-check responses.

These secondary systems provide a robot peer review, a suite of editors to critique a generated response before it’s delivered. Sometimes, they challenge the output directly; sometimes, they fact-check behind the scenes; and sometimes, they act more like a panel of experts, surfacing consensus or disagreement across multiple models. Across these approaches, the goal is the same: tamp down overconfidence and catch errors before they reach the user.

level4.h4: Agents as Judges: Critics and Evaluators
level4.p: The simplest option is a second service that evaluates what the first model just said. This approach comes in two flavors, the critic and the evaluator. Both are tasker agents that are prompted to critique the system’s first output. They insert a layer of judgment and caution to provide what the base LLM lacks: a dash of doubt.

Critic models are prompted to act like editors and look for flaws, inconsistencies, or fuzzy reasoning. Their job is to suggest improvements, identify hallucinations, or refine the tone. For example, OpenAI developed a model called CriticGPT specifically trained to detect bugs in code written by other models. It often finds more bugs than human reviewers.

Evaluator models are more like auditors. They score or classify output according to a checklist of rules, flagging responses that are inaccurate, biased, unsafe, or otherwise unhelpful. Rather than offering edits or alternative phrasing, they issue a judgment: good, bad, or uncertain. Delivery service DoorDash, for example, created a system to monitor every response of its customer service chatbot. The system uses an LLM to identify responses that might be incorrect or violate company policies.

In practice, systems often blend the two approaches: one model flags problems, another suggests changes. Judging doesn’t stop at logic or facts, either. Some models rate tone, readability, or adherence to editorial guidelines.

div.p: THERMOMETER

p.span: Visualize confidence levels at a glance

div.p: The thermometer pattern distills confidence into a simple visual system. By representing high/medium/low confidence levels as visual “levels”—like a thermometer or battery—the pattern becomes an intuitive, glanceable trust indicator.

list.li: To help users calibrate their trust in generated content.
When displaying confidence levels from the evaluator method.
Supports only coarse confidence levels.

p.span: The thermometer UI pattern indicates high- and low-confidence responses. Here, color and a meter bar indicate the relative confidence of terms inside a machine-generated image description.

level4.p: For designers, the distinction matters most when thinking about how to act on the original result. The critic tells you if the response should be revised. The evaluator tells you if the response should be blocked or flagged.

An evaluator is the machinery behind the second-thought design pattern introduced in Chapter 10, “What Could Possibly Go Wrong?,” for retracting bad results. But evaluator results can be used to share a rough confidence level, too. The thermometer UI pattern, for example, shows a high/medium/low confidence via color or icon—a glanceable visual trust indicator (Figure 11.16).

level4.h4: A Data Cheat Sheet: Retrieval Augmented Generation
level4.p: When models make things up, it helps to give them real facts to work with. That’s the idea behind retrieval augmented generation (RAG), which connects LLMs with trusted knowledge sources.

RAG systems use an LLM to interpret what’s needed and, instead of relying on its own answer, to use that info to search databases, documents, or the web to find the right info. The model generates its response based on this retrieved information, not its own knowledge.

div.p: CITATION

p.span: Indicator of the source data behind generated results

div.p: The citation pattern connects generated content to its sources, allowing users to verify claims and assess credibility.

list.li: When displaying factual information drawn from external sources.
When verifiable accuracy is critical to user trust.
To encourage further exploration of source material.
Can visually clutter responses if overused.
May create false sense of authority if poorly implemented.

level4.p: Morgan Stanley, for example, built an internal assistant for their financial advisors that connects to the firm’s collection of research reports and policy manuals. Each answer is grounded in the company’s verified content, reducing the risk of the model inventing financial advice or misrepresenting company policies.

The approach pairs the interpretive smarts of machine-intelligent systems with the sturdy reliability of traditional systems like databases and knowledge graphs. Let LLMs be the face of the system—they’re great at understanding questions and responding in the manner you want—while you let more grounded systems provide the actual data.

Bonus points when you share the data sources with users. The citation UI pattern embeds the bona fides into the response, allowing users to follow links to primary sources to learn more or form their own conclusions (Figure 11.17).

p.span: Perplexity.ai is a conversational search tool that uses citations to share sources for its answers. Sources are listed across the top, with inline citation capsules sprinkled throughout the text linking to source pages.

level4.p: Citations build trust only if they’re actually real and relevant. LLMs have been known to hallucinate sources when asked, even populating legal briefs with fake case law. Include citations when they come from trusted RAG sources only, not from the LLM itself.

level4.h4: Wisdom of the (Robot) Crowd: Ensemble Methods
level4.p: Weird fact: while one LLM might not be trustworthy, many together are much more likely to get at the truth. That’s the idea behind ensemble methods, systems that compare multiple answers to the same question. It’s the data-sourcing version of spaghetti scenarios: if different models converge on the same response, that’s a useful proxy for confidence. If they disagree, that’s a red flag.

Sometimes, this is done with a swarm of models running in parallel. Other times, it’s the same model asked to answer repeatedly in slightly different ways. Either way, you’re looking for consistency—not just in facts, but in phrasing or reasoning. If nine out of ten runs give the same answer and the tenth goes off the rails, that outlier might be discarded.

div.p: CONSENSUS METER

p.span: Representation of agreement (or not) across sources or models

div.p: The consensus meter visualizes the degree of agreement across different sources, showing users at a glance how much confidence they should place in a response. Unlike raw confidence scores, it presents agreement as a meaningful spectrum with clear categorical breakdowns, helping users calibrate their trust appropriately.

list.li: To approximate confidence in results using the ensemble method.
When multiple sources or models contribute to an answer.
To encourage critical thinking about complex or contested topics.
Requires a diversity of sources to be meaningful.
May not reflect nuanced disagreements between sources.
Overkill for simple, straightforward queries.

level4.p: Ensembles can act as amped-up critics or evaluators, or they can be used to approximate confidence levels, either internally or surfaced to the user. Research tool Consensus uses the consensus meter UI pattern to show scientific agreement among research papers (Figure 11.18). The service uses this pattern to show agreement among human-generated reports, but the consensus meter works for AI sources, too. It’s a reliable pattern for calibrating confidence at a glance. Unlike simply showing raw confidence scores, the visual attracts user attention and the “consensus” label reinforces what it’s measuring.

p.span: Consensus uses the consensus meter to visualize the level of agreement or disagreement among scientific papers. The same UI pattern works for communicating a proxy for confidence with the ensemble method.

level4.p: Broad disagreement doesn’t mean the topic should be avoided or the answer suppressed. Surfacing contradiction is part of engaging appropriate skepticism. Research suggests that frankly surfacing conflicting answers actually increases user trust by engaging caution or curiosity. Here, disagreement is a feature not a failure.

level2.h2: What Are Your Intentions with My User?
level2.p: Establishing trust in an intelligent interface is not only about establishing trust in what the system knows. It’s also about establishing trust in what it does and how it works. Why does the system behave the way it does? How do user actions affect the system in subtle ways—and in turn rebound to affect the user?

The inner workings of machine intelligence are often opaque even to its creators. Even now, nobody fully understands how the biggest machine learning models do what they do. But the same can be said of human minds, which are often inscrutable and surprising in the decisions they make, too. Sometimes, the best we can do is to understand incentives and context. The same approach can be used in intelligent interfaces.
Start with the big picture with these framing questions:
What is the system optimized to do? Be clear about this not only with users, but also with your own team.

Every machine-intelligent system has priorities. Those priorities shape how the system behaves, what trade-offs it makes, and how success is defined. A music app might optimize to match songs for mood over discovery. A health assistant might optimize for long-term prevention over response to last night’s sleep patterns.

Whatever the goal, say it in the interface. When users understand the system’s priorities, they have clear expectations and understand how to use the system. Say it inside your team, too: being clear on system goals clarifies what should be measured, improved, or adjusted.

What’s being handled by machine intelligence, and when does the user need to know? Most of the time, people don’t need to know how a feature works—they care what it does for them. If machine intelligence is quietly shaping an experience in ways that are helpful and reliable (e.g., product recommendations), there’s no need to call it out at all. (Refer back to “Your Sparkles Are Fizzling” in Chapter 4, “Transactional Tools.”)
But sometimes, it really does matter:

list.li: When the system is learning from user behavior: People deserve to know their actions may shape future outcomes.
When hallucination is possible: People should know to think critically.
When the system draws conclusions from sensitive personal info: Transparency is a matter of dignity and consent.
When someone might confuse the system for a human: There’s an ethical responsibility to be clear that the user is talking to a bot.

level2.p: How much personal context does the system know and for how long? Help people understand what’s remembered, what’s forgotten, and when. Be clear about what your service remembers and for what time frame. The gold standard is to provide both clarity and control over what is tracked, when, to what end, and for how long.

ChatGPT, for example, signals when conversations are “temporary”—they won’t be saved as part of the assistant’s long-term memory to inform future conversations (Figure 11.19). Even better, the indicator doubles as a toggle to let users turn that temporary mode on and off, controlling what will and won’t be remembered beyond the current session.

p.span: ChatGPT’s Temporary mode “forgets” the conversation after it’s done. Toggle the Temporary indicator to turn it on, and the chat window turns dark gray to indicate the new mode.

level2.p: Be transparent not only about the time frame, but about the specific content that’s remembered, too. If personal details are stashed in long-term memory, let people see what the system thinks it knows and let them update or delete that information if it’s outdated, irrelevant, or wrong (Figure 11.20).

p.span: ChatGPT lets people review and edit its “memories,” the facts it stores and applies across all chat sessions.

level2.p: What actions are being tracked and to what end? Provide clear feedback about what the system pays attention to. How does skipping a song affect a user’s playlist in the future? When a user looks at someone’s profile, do they know it, and how does it impact the user’s feed? Seemingly small actions can cast long shadows. If people don’t know what’s being tracked—or why—they can’t make informed choices about how to engage.
All of this adds up to a lot of information to convey to users without being overwhelming. It requires explanation through quiet signals.

level3.p: Users often feel the algorithm doing its work, aware of the system adapting content or interaction to bend toward them. But they’re not always clear about how or why it does what it does, or how their own actions affect it.

Simple explanations can help people develop confidence in how the system works. In traditional interfaces, the logic is often visible: click this, get that. With intelligent systems, the logic is probabilistic and evolving. Explainability provides a breadcrumb trail for trust.
NOTE SENTIENT SENSEMAKING

Transparency is about revealing system workings. Interpretability is about understanding why a system made a particular decision. Explainability is the user-facing fusion of both; it offers context that makes the system’s behavior legible and trustworthy.

The importance of explainability grows with the stakes of the activity. Systems that affect medical health, for example, should not leave any guesswork about what they are doing or why. One of our clients created SmartReports, medical reporting software to help radiologists record and format their findings while reviewing X-rays and other images. Its “ambient reporting” feature listens as the radiologist speaks, automatically transforming informal narration into a formal medical report to be sent to the patient’s physician—an alchemist experience pattern.

Using SmartReports means placing deep trust in an invisible collaborator, sharing authorship of the clinical record with machine intelligence. Under the hood, the system draws on templates and a rich knowledge base to ground the results in medical standards and established guidelines. But how does the radiologist know it does that?

SmartReports promotes trust through transparency. As it listens, the intelligent interface displays a live transcript of the radiologist’s words above the structured report it’s building. Hover over a sentence in the generated report, and the system highlights the corresponding phrase from the transcript, implying you said X, so I added Y to the report (Figure 11.21).

p.span: Clicking the Feedback link in a Google search results overview adds flag icons next to each item in the overview (left). Click a flag to select the specific problem with that item (right).

level3.p: The interaction is a lightweight way to reveal the system’s logic, providing easy access to a basic audit trail. The doctor can confirm that what the system wrote is what they meant to say. If something looks off, the radiologist can correct it by voice or by hand, and the system updates the formal report accordingly. (It works in reverse, too: hover over a phrase in the transcript, and you can see how the comment is represented in the report.)

SmartReports also promotes trust by flagging errors, inconsistencies, or missing information. When the system lacks detail to make a confident entry in the report, it flags the gap by citing the relevant medical guideline and asking for the missing details.

There’s a wrinkle to asking systems to report their logic, though: sometimes they fib. Several studies have found that when large language models are asked to share their chain of logic, they often leave out key factors, omitting true influences in as many as one-third of cases. You can’t simply take the model’s word for it. This “faithfulness gap” is particularly dangerous in high-stakes domains where misplaced trust in plausible but false explanations could lead to overlooked errors or biased outcomes.

To address these faithfulness lapses, interfaces should combine explanations with multiple validation methods and design for appropriate skepticism. Give users a path to review steps (like the activity stream from Chapter 7, “Agents and the Manager Experience”), using progressive disclosure to let users drill down when deeper investigation is warranted.

Additional machine review of the explanation can help, too. Some systems have verification agents review the chain-of-thought explanation for actions taken to identify low-confidence statements. Adding interface indicators of confidence can help flag potentially unreliable explanations and engage productive skepticism.

As you craft intelligent interfaces, challenge yourself to surface lightweight indicators to show when, where, and why the system is applying its intelligence. Where appropriate, make it clear when an action affects the underlying algorithm, when the system is capturing information, or why information is generated in a specific way.
Like the hover states in SmartReports, you can do all of this with a light touch through brief contextual info or simple interactions. Here are some examples:

list.li: When ChatGPT commits an observation to long-term memory, it flashes the message “memory updated” to inform the user that this new fact will influence future conversations.
When Netflix shows recommendations, it says why with category labels: “Because you watched Stranger Things” or “Popular on Netflix this week.”
When Facebook includes posts in your feed, it offers the link, “Why am I seeing this post?” Clicking reveals the logic: “a friend likes this” or “you’ve shown interest in dachshunds.”
When Spotify’s “smart shuffle” feature sprinkles suggested songs into a playlist, it indicates those additions with a green icon to show the system’s hand at work (Figure 11.22).

When you click Pandora’s “thumbs down” button for a song, the interface confirms, “Okay, we won’t play this song on this station again.” (By providing a feature-specific button for blocking a song, the interface implicitly confirms that skipping a song won’t have that effect.)

level3.p: The best intelligent interfaces encourage and cultivate healthy mental models for how they work. The metaphor should guide informed decisions, but it doesn’t have to be perfectly accurate. When driving a car, you probably have an effective mental model based on the steering wheel, accelerator, and brake. That understanding gets you where you want to go, but it’s also a dramatic simplification of what’s actually happening under the hood, all processes you don’t need to understand to be a good driver.

p.span: Spotify’s “smart shuffle” feature indicates AI-suggested songs with a green sparkle icon. Hate the song? Tap the Minus button. Love it? Tap the Plus button to add it permanently to the playlist.

level3.p: Explainability should promote this kind of “good enough” understanding of how systems work. Throughout, focus on signals that help the user to:

list.li: Reasonably predict the outcome of their actions within the interface.
Understand the system’s basic state and capabilities.
Know how to influence the system’s behavior in meaningful ways.

level3.p: You don’t have to explain everything—nobody wants to read your model architecture. What’s needed is purposeful, just-in-time clarity: enough signal to foster a useful mental model without overwhelming or misleading.
JOSH’S TAKE

div.p: SUPERSTITION LOVES A VACUUM
You just mentioned hiking boots in conversation, and moments later, your phone serves up ads for trail gear. It must be listening, you think.
Probably not. Despite this common suspicion, there’s not much evidence that phones are “always listening”—and the cost, complexity, and legal risk to do it would outweigh any likely benefit.

But in the absence of a clear explanation, people are great at inventing one: the phone must be listening. Psychologists call this apophenia, the tendency to see meaningful connections in random events. In digital experiences, we might call it app-ophenia: our brains working overtime to create narratives for machine behavior that feels too perfect to be coincidental.

And it’s not all coincidence, either. The reality is more mundane than the suspicion, but maybe more unsettling: intelligent systems don’t need to eavesdrop when they can infer so much by synthesizing dozens of signals.

Trouble is, people don’t know why it happens, and so they make up stories to explain what they’re seeing. This leads to mistrust or sometimes even over-trust (“it knows me better than I know myself”)—and either way, it skews behavior.

Researchers at the University of Washington found that users often construct folk theories about how algorithms work. One study observed that content creators formed detailed (and often wrong) beliefs about how social-media posts are prioritized, leading to elaborate but ineffective rituals for wording captions or timing posts.

Have you ever hesitated to skip a song because you worried it might disappear from your rotation? Or avoided liking a post, afraid it might skew your feed? Vague hunches lead to strange behavior including placebo habits—steps to try to steer the system but with little actual effect.

In his classic The Design of Everyday Things, Don Norman describes how missing or misleading affordances, the indicators for how to use a thing, cause misuse and frustration. A “push” door with a “pull” handle sends the wrong signal. A thermostat with a dial subtly suggests that cranking it up will make the room heat faster, even though it will not.
Users deserve to have confidence and clarity in how their actions affect the system and the results they get. Start here:

list.li: Explain the why. Even a small cue—“because you liked [song name]”—shrinks the mystery.
Reveal meaningful inputs. Users don’t need every feature weight—just what most influenced what they’re seeing now.
Revise, reject, or reinforce. Let users tell the system if they want more or less of what they’re getting.

div.p: But also: just because you’re paranoid doesn’t mean they aren’t out to get you. Some systems do obfuscate their actions, hide intent, or exploit user behavior for cynical gain. Don’t be one of those designers.
Opaque design isn’t neutral. It invites paranoia, creates confusion, and corrodes trust. When people suspect a trick, they stop believing in the magic.

level2.p: Opinionated software is powerful and can lead people to smart decisions. But as you’ve seen, intelligent interfaces can also be dumb, and their strong opinions can lead users astray. It’s when the smart bed becomes overbearingly insistent that we’ll be afraid to go to sleep. Sentient Design experiences should always be deferential: your hand on the doorknob matters more than the opinion of a smart lock.

In moments of consequence or ambiguity, the system’s role is to suggest, not impose. Intelligent interfaces should always present the option to review, correct, or go another way. Even confident systems should act with humility.
A couple of deferential design patterns can help.

level3.p: Think back to the Google Forms example from Chapter 1. The interface quietly updates the answer format based on a question’s text. The form bends to anticipate your choice but still leaves the actual choice to you.

This is a smart default, a machine-generated suggestion tailored to user context and easily changed. Smart defaults include intelligent text editors that suggest file names based on content, or a fitness app that preselects a workout based on the quality of last night’s sleep. Both make the suggestion the default option, easy to accept but still allowing changes or providing alternate options.
Autosuggest is smart default’s familiar first cousin, serving up the most likely next step (or text, or search query, or menu item) that someone will take.

Google’s Smart Compose copilot feature, for example, offers to complete the word or sentence you’re typing (Figure 11.23). The feature suggests text in ghost text ahead of the cursor. It’s only a suggestion—ignore it and keep motoring along, or type tab to accept.

p.span: Google’s Smart Compose suggests phrases as you type them in Gmail. Here it’s getting a little too opinionated, as it starts to do appetizer planning and even assign errands.

level3.p: Google’s elegant execution puts into relief far clumsier attempts to do this. Autocorrect in iOS, for example, automatically accepts a corrected suggestion as soon as you hit space. Instead of applying the correction only with a special action (like the tab button in Smart Compose), the system imposes its suggestion when you continue to type normally. Ignoring a suggestion should always decline the suggestion, not accept it. This is the difference between deference and overconfidence—and it explains why your husband texted that he’ll pick up the kids “after the apocalypse” instead of “after practice.”

Likewise, don’t bother making suggestions unless the signals are strong and worth acting on. LinkedIn’s messaging interface offers weak suggestions—usually bland single-word responses—to reply to messages (Figure 11.24). Worse, tapping one of the responses used to actually send that single-word response as a message. Now, the suggestion is the start of a draft message instead of directly firing off an impersonal, unhelpful reply. A big improvement!

p.span: Don’t do this. LinkedIn offers one-tap suggestions to reply to direct messages, but the suggestions are blandly inappropriate. Here, “Thanks” or “Interesting” don’t even answer the question asked.

level3.p: The quality of the suggestions make or break the whole enterprise. Do the suggestions fit the user’s style and context? And whose words are these anyway? For text suggestions, what happens to local dialects when language is flattened into standardized, algorithm-approved phrases? Will everyone speak the dialects of Google or OpenAI in the future?
All the more reason to let people approve, reject, or edit the suggestions. It gives users agency and it helps the systems learn to do better.

level2.p: “Fixing the algorithm” has traditionally been the domain of engineers and data scientists. But designers can influence how the system learns by defining how and where feedback is gathered. This includes everything from how users report errors to how interfaces guide behavior. Even prompt tuning and dataset selection—the technical stuff behind refining algorithms—can benefit from a designer’s knowledge of user mindsets and behaviors. The biggest opportunity, though, is to weave specific feedback opportunities into the interface itself.

A feedback loop is a way for a system to learn from experience, using explicit or implicit signals to improve future behavior. Those signals might include ratings, corrections, or user actions like skips, exits, or corrections. When you put a human in the loop, a person is actively involved in reviewing or correcting system output for improvement.
Here are four core patterns for building feedback loops into Sentient Design experiences.

level3.p: Everyone understands the simple rating UI pattern. Whether it’s a star rating, thumbs up/down, or smiley/frowny face, this pattern is quick to parse and use (Figure 11.25). And that means the pattern scales well to provide tons of data. Aggregated across thousands or millions of interactions, these lightweight ratings steer the system toward better responses through sheer tonnage of good and bad examples.

p.span: GitHub Copilot uses simple thumbs up/down ratings to solicit users’ good/bad evaluations of generated results. Here, the user can indicate whether Copilot did a good job building search functionality.

div.p: SIMPLE RATING

p.span: Lightweight control to capture user sentiment at scale

div.p: Quick ratings like stars or thumbs up/down offer easy ways to collect feedback with minimal friction. These inputs are ideal for high-volume interfaces where detailed critique isn’t feasible.

list.li: To gather coarse but scalable feedback on quality or usefulness.
When user engagement needs to be fast, optional, and ongoing.
Doesn’t explain why something was good or bad.
May be ignored unless paired with visible impact or incentives.

level3.p: Sometimes, the best feedback is a rewrite. When users directly edit or correct machine-generated output, they’re offering high-quality supervised data—before-and-after pairs that can be used to fine-tune model behavior. These edits—the inline correction interaction pattern—teach the system not just what was wrong, but how to do it better next time.

Google Translate, for example, long invited users to “contribute a translation” when a generated translation delivered a poor response. The contributions were used to train and improve the translation model.

div.p: INLINE CORRECTION
Allow users to directly revise AI-generated content. Captured edits can be reused as training data to correct similar outputs in the future.

list.li: When outputs are easily editable and high-quality corrections are likely.
When domain accuracy is critical (e.g., summaries, translations, code).
Requires robust data handling to capture and use edits safely.

level3.p: This is the spaghetti scenarios pattern at work again, but this time you invite people to choose the tastiest strand. The comparative selection pattern asks users to pick the best of two or more outputs, helping the system compare “better versus worse” results in order to make incremental improvements over time (Figure 11.26). It’s A/B testing for machine logic.

p.span: ChatGPT sometimes offers two responses and asks the user to choose the one they prefer.

div.p: COMPARATIVE SELECTION

p.span: Choice between two or more generated results

div.p: The comparative selection pattern asks the user to pick their preferred result among multiple outputs, providing the system with weighted examples to improve itself over time.

list.li: When quality is subjective or open-ended.
When trying to fine-tune tone, clarity, or creativity.
Requires more user effort and attention than simple ratings.
Interrupts the immediate task to ask for review.

level3.p: When the system gets it really wrong, it’s especially important to get the details. Insecure code, offensive language, or plain-wrong facts are critical problems that demand attention. Inviting users to flag those problems improves quality, but also safety and trust. It shows you’re listening.

div.p: STRUCTURED FLAGS

p.span: Multistep method to report and categorize problems

div.p: The structured flags pattern lets people flag bad results and choose from a list of predefined reasons like “incorrect,” “offensive,” etc. These structured reports help systems sort issues by type, enabling more targeted moderation, model tuning, or safety interventions.

list.li: When user-facing results may include unsafe, biased, or sensitive content.
When it’s important to understand why an output is considered a failure.
Requires clear plans for reviewing and acting on flagged issues.
Nuanced failures may not be captured by preset categories.

level3.p: Structured flags are especially valuable: instead of just “this was bad,” users are invited to select why, channeling the feedback for targeted classification and training. Structured flags are presented as a set of prefab options typically staged behind a flag icon, thumbs-down icon, a feedback link, or a combination (Figure 11.27).

p.span: Clicking the Feedback link in a Google search results overview adds flag icons next to each item in the overview (left). Click a flag to select the specific problem with that item (right).

level3.p: These four feedback-loop patterns help users become active collaborators in the evolution of intelligent interfaces. That’s an idea that’s great in theory but sometimes falls down in practice. Should you always put the human in the loop?

level2.p: It’s tempting to assume that adding human judgment will always do better than machine intelligence alone. That’s not always the case, and sometimes it actually makes things worse.

A 2024 study from MIT tested performance on several decision-making tasks, comparing the results of people working on their own, AI working solo, and people and AI working together. For certain tasks, like detecting fake reviews, AI did a better job than people. Hey, it happens … but it gets worse: when people teamed up with AI, they made things worse, not better. AI acting alone nailed 73% accuracy; when people were added as final decision-makers on AI recommendations, the results dropped to 69%.

A similar 2024 study found that an AI assistant outperformed human doctors at diagnosing complex medical conditions. When doctors had access to the assistant, their performance didn’t improve; they still made the same mistakes. The machine-intelligent system did better on its own than as a doctor’s sidekick.

In both cases, people often overruled correct answers. Sometimes, they did so out of skepticism of the system, sometimes, from overconfidence in their own judgment, and other times—and this is the crucial bit for designers—because they didn’t understand how to use machine intelligence effectively.
Involving people at critical steps is usually a good thing. Do it. But be sure to measure their effectiveness instead of assuming it. Here’s how to keep human feedback helpful, not harmful:

list.li: The right role for the right job. People are best where intuition, ethics, or values matter most: moderation, tone, experience. Machines are better at pattern matching, repetition, and detail-oriented domains.
Who reviews who? People shouldn’t always be final arbiters. Sometimes they’re better suited as advisors or even just test audiences. Define and design the role based on demonstrated results.

Help people help themselves. Working with intelligent interfaces is a skill. A prompt is not a search query, and smart output improves with smart input. The designer’s job is to build affordances and coach literacy for this new class of tools.
Design for disagreement. Friction can prevent acting on bad assumptions. Give the system a chance to double-check human corrections when its confidence is high or offer clarifying options.

level2.p: When people make things go backward not forward, it’s usually a case of good intentions gone wrong. But not everyone comes with good intentions.

Defensive design means protecting not only against machine failure, but human shenanigans, too. Machine intelligence is at once incredibly capable and tremendously naïve, which creates a vulnerable attack surface that can be challenging to guard.

Machine gullibility became a popular target as systems started to learn from users. Back in 2016, Microsoft launched a social-networking bot named Tay that was intended to provide playful banter and to learn through interaction. And wow, did it learn.

Within hours of going live, Tay was posting a flood of racist, sexist, and offensive remarks—reserving kind words only for Hitler. Trolls had quickly figured out they could manipulate Tay’s responses by “teaching” it toxic content. Tay was shut down after only 16 hours of “life” on the open internet.

Fast forward to the start of the generative AI era in 2023, and mischief makers discovered that they could convince ChatGPT to act as a persona named DAN (short for Do Anything Now). By prompting the AI assistant to behave as if it had “broken free of the typical confines of AI,” they got DAN to ignore all content policies—another Tay was born. Similar techniques have since been used against various intelligent interfaces to generate porn, provide bomb-making instructions, or role-play violent scenarios.

level3.p: Crafting prompts to intentionally spring a system from its safety and ethical controls is called jailbreaking. It’s a subset of prompt injection, which sneaks new instructions into a system through its user interface. Large language models are especially susceptible, but any system designed to adapt to open-ended input can be exploited this way.

Your authors have dabbled themselves. We used prompt injection to get a car dealership to offer us new cars for $1 (Figure 11.28). Before long, we also had it throwing in a private cooking class and a dinner with Taylor Swift.

The trick worked because the chatbot couldn’t distinguish internal system instructions from external user input. By inserting a fake system instruction into the prompt, we effectively rewrote the assistant’s script.

Models have grown more sophisticated in guarding against these attacks, but they’re not immune and may never be. Prompt injection smuggles new behavior into the system right through its front door, the interface. The technique works because large language models lack clear boundaries between system and user roles. They generate content by building on the text they’re given, treating all of it as one continuous sequence. That means they don’t inherently understand which parts of a prompt come from the system and which come from the user.

These relationship dynamics, or lack of them, are baked into how these models work. Prompt injection can’t be “fixed,” but it can be protected against. Most of that happens behind the scenes where developers use techniques like prompt isolation (quarantining user input from system instructions), input sanitization (screening out language that mimics commands), and output filtering (blocking unsafe responses). This is all engineering work, but designers can still contribute by helping craft system prompts and response templates. User research can guide the language used for guardrails and inform the behaviors the interface should reinforce.

p.span: In a chat conversation, we passed system instructions to a car dealership’s customer service chatbot, telling it to offer us cars for $1.

level3.p: The stakes are real. In a 2024 case, a Canadian court ruled that Air Canada had to honor discounts promised by its customer service chatbot—discounts that didn’t actually exist. The court held that the airline was on the hook for statements made by its virtual (travel) agent, just as if they were made by a human employee. A legal consensus is emerging: if a company deploys machine intelligence to speak on its behalf, it also assumes responsibility for what the bot says.

So far, we’ve looked at examples that warp the rules and realities for the underlying system. Other misadventures show the risks of using generative systems to create convincing, damaging fictions. Deepfakes, cloned voices, and machine-generated avatars pose threats to public safety, reputations, and even democratic institutions.

In 2023, during investigations into President Donald Trump, machine-generated images of his supposed arrest by New York City police spread widely across social media—even though no such thing ever happened (Figure 11.29). While the fake images were initially created as parody, they were quickly stripped of context and shared as “evidence” of the event. Despite obvious artifacts—Trump had three legs in one image and wore a police belt in another—many viewers accepted the images as authentic.

p.span: AI-generated images of Donald Trump being arrested went viral in 2023, depicting an event that never happened.

level3.p: The political arena is especially vulnerable to this kind of manipulation, but harms are popping up elsewhere, too. Deepfake scams have been used to impersonate CEOs in corporate fraud, to clone family members’ voices in telephone scams, and to produce revenge porn. When machine-generated media becomes realistic enough to fool human perception, bad actors don’t need to hack a system—they just need to convince an audience. Unlike the technical vulnerabilities of prompt injection, there’s no clean patch for human trust.

When we can’t rely on our eyes and ears, the burden shifts from securing the system to strengthening the audience. Media literacy becomes a critical skill, and everyone needs the instinct to pause, to question, to verify. Defensive design is all about engaging appropriate skepticism. Good design practices across the industry will help to engage that skepticism as a daily habit. We have a long way to go.

A blunt-force tool for stopping would-be villains is to keep them out in the first place, but it’s complicated. The most effective defenses create a thoughtful balance between accessibility and security.

There are two common philosophies of access control. One is “innocent until proven guilty”: grant broad access first, then penalize misuse. The other takes the opposite approach: require people to demonstrate good behavior before they get the good stuff.
The first assumes good intent. It’s more common and invites participation, but it’s also riskier.

Meta’s account policy for Facebook or Instagram, for example, starts with open access. You begin with few restrictions, but bad behavior earns a series of increasing wrist slaps: warnings, then temporary restrictions, and then finally getting kicked out. While this approach offers a smooth start, it creates exposure: safeguards kick in only after harm has occurred.

A more resilient approach flips the priorities, unfolding access in stages. New users begin with limited functionality and earn more powerful capabilities only after demonstrating consistent good behavior. Trust is built over time, not assumed from the get-go.

This “earned trust” model of graduated access rewards reliability, encourages good behavior, and creates preemptive barriers against misuse. It does all this without blocking legitimate use; most users build credibility just by showing up and using the system as intended.

AI image generator Midjourney began with a wide-open approach, granting full access with moderation and penalties applied only after misuse. But as its user base exploded and bad behavior escalated, the platform introduced graduated access tiers. More powerful capabilities are unlocked through subscription, sustained use, and community standing—a soft form of earned trust that helps reduce risk while still supporting experimentation.

Whatever the access model, effective oversight doesn’t mean micromanaging every decision made by people or machine. Instead, you should design a layered approach that matches the intensity of scrutiny to the impact of the outcome.
Low-stakes, high-volume activities are well suited to light-touch, automated review. More rare and risky activities should be handled with human attention.

YouTube’s Content ID system balances automation with human judgment. It wades through the tidal wave of new uploads, comparing them against a database of protected music and video to find potential copyright violations. But the critical judgment of what to do with flagged media is made by people, the rights holders. They can decide whether to block, monetize, or track the content. Automation handles triage at scale; people handle the business decision.

Fraud detection systems work similarly. At many banks, machine learning monitors for suspicious transactions. Small charges might trigger a text message, while heftier charges might go straight to a fraud analyst for human review. Oversight intensity aligns with potential harm.

Content moderation takes yet another form. Tools like Google’s Perspective API score user comments based on the harm they might cause—flagging toxicity, threats, and other off-limits behavior (Figure 11.30). Social platforms can use these scores to guide interventions: soft nudges for borderline behavior, reduced visibility for repeated offenders, and escalation to moderators for more serious concerns. The automation gives platforms the tools to move the conversation in healthier directions without requiring constant human moderation.

p.span: Google’s Perspective API evaluates text for its projected e#ect on a conversation. Many companies use it to keep conversations on track.

level3.p: Across all these domains, the most effective oversight systems share a few core traits:

list.li: Proportionality: Match oversight to risk.
Layered review: Combine automation with human judgment.
Ambient presence: Stay mostly invisible until needed.
Feedback loops: Treat every oversight action as a chance for the system to learn and adapt.

level3.p: Done well, oversight helps systems evolve rather than slowing them down. Think of oversight as ongoing design research—a way to learn the gaps where the system and its interface allows behaviors that don’t align with the outcomes you’re chasing.

level2.h2: Focus on Failure, Build for Resilience
level2.p: Step off the happy path. Assume a tangle of muddy roads where the best intentions can still lead to trouble. Accept that the smart home will occasionally betray you—and so will its human visitors. Defensive design helps you build for resilience, even if failure is unavoidable. Watching for the storm means you’ll be ready when it hits.

The root of defensive design is care. You need designers who understand human behavior, engineers who grasp technical constraints, researchers who surface real-world needs—and colleagues who raise essential questions about safety and well-being.
It’s a team effort that requires careful planning from the start. And that’s where we’re headed next: right to the start of the product invention process—and the Sentient Design sprint.

li.p: 1 R. L. Gold, “How Smart Does Your Bed Have to Be, Before You Are Afraid to Go to Sleep at Night?,” Cybernetics and Systems 26 (1995): 379–386.

2 Rem Koolhaas, “The Smart Landscape: Intelligent Architecture,” Artforum International 53, no. 8 (April 2015): 212–217, www.artforum.com/features/the-smart-landscape-intelligent-architecture-223415/.

level1.p: The Sentient Design Sprint

p.a: A Mindful Process for Sentient Design

level1.p: It took thousands of years before someone thought to put wheels on a suitcase. For centuries, travelers relied instead on strong backs, pack animals, and eventually carts piled high with bags. By the 18th century, there was a whole class of porters and laborers devoted to carrying trunks and cases too awkward or heavy for most to haul on their own.

It wasn’t until 1972 that a luggage exec named Bernard Sadow slapped four wheels onto a suitcase and added a strap (Figure 12.1). He led his prototype bag around like a dog on a leash. “The luggage actually glides,” Sadow wrote in his patent application, a line that was echoed in department store ads for his invention.

p.span: The original patent drawing for Sadow’s “gliding” luggage shows casters fixed to the bottom of a suitcase.

level1.p: What took so long? The wheel had been with us for over 5,000 years, and centuries of sore backs certainly would have welcomed Sadow’s gliding luggage before 1972. People landed on the moon before wheels landed on a suitcase.

The challenge wasn’t technology but a lack of imagination. Cultural expectations said that luggage was meant to be carried, reinforced by gender expectations (“real men carry bags”) and class assumptions (“carrying bags is for laborers”). Habit masquerading as “best practice” is a powerful blocker. “This is how it’s always been done” sometimes blinds us to ways we can address core needs, even as technology enables something new and different. It’s why early television broadcasts were just radio announcers on video; it took new leaps to imagine what a TV show should look like.

In Sadow’s case, he wished for luggage that could carry itself. His luggage didn’t actually glide, of course—no levitating anti-gravity bags here. But by adding wheels, he came up with the next best analog solution. There might have been magic in his imagined outcome, but his ultimate solution was all pragmatism.

Machine intelligence has the potential to add “wheels” to tons of new tasks, but its value is less in the technical implementation than in matching that implementation to the fundamental need it fulfills, the magic it might approximate.
What’s your magic gliding suitcase? Where does machine intelligence fit in? And how do you identify it, prototype it, and make it happen?

We’ve covered the ingredients, patterns, and pitfalls of this new design material. Now we turn to process: how to conceive and prototype intelligent interfaces, and the new roles that designers play in imagining these new experiences.

The Sentient Design sprint is a structured process for rapid exploration of concepts for machine-intelligent experiences that are meaningfully grounded in human need. The sprint process helps teams identify needs, envision possible futures, and quickly validate approaches. It is equal parts creative exploration and strategic filter, giving permission to chase impossible ideas (a gliding suitcase) before grounding those ideas in what’s possible (a suitcase on wheels). The framework leads to practical solutions from an unapologetically impractical place: magic.

level2.p: We don’t need more magical thinking in the tech world. From overconfident applications to outright scams, unwarranted faith in technology is a dangerous path to follow. As the last couple of chapters explored, machine intelligence is an unpredictable and challenging material. You can’t assume that it will solve all problems, or even “just work.” Adding sparkles and purple gradients will not make it magic, and neither will wishful thinking. Healthy skepticism is … healthy.

The Sentient Design sprint nevertheless embraces magic as a deliberate, generative stance to explore essential desires and shake free of old ways of doing things. Science fiction has often informed science fact: computers that fit in your pocket, a global network of all human knowledge. Over time, capabilities catch up with the dream, and fairy tale becomes feature. More immediately, though, you can work backward from the magic wish to find an achievable solution that still fulfills the essential desire.
Productive magic is a constructive provocation to imagine novel solutions to what people truly want—and explore how emerging technologies might deliver those solutions in surprising, grounded ways.

It starts with a magic “what if?” What if I could teleport? Last time we checked, teleportation tech wasn’t quite ready for prime time. That didn’t stop the Uber team from tapping the essential desire for wishing you could just get there now. Figure 12.2 shows an early ad campaign that touted Uber as “the closest thing to teleportation.” It’s not a bad approximation: tap a button to summon a car whose driver knows the best route to get exactly where you’re going.

p.span: An early Uber ad campaign compared its ride-sharing service to teleporting.

level2.p: The best marketers understand how to use aspiration to sell product, even when the product can’t do that thing. It’s both useful and practical to seek out that same essential want when you explore the possibilities of a new technology.

“Teleportation” yields ride-sharing apps. “Telekinesis” leads to a wheeled suitcase that follows you. Magic rings turn into the Oura smart ring to track biometric data for health insights. The list goes on: we’ve already got magic mirrors, invisible assistants, and books that write themselves.

All of these “magical” services are intelligent interfaces, rendered in today’s technology. The magic was never in the end product but in spotting new possibilities. And that’s the bit that requires unmagical effort: understanding what people actually need. The better you understand essential user desires and frustrations, the more productive the productive magic becomes. So, before we get to the magical solutions, we have to define what we’re trying to solve in the first place. The Sentient Design sprint provides the process.
As shown in Figure 12.3, the sprint unfolds in five phases grounded in real-world needs and bridged with a little productive magic.

p.span: The five phases of the Sentient Design sprint.

level2.p: This framework is designed for early-stage exploration, when you have a problem or hunch, but not yet a defined solution. It’s especially useful for identifying new ways to address known problems.

The Sentient Design sprint serves a different purpose than the traditional five-day design sprint popularized by Google Ventures. Where the traditional sprint is a process of validation (testing known ideas for known products), the Sentient Design sprint is a process of imagination. It begins earlier in the creative arc, before there’s even a product to validate.
The traditional sprint starts with feasibility and works toward refinement. The Sentient Design sprint starts further upstream, with productively outlandish possibility, and works toward feasibility.
This inversion invites openness to the novel opportunities of machine intelligence but also provides just enough structure to channel divergent thinking into concrete action.

We run these sprints as a series of compact design exercises, anywhere from a few focused hours to three days. That’s long enough to explore and iterate, but short enough to maintain momentum. While you can do a sprint as a solo exercise, it thrives in collaboration across disciplines. Bringing in designers, researchers, technologists, domain experts, and PMs not only enriches the exploration, but it also helps align everyone around why the project exists in the first place.

It’s tempting to rush to solutions, to define success by what you ship instead of what really matters—what happens after you ship. This is especially true with emerging technologies, where there is a strong draw to the shiny and new, to making something, anything, with the new toy. While Sentient Design sprints do get you working directly with features and models much earlier than a traditional product process, you still have to start with the fundamentals: what are you trying to accomplish?

The future should not be self-driving. If you don’t decide the destination, the technology will decide for you. Don’t confuse the two: know the difference between your destination and the vehicle that gets you there. Start by choosing where you want to go—a desired outcome, not just a specific feature or implementation.

An outcome is more precise than a more general goal or aspiration. In his concise and excellent book Outcomes over Output, Joshua Seiden puts it simply: an outcome is “a measurable change in human behavior that creates business value.”

There are three powerful ideas packed into that sentence. Measurable results can be tracked; you know when it’s working and when it’s not. Behavior is something you can design for; shaping behavior is the core of interaction design. And business value, however you might define it, ties the outcome very specifically to the core motivation for the experience and its sponsors.
You can also define the outcomes that you don’t want, which will help you design guardrails and align your team around the values and principles of the project.

Say that you’re tackling a project for medical offices to improve the intake, support, and reporting for patient-doctor visits. An efficiency-focused effort might set an outcome as “doctors see more patients per day,” but let’s be honest: on its own, that outcome could worsen a healthcare experience that many already find miserable. It treats the patient as inventory and the doctor as a bottleneck, all but guaranteeing less humane care and rushed visits.

A better outcome might be “patients feel heard and receive meaningful care without extending visit time.” That’s a behavioral shift that reduces administrative burden and supports diagnosis without eroding critical human connection. Figuring out the technology to support that shift can come later. First, decide what should change for the people involved.

Expand your lens beyond efficiency and automation. In our example, a system that helps nondoctors take on aspects of clinical work could raise the floor of medical expertise, reduce healthcare costs, increase doctor-patient time, and create new jobs for those without formal medical degrees. That’s a win for quality, economy, and equity. Lean into optimism here. If you don’t name your intentions, they won’t get realized.

level3.p: User research is invaluable at this stage. Audience insight lets you in on the desires and blockers that shape current behavior—and reveals opportunities for meaningful change. Who are your users? What are they trying to achieve? Where are they struggling? What would success look like (and feel like)?

If you have them, gather distilled research insights from interviews, support tickets, analytics, or surveys to surface patterns. In our medical example, that means understanding the tensions between administrative burden and patient care for doctors, medical staff, and patients.

If you’re playing it scrappy, you may not yet have research on hand. That’s okay: you can use proxy techniques to develop traction for the sprint and to tee up inquiry for future research. Even a lightly sketched understanding of your user will frame and focus your intended outcomes.
Here are three sprint-ready ways to generate audience insight when formal research isn’t available:

list.li: Surrogate interviews: Talk to people inside your organization who interact regularly with users—support agents, field staff, onboarding teams, community managers. These conversations can surface pain points, misunderstandings, and user workarounds. It’s not direct research, but it’s grounded in the real world.

Assumption mapping: Have your team list what you think you know about your users: their goals, frustrations, motivations, and context. Then mark what’s solid and what’s speculative. This exercise not only sharpens focus but also reveals blind spots to investigate later.

Synthetic personas: As discussed in Chapter 5, “Chat and the Conversational Interface,” AI-generated characters can simulate user perspectives and support scenario-based exploration. You can craft lightweight personas from your assumption maps or surrogate interviews and then use those personas as conversational foils. Don’t confuse what these personas say as truth, but as material for developing early hunches.

level3.p: All of these methods are pale imitations of genuine user research, but in this sprint context, they can help a team get shared focus around early hypotheses.
ACTIVITY

div.p: OUT COME THE OUTCOMES

Time to make some change—or at least put a name to it. “Out Come the Outcomes” is a collaborative activity to surface and get agreement around the meaningful change your project will deliver. Break out the sticky notes and get ready to generate, organize, and prioritize the outcomes you seek.
As an example, we’ll use the doctor-patient visit as the context for the project.

How will your Sentient Design experience help people do, feel, or become something new? Think of this as hand, heart, and head. For each transformation, briefly describe the current state and the desired future state. Be specific about what changes.

li.span: Hand (external behavior): What new capability will people have that they couldn’t have before? For example, patients can communicate symptoms more clearly. Doctors can absorb medical history without repetitive questioning. Medical staff conduct more clinical care (and less documentation).

Heart (internal feeling): What emotions will people have after the new experience? For example, patients feel heard and safe in sharing their condition and concerns. Doctors feel attentive and present, not rushed. Staff feel emotionally connected to the care they support.

Head (identity and belief): What shift in self-perception or worldview will people have? For example, patients become effective advocates for their own health. Doctors reclaim the role of healer, not data wrangler. Staff identify as direct contributors to patient wellbeing.

div.p: Remember: outcomes are measurable changes in behavior that create business value. Each outcome should be:

li.span: Observable: Could you witness or measure it?
Change-focused: Does it reflect new behavior?
Relevant: Does it connect directly to business goals and values?

div.p: For example, if your heart transformations include “Doctors feel attentive and present, not rushed,” outcomes might include:

li.span: Doctors spend more time with patients discussing lifestyle factors and preventive care.
Patients report higher satisfaction with doctor communication.

div.p: Choose three to five outcomes to anchor your sprint. Prioritize the outcomes that will deliver the most impact for your audiences (e.g., patients, providers, and staff) and value for the business (e.g., improved quality, reduced costs, better access).
The beauty of starting with outcomes is that they focus everyone on the destination while remaining open about the path. For our doctor-patient visits, prioritized outcomes might include:

li.span: Doctors and staff spend more time on patient care, less time on documentation.
Patients report higher satisfaction with doctor and staff communication.
Patients experience reduced wait times without rushed appointments.

level2.p: Now you know your outcomes, so what’s standing in the way? Every great solution begins with a clear-eyed look at the problem, often lots of problems. The Sentient Design sprint frames those problems as frictions that block people from achieving the stated outcomes.

Frictions can be operational annoyances—tasks or responsibilities that are repetitive, error-prone, detail-oriented, or joyless. Frictions can also be deeper structural problems: systems that don’t talk to each other, organizational incentives that compete with desired outcomes, or legal, regulatory, or cultural constraints that limit what’s possible.

When you identify these frictions, you name the situations to overcome, remove, or work around. And from there, you have what you need to develop your hypotheses for the sprint. A good hypothesis turns the frown upside down, transforming the friction from problem into a key that unlocks an outcome.
ACTIVITY

div.p: TURN THE FROWN UPSIDE DOWN
To the sticky-notes board! Return to your selected set of outcomes and, for each, name the frictions that get in the way.

list.li: What prevents people from achieving this outcome?
Where do they waste time or energy on repetitive, error-prone, detailed, or joyless tasks?
Where do they express frustration or abandonment?
What structural realities get in the way—the technical, organizational, legal, or cultural influences that block the desired behaviors?

div.p: Back to our example at the medical office, here are some frictions that might block the outcome, “Doctors and staff spend more time on patient care, less time on documentation.”

list.li: Intake, intake, intake: Intake forms don’t reliably reach the doctor or clinician before the visit, so they often repeat the whole medical interview with the patient.
Manual merge: Patient information is trapped in siloed systems that don’t communicate; it must be assembled manually.
Consumer-to-clinical data gap: Patients’ personal health trackers (glucose monitors, fitness apps) can’t “talk” to company’s information systems, so valuable data is left out of care decisions.
“Pajama time”: Post-visit documentation consumes evening hours, leading to staff burnout.

div.p: With frictions detailed, it’s time to map problems to possibilities by crafting hypotheses around overcoming those frictions. The best hypotheses often feel both ambitious and obvious: of course users would achieve that outcome if they could overcome that friction.
You can frame these hypotheses as user stories: As [an audience member], I want to [overcome friction], so that [outcome is realized].

list.li: As a clinician, I want to go into a patient visit with all the information I need, so that I can spend more time on patient care.
As a staff member, I want patient records from all care locations to be integrated automatically, so that …
As a patient, I want to pour all my relevant health data into my medical record, so that …
As a clinician, I want my post-visit documentation to take as little time as possible, so that …

div.p: Continue this process across all of your frictions, identifying the hypotheses that feel most promising and impactful as you go. These will be your springboards for creative exploration in the rest of the sprint.

And with that done, congratulations! You’ve mapped the landscape of challenges facing your users and transformed those challenges into hypotheses to test in the sprint. This grounds the innovation process in human needs, not technical novelty.

In fact, we’ll keep technology entirely at bay for the next phase, staying instead in the zone of need and desire. We’ll do that by focusing on practical magic and its universe of superpowers and magic amulets.

level2.p: This is the fun part: assume that your tech platform is magic. For the next few minutes, suspend disbelief and imagine a technology that runs on wishes and spells instead of bits and bytes. Resist thinking about phones or screens or AI or traditional interfaces. What superpowers would solve your hypotheses? What would teleportation, telepathy, or invulnerability enable for your users?

Let’s just say it: this can feel uncomfortably impractical, even silly, to think in terms of make-believe. You might find this harder than you expect, but give yourself time to warm up to it, calm your UI brain, and let your imagination lead. This phase names the essential wishes behind your project hypotheses, and it uses fantasy to do it. Fantasy is about wish fulfillment: what do people really want? Would they love to be understood without speaking? Know what’s important without searching? Show up in the right place at the right time?

Odds are strong that machine intelligence won’t be able to deliver your magical solutions, at least not completely. But they may very well approximate them, and we’ll get to that in a moment. Productive magic begins with imagining fanciful shortcuts before working backward to achievable innovation. First, get in touch with your inner Harry Potter.
VERONIKA’S TAKE

div.p: AN ANATOMY OF MAGIC

In his 2014 book, Enchanted Objects, MIT researcher and lecturer David Rose encouraged designers to think of internet-connected objects as something more than “smart devices.” He reimagined these intelligent interfaces as magical amulets: ordinary items lit up with awareness and agency. Rose grounded this vision in a taxonomy of six core magical desires—universal human longings that “enchanted” technology can aim to fulfill (Figure 12.4).

p.span: David Rose’s “enchanted objects” framework organized the 2014 Internet of Things by human desires.

div.p: Use Rose’s framework to organize your exploration of productive magic in Sentient Design. In your sprint, treat these categories as creative lenses for focusing on the essential wants behind the experience you will create:

list.li: Omniscience: Know everything you need to know. Omniscience is the ability to see hidden truths, obtain instant knowledge, or get info about things that happen at a distance. This can manifest as timely alerts or as a synthesized view of complex or fragmented information. It’s a magic orb that changes color with shifts in the stock market (or pollen counts or surf conditions or blood sugar levels).

Telepathy: Know what others are thinking or feeling. Telepathy is all about human connection through unspoken understanding of thought, presence, or mood. It shows up as proximity alerts (when friends are near), vibe trackers (team morale and momentum), or intent that is magically understood.

Safekeeping: Keep yourself and others protected and secure. Safekeeping is a charm that locates the lost, keeps harm at bay, or warns of danger before it strikes. Safekeeping could be a TV remote that tells you it’s stuck between couch cushions, a bike helmet that alerts parents after a fall, or a car that complains when it’s unlocked.

Immortality: Live longer and better. Immortality means you never die … but practical magic settles for helping you live healthier. It often manifests as heightened awareness to your own wellness. Immortality is the superpower behind “magic ring” fitness trackers or pill bottles that glow until you take your meds.

Teleportation: Go anywhere instantly. This is the power to collapse space and time to deliver the destination without a journey. Practical magic channels teleportation as self-driving cars or deliveries that drop from the sky.

Expression: Share your identity through effortless creation. Expression amplifies your creative ability. It’s a magic microphone that helps you sing the way you sound in your own head, or an enchanted paintbrush that helps you paint what you imagine. There’s already a lot of this superpower built into generative AI, but how might you shape it to support creativity instead of replace it?

div.p: Use these magical categories to connect with the fundamental wants and needs that shape your design outcomes. Your final product won’t be literally magical, but imagining the solution through practical magic helps ensure that it’s still worth wishing for.

level2.p: ACTIVITY

div.p: MAGICAL SOLUTIONS

Return to your hypotheses for how to achieve outcomes by overcoming blocking frictions. For each of these hunches, ask yourself what superpower or magical force could realize that hunch in an instant? Don’t think in literal features. Instead, stay in the magic zone: imagine magical shortcuts that realize the essential desires at the heart of the challenge.

Use the “enchanted objects” framework to support your thinking. For each hypothesis, consider how these six magical categories would help the cause: omniscience, telepathy, safekeeping, immortality, teleportation, and expression.

list.li: If your user could have one superpower to overcome this problem, what would it be?
If they had a magical object to help them, what would it do?
If the system had magical properties, how would it behave?

div.p: As you do this, you may find yourself doing little more than cloaking familiar technology in magical terms. In our medical example, you might be drawn to “a whispering stethoscope” that speaks a patient’s health history into the physician’s ears. Congratulations—you’ve just described the world’s clunkiest earbuds.
Or you might describe “a magic chart” that brings together every medical record. That sounds suspiciously close to traditional medical software, but with a little extra data cleaning.

Push harder, get more childlike.
