If your magic sounds like a feature roadmap (“an assistant that transcribes visits”) or a series of API calls (“all the systems can talk to each other”) or something that already exists (“patients and doctors can videoconference”), then that’s not real magic. It shouldn’t be something you can just build next quarter.

Instead, lean into the impossible. Real magic warps the physical world to allow fantastic leaps. What if healing were contagious? What if knowledge and wisdom could be communicated without words? What if empathy flowed from faucets? Try to keep your brain in kid mode: Kids don’t ask for “better data integration”; they ask for results without effort, or medicine that tastes like a hot fudge sundae. That’s what we’re going for here: magic shortcuts that get to the core of the essential want. It’s harder than it sounds but stick with it.

level3.p: Here’s how productive magic might apply to our healthcare hypotheses for focusing time on care instead of documentation.

p.em: “As a clinician, I want to go into a patient visit with all the information I need.”

level3.p: Magic fix: a lens of historical healing. This omniscient artifact lets the practitioner read the patient’s body like an archaeologist reads a historic site, peering through the layered sediment of health experiences to reveal not only what is but what was. Essential desire: immediately understand the problem and how it got that way.

p.em: “As a clinician, I want my post-visit documentation to take as little time as possible.”

level3.p: Magic fix: a “stopwatch” that stops or steals back time. Every minute spent on documentation magically returns to your day. The moment the patient visit ends, complete documentation appears instantly, as if no time passed at all. Essential desire: time no longer matters, and attention is abundant.

p.em: “As a staff member, I want patient records from all care locations to be integrated automatically.”

level3.p: Magic fix: a “constellation of care” crystal. When held near a patient, the crystal taps omniscience and telepathy to project a constellation of their healthcare activity—each star is a touchpoint of insight and emotional resonance for every visit in their health journey. Essential desire: connections are made clear, and emotions are understood.

These probably aren’t the typical features you find in your team’s backlog. And yet all are intelligent interfaces that amplify healthcare knowledge and agency. The trick now is to transform the magical properties of these enchanted objects into real-world capability—or the best approximation of them.

level2.p: The magical powers and enchanted amulets you’ve dreamt up have surfaced the essential desires implied by your hypotheses. These are the tech-agnostic capabilities that will unlock your stated outcomes. How can Sentient Design make those impossible desires real, or at least move your users in that direction?

Machine intelligence has quite a few magic powers of its own. As you’ve seen, applying those capabilities through different postures can manifest a wide range of user experiences. In this phase of the Sentient Design sprint, the goal is to identify the experiences that can deliver the user superpower we’re after.
Start by thinking about core technical capabilities of machine intelligence, your design material. Or better, what it lets you accomplish.
There are six broad goals that machine intelligence can help you unlock. As you consider the desires your practical magic revealed, think about which of these goals those desires touch:

list.li: Get better answers to questions you already ask. Use machine learning to find once-elusive insights: related documents, similar patterns, or relevant experts.

Ask new kinds of questions. Move beyond content-based queries to surface insights based on new dimensions like urgency, emotion, or style. Machine intelligence can see dimensions of content that were once impenetrable to traditional systems.

Unlock new data sources. Tap into any of the wide-ranging, messy ways that humans communicate: speech, handwriting, video, photos, the whole gamut. Systems can understand media and “speak” in channels that were opaque to most systems until only recently.
Reveal “invisible” patterns. Uncover connections buried in vast datasets or across domains that would otherwise remain hidden.
Understand intent and context. Interpret open-ended language or behavior patterns and translate them into crisp distillations of what the user is trying to do.

Create content or experience. Generate prose, media, and code in order to manifest user-requested artifacts or—the big focus of Sentient Design—to shape the environment and experience for a truly intelligent interface.

level2.p: Ask yourself how these capabilities align with your hypotheses and with the magic powers you want to give the user? For example, the “lens of historical healing” shares the capabilities of items 1, 4, and 5 above: smarter retrieval of past health data, detection of hidden patterns in that data, and bottom-line understanding of the context that emerges.

Look for the slices of activity where people tend to flail or for tasks they tend to avoid. Identify jobs that are time-consuming, repetitive, detail-oriented, error-prone, and joyless. Read that list again; none of those chores will get you out of bed in the morning, but machine intelligence eats them for breakfast.
Let people and machines each do what they do best; they are rarely the same things.

When you hold your own examples of practical magic up to the light of these aspirational goals and friction-filled adjectives, you’ll identify not only where machine intelligence can help, but the specific types of models that can help you do the job.
In Chapter 2, “AI as Design Material,” we reviewed the five categories of machine learning models. As you can see from Table 12.1, many tasks work best with a coordination of multiple systems.
TABLE 12.1 MACHINE LEARNING GOALS AND MODELS

p.span: Get smarter answers to existing questions.

level3.p: After reviewing the goals that machine intelligence can unlock, return to your practical magic concepts and start considering what their analog substitutes could be. Consider the essential desire that each one represents and think about how an intelligent interface could satisfy that desire, even in part.
VERONIKA’S TAKE

div.p: BUSYWORK ISN’T ALL BAD

It’s tempting to try to automate away all the “dumb” tasks … but hold on. If people pass off all of their lightweight, low-judgment tasks to AI, that means everyone will be saddled only with heavy, high-cognition tasks. Do we really want that? Can we even handle that?
It makes good sense to put people in the loop for difficult, high-stakes, or creative problems. But that doesn’t mean people should do those tasks exclusively.

Rote busywork gets a bad rap. This may be counterintuitive but glazed-eye tasks can be restorative in the thick of heavy brainwork. It provides a break that lets you return to more intensive tasks with fresh eyes and enthusiasm. A pause to wash the dishes can yield a creative breakthrough.

As you give machine intelligence more work, be careful to consider what remains. What is the shape of the user’s work life after this intervention? How have you skewed the burden of their workday? How might you allow the option to do “dumb” work in order to make room for “smart” effort?
Just because you can automate away tasks doesn’t mean you should. Keep your eye on the big picture—and the well-being of the people whose lives you affect.

level3.p: In our medical clinic example, we imagined a magic stopwatch that could stop or steal back time to allow practitioners to spend no time on documentation, letting them focus on patient care instead. The real magic here is removing time as a barrier to care, allowing limitless attention. How can machine intelligence help?

We can’t stop time, but perhaps we can protect attention by reducing practitioner busywork or reduce time pressure by making schedules flex to the realities of patient needs. With this shift in perspective, ideas begin to emerge for actions to support those goals:

list.li: Summarize and deliver a 90-second pre-visit briefing so practi­tioners can have more time with the patient instead of catching up.
Build buffer time into the schedule when complex cases are detected and longer visits predicted.
Listen in to generate real-time documentation to preserve the practitioner’s full attention for the patient, giving that reporting time back to the clinician and their patients.

level3.p: With that last idea—it’s called ambient reporting in the industry—we’re starting to get somewhere. It’s the closest real-world analog to collapsing time. It doesn’t quite stop the clock, but it steals time back from the never-ending grind of clinical documentation. More than that, it preserves the practitioner’s most precious resource: focused attention. By offloading notetaking and form-filling, the approach lets care providers stay fully present in the moment that matters.

As you land on “aha” moments and opportunities in your sprint, take forward the ideas that seem to have the most promise or provide the most creative energy for meeting your outcomes. Let’s do that with our magic stopwatch and ambient reporting.
Look back at the six goals machine intelligence excels at, and you’ll see that ambient reporting hits three of them:

list.li: Unlock new data sources. Gather real-time information from audio and (in appropriate contexts) video for an ambient, multimodal experience.
Understand intent and context. Marry the live exchange with patient history, along with knowledge of standard medical protocols.
Create content. Generate a well-structured report, including symptoms, patient history, exam details, ordered tests or prescriptions, and so on.

level3.p: Taken together, those capabilities lean on classification and generative AI models, married with a knowledge base of patient history and medical guidelines. Instinctively, this already feels like a good match for machine intelligence. But what does it look like?

As you start to imagine how a feature might take shape in actual working software, go beyond the six goals we identified previously. Think back to Chapter 2 and its review of the different applications of machine intelligence for providing both input and output from the system. Table 12.2 offers a list of greatest hits to get you thinking: what applications feed the activities and outcomes you want to support?
Complete solutions almost always involve more than one of these applications. Consider the ambient reporting example. It could put any of these applications to use:

list.li: Speech recognition and processing to capture the conversation between provider and patient, and post-visit notes from the provider.
Cross-system communication to pull in patient history, medical protocols, drug databases, and so on.
Intent detection to understand what’s being discussed (symptoms vs. diagnosis vs. treatment plan).
Synthesis and summarization to distill the conversation into structured documentation.
Classification and categorization to organize information into proper medical report sections.
Content generation to create the structured report.

level3.p: TABLE 12.2 APPLICATIONS OF MACHINE INTELLIGENCE

td.p: Content generation (text, image, speech, audio, code)
Format translation (PDF to podcast, sketch to UI, prose to JSON, etc.)

level3.p: And just like that, we see that machine intelligence applications provide all the technical pieces to provide ambient reporting. In fact, this bit of analog magic is already emerging as a real-world feature. In Chapter 11, “Defensive Design,” we described how one of our clients created SmartReports, an ambient assistant that listens in on radiology dictation to create structured reports. More generally across the industry, hospitals report that ambient reporting is saving clinicians over two hours per day and leading to 20–60 percent reductions in physician burnout. Patients report that practitioners are more engaged and empathetic and that they feel more connected and respected during visits.
Those are remarkable human-centered outcomes by automating work that humans are bad at—those same time-consuming, repetitive, detail-oriented, error-prone, and joyless chores we just called out.
Now that you’ve mapped magical desires to plausibly practical goals and applications, you can start to craft the experience, starting with the right posture from the Sentient Triangle.

Fetch your trusty map of the Sentient Triangle (see Chapter 3, “The Sentient Triangle”). We’ve spent considerable time traversing that terrain, and at last you can apply the triangle to your own feature. The right posture depends on the context of your solution.

list.li: Will the user stay locked in on managing the task (tools and chat) or drift while the system works (agents and copilots)?
Does the job have a discrete, defined endpoint (tools), an ebb and flow of exploration (chat and copilots), or a long-running series of tasks (agents)?
Do the stakes demand constant oversight and control (tools and chat), or can decisions be delegated (agents and copilots)?
Will the experience benefit most from grounded reliability (tools), radically adaptive flexibility (chat), interoperable coordination (agents), or a blend of all (copilots)?

level3.p: Explore the Sentient Triangle to consider the specific experience patterns for your selected posture. Consider adjacent patterns, too, as you try on different experiences for the task and outcome to support. How do experience patterns that overlap two or more postures change how you might approach the task at hand?

For our ambient reporting example, the clinician needs to be fully present with the patient, not managing the system; this points away from a tool or chat toward an agent or copilot experience. The documentation and reporting takes place continuously throughout the visit, not as a single transaction, which feels agentic. The report requires high accuracy in the result; the provider needs to review and correct the report but doesn’t require constant oversight while it’s getting built. Finally, the rigor of the medical environments demands a blend of grounding, radical adaptability, and interoperability. On balance, we’re looking at a copilot with heavy agentic tendencies.
ACTIVITY

div.p: GET REAL
Now you try: transform your magical solutions into practical experiences. Work through each of your magical concepts using this five-step process:

list.li: Connect to goals and capabilities.
Explore applications.
Pick your posture.
What could go wrong?
Build your brief.

div.p: In the Magical Solutions activity, you named the desires that your magic fixes reveal. Put aside the mystical forms wrapped around those desires and focus on those essential user wishes. For each one, consider how or if each of the six capability goals of machine intelligence might support that desire: better answers, new questions, new data sources, hidden patterns revealed, intent and context made clear, and content creation.

Recast the magic to fit these attainable goals. Like we saw with the time-stopping stopwatch, that shift might mean pivoting to a new but related path (protecting attention instead of stopping time). Instead of a lens that can see through time, maybe we only need a data visualization that highlights the patient’s historical patterns when examining current symptoms.

list.li: What user goals map neatly to the capability goals of machine intelligence?
What feature does that suggest?

div.p: Browse Table 12.2, “Applications of Machine Intelligence,” to find the real-world superpowers that feed the goals and features you identified in the first step. Mix and match capabilities for inputs, sensemaking, and outputs to find combinations that are suited to the context.

Consider which of the four Sentient Design postures are best suited to the task, the user’s mindset, and their physical and temporal context. Is this experience best suited to tool, chat, agent, or copilot—or perhaps it straddles more than one? Place your solution on the Sentient Triangle to identify the specific experience patterns to explore.

Challenge yourself here. Initial concepts are often function-first—dedicated tools that privilege transactional results over experience. Explore beyond simple tool experiences to test opportunities for something more radical.

list.li: How would this interaction model change the experience?
How would this style of system collaboration shift attention or effort?
How would this experience pattern marry with the applications you’ve discovered?
How would this direction yield the desired outcome or prove the hypothesis?

div.p: In Chapter 10, “What Could Possibly Go Wrong?,” we explored the many ways Sentient Design can go glitchy. Run a quick vulnerability check against each of your feature concepts for these common vectors of AI gone bad:

list.li: Wrong context: What could happen if the system misunderstands the situation or optimizes for the wrong thing?
Bad content: Where could hallucinations, bias, or flawed data cause harm?
Broken formats: What might go wrong if systems fail to communicate properly?
Bad actors: How could someone misuse or manipulate this feature?
What could go wrong, and who could get hurt?
What dependencies will your service create?
What social dynamics might get disturbed and to what effect?

div.p: Use the “value vs. risk tolerance” grid from the last chapter (Figure 11.1) to visualize tasks that can safely benefit (or not) from machine intelligence. These tasks might be subsets of the overall feature or application that you’ve imagined. This is a good moment to think through the specific elements or actions that your feature will support. Sketch out the chart and place your proposed solutions.

For example, Figure 12.5 shows the chart for the ambient reporting concept, introducing several specific features and functions it might support. The good news is that most of the features are high value, although some are highly sensitive to errors, with very low risk tolerance.

span.span: In this “value vs. risk tolerance” grid for ambient reporting, some features are safe to automate with clear value (top right), while others demand human oversight or careful framing (top left). At the bottom of the chart, some ideas offer little value even if they feel low risk (bottom right). Here, real-time exam alerts may be more distracting than helpful, even if technically low risk.

div.p: In the top-left quadrant, clinical decision suggestions and generated medical reports are highly valuable but could do serious damage if errors slip through. Risk doesn’t mean these features shouldn’t be pursued, but it does suggest that some serious design work is required to reduce risk. How and when should the provider review the results? How do you make that review easy and the generated results explainable?

Meanwhile, in the top-right quadrant, we have high-value features that feel fairly safe; even if mistakes are made, they are recoverable. “Gap identification & protocol suggestion,” for example, would provide reminders to the clinician to ask questions or follow protocols when the evidence suggests specific next steps. Because these are suggestions, not impositions, they are low risk but provide strong value by jogging the memory of a busy clinician.
NOTE MAGIC ALWAYS COMES AT A COST

The ring from Lord of the Rings corrupts its wearer. Asking the genie to “make me a sandwich” will certainly turn you into a sandwich. Our oldest mythologies show a deep distrust of power without effort, of taking bold leaps without understanding the very thing we’re leaping.

Sound familiar? These same concerns echo common anxieties about what we might lose, who could be harmed, or the unseen costs of machine intelligence. As you design your own “magic,” consider what might go wrong with a mistake or misunderstanding.

You’ve developed a high-level notion of what you want to make and the risks you need to anticipate. Now write it down in a lightweight design brief. Ground your thoughts and cement alignment around what you’re building and why. Much of this is simply summarizing all the work you’ve done so far into a single point of reference.

Your Sentient Design brief should tell a coherent story that connects human needs to the end solution you imagine. Include the essential elements below but keep it short. A sentence or handful of bullet points is plenty for each section.

list.li: Desired outcome: The measurable change in user behavior or experience you aim to create.
User story: A brief narrative expressing your hypothesis in terms of what the user wants, what’s stopping them, and what success looks like from their perspective.
Magical solution: The idealized experience that inspired your approach and the essential desire(s) it represents.
Practical solution: The real-world fix that can approximate your magic solution.
Experience pattern: The Sentient Design posture and experience pattern you’ll apply, with a brief description of how it will work.
Defensive design: Design strategies you’ll use to prevent or cushion identified risks.

Technical feasibility: Initial high-level assessment of data readiness (requirements, sources, and regulatory or ethical constraints). What kinds of machine intelligence will this require—prediction, classification, generation, clustering, recommendation? Can you use off-the-shelf models or is training required?
Success metrics: How you’ll know it’s working.

div.p: Think of the brief as your record of intent. Revisit it to make sure you’re building what you actually meant to build—and to hold yourself accountable to the outcomes you set out to achieve. Because now you’re ready to make it happen.

level3.p: DESIGN BRIEF: AMBIENT REPORTING

div.p: Here’s a sample design brief that might emerge from our “magic stopwatch” design sprint.
Desired outcome: Doctors and staff spend more time on patient care, less time on documentation.
User story: “As a clinician, I want my post-visit documentation to take as little time as possible, so that I can spend more time on patient care.”

Magical solution: With a “stopwatch” that stops or steals back time, the care provider no longer has to spend time on things they don’t like (documentation) and can spend it on things they do like (patient care). Essential desire: time no longer matters, and attention is abundant for both clinician and patient.

Practical solution: Steal back time by partially automating post-visit documentation. Draft visit reports using live transcription, summarization, and clinical/protocol suggestions based on patient history and best practices.

Experience pattern: Copilot pattern enables background transcription, with generated draft reports for the clinician to review, edit, and approve. Adjacent opportunities for NPC (comments/suggestions) and bespoke UI (on the fly suggestions and summaries).

Defensive design: All outputs are clearly marked as drafts until reviewed and confirmed. Review is eased by making summaries explainable, with clear links back to source transcript snippets. Gap identification and protocol suggestions are framed as optional nudges.

Technical feasibility: The system will rely on transcription, medical concept extraction, summarization, and lightweight prompt engineering. Off-the-shelf LLMs may do the trick for early prototyping, with custom tuning later for reliability and compliance. We will rely on a database and knowledge graph to make clinical suggestions and protocol recommendations. No protected health data is stored without encryption or used without permission.

Success metrics: Reduce average documentation time per visit by 30%. Ninety percent of clinicians rate the output as accurate or easy to edit. Patients report improved satisfaction with medical visits and with clinician attention. No increase in documentation-related errors or omissions. Improved clinician satisfaction, with reduced reports of burnout.

level2.p: When “gliding suitcase” inventor Bernard Sadow made his first prototype, he drilled furniture casters into the bottom of a suitcase. It was a scrappy test of the concept, using available parts to approximate the anti-gravity magic he imagined. He built his minimum magical product.

That’s what you’ll do in this final phase of the Sentient Design sprint. You’ll use real AI models, tools, and data to spin up scrappy prototypes. These are fast, exploratory builds designed to test feasibility, surface surprises, and sharpen your sense of what your idea can actually do. These prototypes also help surface emergent interaction patterns that wouldn’t reveal themselves in wireframes or Figma designs.

Prototyping has a long history in interaction design, but it takes on fresh importance when you’re working with machine intelligence. You often don’t know what’s easy, hard, or impossible until you try it.

The old-school waterfall process of “design then build” doesn’t work here. With intelligent interfaces, building is designing. By making working prototypes and tinkering directly with models, you adapt and learn throughout the ideation and design process.

level3.p: Lightweight prototypes allow for more adventurous exploration. When prototypes are expensive in time or resources, only the safest ideas tend to get tested.

Aim for lots of fast experiments and demonstrations by working directly with the models. Like Sadow and his furniture casters, you can start with available materials—off-the-shelf LLMs and applications—just to see if your ideas are directionally correct.

Prototypes begin with low-fidelity explorations. But here we’re not talking about the polish of visual fidelity but of capability fidelity (can AI handle core tasks?) and behavioral fidelity (does it behave as expected?). To that end, your prototype could be anything from a single system prompt to a front-end demo of NPCs responding to the user in the interface. The goal is not necessarily polish but confidence in the concept. Ten tested prompts alongside a spreadsheet of mock data can be more valuable than a polished UI mockup. If you emerge from this phase with real signals for what’s feasible and what needs rethinking, that’s success.
Specifically, there are three areas to test and explore.

Model-View-Controller (MVC) is a classic architecture of software design that separates an application into three distinct functions: the model incorporates the data and rules that back the system; the view is the interface the user sees and experiences; and the controller is the brain in the middle, juggling user input and system info to control the whole shebang.

Designers have traditionally worked only on the view (the interface) while the model and controller have been the developer’s focus. Sentient Design invites designers to blur those lines of responsibility. Prototyping and building these layers across disciplines means stronger alignment, shared vision, and faster validation. We’ll dig into the controller first, since that’s the most essential and exciting part of the designer’s new work.

level4.h4: Controller: The Brains of the Operation
level4.p: The controller layer is where the system decides what to do next. Until recently, that’s always been accomplished by programming the specific logic of every decision. Now you can give the underlying algorithm some general rules and requirements, and it figures out what to do on the fly based on user actions.

Describing application requirements is nothing new for designers, but now the site of that work changes. Instead of red-lining requirements to developers through design-file annotations, designers can talk directly to the system itself via the system prompt. As we introduced in Chapter 6, “Chat Is Not All Talk,” crafting the system prompt is now a central design activity, as well as an important surface for collaboration.
Use this prototyping phase to explore rough-and-ready system prompts to test the viability of specific parts of your idea and whether the system can make good decisions.

You can do this with mainstream AI services. All of the big AI providers offer “playgrounds” to try out system prompts for different models. These dashboards act as interactive workbenches to create primitive, instant applications for specific jobs. Use them as prototyping platforms to provide specialized knowledge in the form of subject matter documents, or to define role, guidelines, personality, and so on.

These tools are evolving quickly; examples at this writing include ChatGPT’s custom GPTs, Google AI Studio, Claude’s Projects, and Hugging Face Spaces. Whatever platform you choose, the goal is to create a sandbox where you can try ideas fast and learn from the system’s behavior.

A productive place to begin is exploring how well a system might understand intent for your user context. In Chapter 6, for example, we explored a system prompt that maps user intent to a design pattern for a city tour (see “Wiring Interface to Intent”). The simple prompt asked the system to interpret a request, identify the corresponding interface element to display, and turn it into structured data. It’s the essential foundation for creating call-and-response bespoke UI and a great place to start prototyping that kind of experience. You’re simultaneously testing and teaching the system, building up a prompt to inform the interface how to respond in a radically adaptive way.
ACTIVITY

div.p: EXPLORE SYSTEM PROMPTS

Use that same city tour prompt (or your own with similar instructions) and plug it into your preferred playground platform. Try a few different inputs against the system prompt to see what kind of results you get:

p.em: “I want to explore murals and underground galleries in Miami.”
“Where should I go to eat street food?”
“I only have one afternoon and love history.”

div.p: As you test, look for patterns in how the system succeeds and fails. Does it consistently understand certain types of intent but struggle with others? Are there ambiguous requests where it makes reasonable but wrong assumptions? These insights shape both your prompt and your interface design—you might need sharper input constraints in the UI, or you might discover the system handles ambiguity better than expected. Keep refining and learning.

The goal of this prompt exploration is to build intuition and validate how well the system can understand context and then do the right thing. As the designer, you’ll develop a sense of the input and framing the system needs, along with the kinds of responses you can expect in return.
Experiment broadly across the range of activities your system might support. Table 12.3 offers some examples and simple constructions to get you started.
TABLE 12.3 SYSTEM PROMPT TEMPLATES

td.p: Determine the user’s intent from the user’s [message or actions], and classify it into one of these intent categories: x, y, z.
Based on the user’s request, choose the most appropriate action to take: x, y, z.
Choose the appropriate tool or data source to fulfill the user’s request: x, y, z.
Identify the appropriate tone (x, y, z) to respond to the user’s message and respond with the [message, color theme, UI element] most appropriate to that tone.
Determine the user’s goal and make a plan to accomplish it in a sequenced list of 3–5 tasks.
Evaluate if the user’s request is clear and actionable. If it is ambiguous or lacks details, respond with a clarifying question.
Evaluate the user’s request for prohibited, harmful, or out-of-scope content. If found, respond with a safe alternative or explanation.
Respond in the voice, style, and knowledge domain of [persona]. Maintain this character across all replies and do not break that role.
Based on the user’s [message or actions], select the best UI pattern to provide or request info as appropriate: x, y, z.
After every answer, include a short explanation of why you chose this response and your confidence level on a 0–100 scale.
If the task is outside your capabilities or should be handled by [human/tool], explain why and suggest or enable the next action.

div.p: Try your own versions of these prompts to test how well the system behaves across these narrow slices of capability. Start small: pick one capability, test it with a handful of representative user requests, and examine how the system performs. If it works, keep going. If it breaks, celebrate—because you just found your next design move. Expand and refine your prompt to provide the detail the system needs to make good decisions. In most cases, you’ll end up combining several of these into a more comprehensive system prompt.

In the ambient reporting concept for our medical clinic, for example, you might try a variation of intent classification to classify sentences from a patient transcript into specific categories for organization. Here’s a sample prompt to give it a spin:

p.code: You will receive spoken or written statements from a patient during a medical visit. For each statement, classify it into one or more of the following categories:-Symptom-Medical history-Medication-Lifestyle factor-Clarification or question-Personal life-Other/irrelevant
Return your classification along with a brief explanation.

div.p: Try feeding this prompt some sample patient statements. Notice how it handles ambiguity: does “I’ve been tired lately” get classified as symptom, lifestyle, or both? These edge cases reveal where you’ll need to refine your categories or add clarifying logic.

Speaking of categories, we’re doing something tricky here: we’re using a large language model (generative AI) to do work that’s more efficiently done by another kind of algorithm (classification), as you’ll recall from Chapter 2. LLMs are a flexible starting point because they’re broadly available and easy to prototype with. But as your idea matures beyond the prototype, you might consider swapping in other algorithms—recommendation, classification, prediction, clustering—to improve performance, speed, or cost. Many platforms offer low-code or no-code tools that let you upload data, define categories or goals, and train lightweight models with just a few clicks. (At this writing, platforms like Google Vertex AI, Azure ML Studio, Amazon SageMaker Canvas, and Hugging Face Spaces support this kind of prototyping.)

No matter what algorithm you use, the quality of the response is only as good as the context you give it. That’s where the “model” layer comes in; the “M” in MVC provides the system with grounding knowledge and data.

level4.p: The model layer gives a software system its worldview—its memory, knowledge, and rules. It provides the system’s facts, both from past experience (like user history) and from broader domain knowledge (like business rules or product data). Prototyping the model layer means figuring out the intel the system needs to make good decisions.

Mainstream AI models already come with a big chunk of world knowledge. The bit you have to provide is the domain knowledge (subject matter expertise and business rules) and user knowledge (current context and user data).

In the final application, the model layer typically takes the form of an internal database or calls to external systems. For prototyping, though, you can use simple documents like PDFs, spreadsheets, or plain text files.

In our ambient reporting example, domain knowledge might include PDFs of medical coding guidelines or common prescription protocols. User knowledge could be anonymized patient histories and text files of visit transcripts. Feed these materials directly to your prototype through your playground platform’s upload features or just by pasting relevant excerpts into the conversation.

Evaluate how this additional context affects the results of your system prompt. Does the system better understand medical abbreviations, for example? Does adding prescription guidelines help it alert to potential drug interactions?

The goal is to discover not only what kind of information matters most in what context, but also how much is enough and when. In Chapter 5, we explored the strange limits of working memory for large language models. Because of the constraints of context windows, these systems can focus only on scoped chunks of information at a time to do their decision-making. It matters what you share and when.

Use this prototyping exploration to be strategic about the info you provide. What’s essential for helping the system understand context and intent to make good decisions? When should you provide the system with a summary of a patient visit, for example, versus the whole transcript?
ACTIVITY

div.p: TEACH THE SYSTEM

Start by getting a handle on what mainstream models can do without special training. Do this by sending requests with your system prompt, relying only on the world knowledge that comes out of the box. Gradually add one domain document at a time and then try the same requests again.

list.li: What improves and what doesn’t?
How do results change when you give the system summaries of certain info instead of complete content?
What do these results tell you about the kind of information that the system needs to make good decisions?

div.p: Exploring the model and controller layers is likely new to your design process. Think of these activities as design research. You’re learning the inputs the system needs from the user and the business, and what kinds of results you can expect to come out the other end. This develops your intuition for what the system is good and bad at, and these inputs and outputs also give you the working material for designing the user experience. That means you’re ready to start prototyping the user experience: the view layer!

level4.p: Take a moment to think where the most magic lies in your minimum magical product. What’s the stuff that feels hardest, newest, or strangest? Go back to your selected posture and experience patterns from the Sentient Triangle. Flip back to the chapters or sections that detail what makes those specific experiences work and identify the core interactions that will make your magic fly.
In particular, look for moments when the system needs to:

list.li: Interpret ambiguous input.
Make decisions.
Adapt presentation.
Collaborate with the user.
Recover from mistakes.

level4.p: ACTIVITY

div.p: SKETCH YOUR MAGIC MOMENT

Now you’re ready to do your designerly work. Sketch out interfaces that demonstrate key interactions and present your riskiest assumptions. This starts with a familiar design process: storyboard core flows, wireframe fundamental layouts, and build foundational components to draw upon as a basic toolkit. Ask AI to take a crack at doing this for you, using the document canvas features of tools like Claude, Gemini, or ChatGPT. Give the tool your design brief and follow up with prompts to generate interface mockups or working prototypes.

Don’t commit too early to a single direction. Explore adjacent experience patterns from the Sentient Triangle. Experiment with small interventions of casual intelligence and then pivot to ambitious, more radically adaptive experiences. Challenge yourself to sketch three to five different concepts for an idea.

As you go, capture not only static states but also dynamic behaviors. How does the interface respond when the AI is uncertain, or what happens when it suggests something unexpected? Annotate your sketches with these behavioral notes or open questions.

Fight your instinct to control every aspect of the design. Leave some room for the system to introduce some serendipity and productive friction. Where should those moments be? When should the system itself be responsible for selecting UI elements instead of relying on fixed design decisions?

level4.p: These are your magic moments. In the copilot experience for our medical clinic’s ambient reporting application, for example, there are several different stages and interactions to focus on: gathering and summarizing patient history before the patient visit; making sense of the conversation during the meeting; suggesting topics or questions throughout the conversation; and reviewing and editing the generated report.

Just focusing on that last stage of report editing provides plenty of design work. How might the system act as an NPC offering suggestions or prompts during the editing process? How can well-understood conventions for commenting and editing be deployed there? How can those conventions support explainability for showing why the report says what it says, tracking back to the original patient conversation?

From there, review what you learned from the controller and model activities about the level of intelligence and messaging you can expect from the system. That gives you the building blocks for how your interface will look and behave.

level3.p: As you work through your designs, keep revisiting your system prompt and model documents. Can you refine the prompt to approximate the logic and messaging that your interface promises?

Let’s say you want the system to take the role of NPC editor adding comments to the medical report. Can you get the system prompt to identify passages that deserve a comment and return the passage/comment pairing? If you can get the system to associate a patient’s comment about “occasional chest pain,” for example, with a generated pointer to cardiac assessment protocols, you’ve suddenly got data objects you can turn into design objects. Flip back to your design: how would this info appear? As a sidebar comment? An inline suggestion? Does the model layer contain enough detail about those protocols to give reliable answers?

Constantly moving between model, view, and controller like this helps you validate the gist of your design as you go. It’s almost certain that some ideas won’t work as you hoped, but new interaction concepts will emerge. Let the view layer inspire prompt refinements to the controller layer; let prompt limitations inform interface constraints. Dig into it, explore, have fun.

level2.p: By the end of this phase, you will have a glorious jumble of interface designs, system prompts, and model documents. It might feel unsettled, like a mechanic’s garage where all the parts are scattered everywhere but no car in sight. Don’t worry, you’re right where you’re supposed to be. Now it’s time to see if this contraption can actually take you somewhere.

In fact, you’ve already traveled a long way in your magic mobile. Check in: do your prototype pieces address your original outcome and hypothesis? If the focus has drifted, has that drift moved you in a productive direction? Does your work point to a solution to a meaningful question, one that might add dramatic new value?

If the answer is yes (or even just maybe), start putting the pieces together. If you’ve been prototyping so far as a solo designer, now is a good time to bring others into the mix. Work with a cross-functional team of design, product, and engineering to start welding it all into a real application. Work with your team to move out of the playground environment, connecting your draft system prompts to the front-end interface you’ve created.
Focus on small next steps. Keep your minimum magical product minimal as you learn how it works and behaves. Then comes the fun part: run it through real scenarios with real people.

list.li: Does the system understand intent and make good decisions?
Do users “get” the new experience patterns and feel served by them?
Do you see your desired outcomes emerging from how people use and respond to the experience?

level2.h2: A Mindful Process for Sentient Design
level2.p: The Sentient Design sprint format won’t give you all the answers, but it does provide a framework for asking the right questions. Along the way, promising ideas will flop, and crazy ones will fly. Failure and discovery should be an intentional pairing when you craft new experiences with emerging technology. That’s how you learn the opportunities and limits of your design material.

You might discover some new muscles as you go. From the whimsy of practical magic to the “building is designing” mindset of rapid prototyping, the Sentient Design sprint asks you to tackle not only new technique but also new perspective. Working with machine intelligence will change how you conceive and deliver digital products. The same goes for your colleagues across disciplines, which means inevitable shifts in roles and team dynamics.

The work changes. Suddenly everyone in the production process can collaborate across model, view, and controller—every area of implementation—to shape the experience. Even the algorithms themselves are participating, reaching upstream of the end product into the design process itself as AI tools take on production tasks.
Like Sadow’s gliding suitcase, machine intelligence gives you new wheels to make magic a practical reality. But how does it change what you’re making and how you work with others? Let’s find out.

level1.p: Work the Problem

p.a: The Promise and Threat of Automated Creativity
A Unifying Mission: Only One Deliverable Matters
Machine Literacy Is Everyone’s Job Now
You Are Needed More Than Ever

level1.p: “HOUSTON, WE’VE HAD A PROBLEM.”

When an explosion ripped through the Apollo 13 spacecraft in 1970, it triggered one of history’s most iconic rescue missions, upending NASA’s traditional way of working in the process. The agency was famous for its meticulous checklists and rigid procedures, but this emergency wasn’t in the script. So, NASA put aside the binders and established a nimble team focused on just one goal: bring those astronauts home.
Crisis demanded the change, but the habits were already embedded in NASA’s culture. Five elements made the difference, and they’re the same five that define successful Sentient Design teams now:

list.li: Human-computer collaboration. NASA’s mainframes were slow and clunky by today’s standards, but the hulking machines modeled “what if” scenarios that unlocked the controllers’ creative hacks. The computers didn’t provide solutions, but they gave confidence to the improvisation that followed.

A common problem space. NASA trained its engineers to be “generalist-specialists” who understood the whole system as well as their own specialty. When Apollo’s oxygen tank exploded, everyone could instantly grasp the cascading failures. Propulsion engineers could talk meaningfully with life support engineers, or electrical with guidance. The shared mental model meant specialists could work on the same problem at the same time.

Improvisation mindset. NASA’s relentless simulations drilled teams on what to do when things went wrong. Controllers were used to thinking on their feet, so they could abandon procedures when reality demanded that they “work the problem” to find a new way.

Hands-on prototyping. In Houston, full-scale spacecraft mockups let engineers experiment in real time with the same materials the crew had. They cut hoses and taped cardboard together to model fixes before radioing instructions to the astronauts (Figure 13.1).
Unifying mission. A single clarifying goal—bring the crew home alive—overrode all other concerns. The crisis dissolved turf wars and red tape to create a sense of collective ownership.

p.span: NASA controllers used duct tape, maps, and other materials available to the astronauts to design devices like this DIY carbon dioxide filter.

level1.p: The urgency of adopting AI-mediated interfaces may not compare to the life-and-death stakes of Apollo 13, but it’s still a fundamental challenge that requires the same kind of organizational rethink. The technology has changed since 1970, but the Apollo team’s habits are exactly what product teams need now.

You’ve seen how Sentient Design requires new perspectives, techniques, and processes. This does more than change how you arrange pixels on screens. It changes how you arrange people, too. Roles change, including your relationship to technology and tools. What does the new product team look like, and how should designers expect their roles to change? What new skills are required, and which old ones can we set aside?

level2.h2: The Promise and Threat of Automated Creativity
level2.p: Look, we need to talk about the thing that may have been nagging at you from the first pages of this book: If machine intelligence can design interfaces and drive the interaction, what happens to me? You may have had this feeling if you’ve seen AI tools conjure working prototypes in seconds. As you watch machine intelligence speed and democratize the creation of software, you might be asking if your carefully honed skills are becoming irrelevant.
You’re not crazy: some of those skills will no longer be needed. Machine intelligence is already more than capable at low-level production tasks, and the tools are becoming more capable every day.

But new doors open to brand new skills. We haven’t explored how designers can use AI-powered design tools because there’s something bigger at stake. Rather than grinding out process efficiencies, Sentient Design emphasizes the use of machine intelligence to create valuable new experiences.

The twist is that the tools for building software increasingly have their own Sentient Design experiences. When you use machine-intelligent tools to whip up illustrations or manifest an instant prototype, you’re experiencing firsthand how your users will collaborate with machine intelligence in your own products. Every interaction with AI tools teaches you about creating Sentient Design experiences, and every AI experience you design will change how you think about your tools. The snake eats its own tail as our creations remake the act of creation.

p.em: The products of Sentient Design are in turn changing the act of design.

level2.p: Every technological wave has created entirely new job categories even as it sweeps away old ones. But those new roles don’t always appear in the same place or for the same people. When this is happening around you, it’s deeply unsettling: What if those new jobs don’t come this time … or they don’t come to me? That’s an entirely reasonable fear, since we don’t yet know what those new roles might be.

Machines joining the fray isn’t new, of course. Robots have been on physical assembly lines for decades, and technology has long supported white-collar workers—including you, designer. But machine intelligence feels like something different. Unlike the assembly lines of the industrial revolution or the spreadsheets of the information age, AI seems to be coming for creative work itself.

It will be okay. The automation of tasks doesn’t always (or even often) eliminate entire jobs. Creative work is full of chores nobody will miss. Let the robots resize your assets or update the documentation or organize all those sticky notes. Offloading chores can redefine and clarify roles rather than erase them.
So, let the chores go. Focus on the new creative work that we’ve explored here. There’s so much invention ahead to design for this weird, ambiguous design material.
Seen this way, AI elevates design instead of replacing it.

Sentient Design creates a new kind of collaboration between the designer and AI. When the Apollo 13 controllers needed solutions, they asked their IBM mainframes to calculate trajectories, while the engineers figured out what to do with the opportunities that emerged. Do the same: let AI draft possibilities while you and your team guide those possibilities forward. Spend less time on mechanical execution and more time defining problems and shaping solutions. What remains is the work that requires vision, heart, and connection—qualities that don’t collapse neatly into probabilistic pattern-matching.

Sure, the conveyor belt is getting faster, but what Sentient Design really needs isn’t a faster assembly line but a new kind of factory entirely. That includes a new job description that you might already be experiencing in your own work.

level3.p: Forget NASA for a sec: If you want to see Star Wars–level droids in action, visit the warehouse of a company like Amazon or Walmart. Fleets of squat orange robots glide under shelves, and self-driving tuggers ferry pallets across vast aisles. Forklifts drive themselves, guided not by the Force but by sensors, software, and a growing crew of people who keep them on track.

Many of that crew used to drive the machines but now manage them, a job sometimes called robot wrangler. Part of the gig is rescuing wayward machines that get stuck, just like a Roomba vacuum cleaner might get lost in the corner of your living room. The robot wrangler manages the system that guides the robots and helps them out when they stray beyond their guardrails.

Robot wrangling is a likely aspect of everyone’s future work. When machine intelligence takes on production tasks, someone has to keep an eye on them. As you saw in Chapter 7, “Agents and the Manager Experience,” working with agents shifts the user experience to delegation and oversight. As the roles and work of people and machine continue to overlap, designers will become robot wranglers, too.

This work could turn into dreary housekeeping, tidying up machine-generated mistakes and hallucinations as if cleaning up after unruly teenagers. Technology creates chores in some places even as it saves work in others. But there’s more exciting and creative work to be done.

“Good, fast, cheap—pick two.” That was the long-standing rule of thumb for building (and differentiating) software. But as machine intelligence turns fast and cheap into givens, all that remains is “good” or “not good” (Figure 13.2).

AI-powered tools turn rapid prototyping into instant prototyping. Designers can spin up working code to test ideas on the fly. Developers get wild speed boosts for faster, cheaper delivery. Point these tools in the right direction, and they spin out respectable results fast.
When the machines handle the implementation, they’ve got the “how” covered. Your job is to handle the “what” and “why” to figure out the good.

Insight, strategy, originality, experience, deep audience understanding—these are the things that remain scarce in an era where fast and cheap are abundant. They are also uniquely human traits, sometimes rolled up into something called taste.

Taste goes deeper than decoration. Good taste is knowing what is perfectly right for this moment, this user, this outcome. Discretion informs what to serve in the moment and discernment informs what to hold back. How do interactions land? What info does the user need in the moment?

Yet again, technology is pushing our attention higher up the stack. It always has: Punch cards gave way to byte-sized assembly code, which gave way to computer languages … and now we can just describe what we want. So that description has to be considered and thoughtful.

p.span: Machine intelligence is collapsing the traditional project-management triangle of good, fast, cheap. Will you choose “good” or “not good”?

level3.p: The brief becomes the thing. For the Sentient Design team, this returns the focus to fundamentals. Everyone on the team engages in more human inquiry and less technical implementation: more emphasis on research, requirements, and outcomes, less on specific outputs. Instead of writing the code or designing every pixel, the team works together to define the goals and the approach to meet them. Apollo 13’s unifying mission begins to take hold here.

In Sentient Design teams, everyone contributes to the “what” and “why,” regardless of core discipline. That’s a change for many organizations, so let’s turn from human-computer collaboration to how the people on your team can work together and why.

level2.p: Machine intelligence creates a powerful opportunity to break down silos between product, design, and engineering. Rather than organizing teams by discipline, we can organize them around the problems we’re solving.

Many organizations already do this with feature teams, multidisciplinary teams tasked to deliver on tightly-focused feature missions. In practice, though, those teams often continue to rely on traditional waterfall process, just at a miniature, feature-level scale. Product folks still define requirements, designers plan the experience, and then developers make it work, all handing off one to the next. Everyone might be on the same team, but the process remains siloed, and everyone tends to work in separate spaces and documents.
Sentient Design projects flatten and collapse those silos. This is something that happens almost on its own, simply because so much happens in a common workspace: the system prompt.

Chapters 6, “Chat Is Not All Talk,” and 12, “The Sentient Design Sprint,” revealed how crafting the prompt is now a central design focus, the place where designers describe how the system should make decisions and interact with users. But this is not only a design activity. It’s also the place where product managers realize product requirements and where developers tell the system how to work with the underlying architecture.

No single discipline “owns” the system prompt any more than any of Apollo 13’s mission controllers owned the spacecraft. Then as now, a common problem space promotes a generalist-specialist mindset. Every specialist contributor has a shared, plain-language description of how the system works, with easy insight into other disciplines’ contributions. Just like the Apollo team, you get a cross-pollinated understanding of the system across all roles.

The common problem space unlocks something special: Instead of working in a linear fashion, everyone can work together simultaneously, riffing off each other’s contributions to formally encode the what and why.

This is a profound change to how product development typically works, and it was enabled by the rise of large language models. For the first time, anyone can talk directly to the system in plain language. Access and influence over the system’s central behavior is no longer gated by developers’ exclusive knowledge of the code and framework, or by designers’ facility with Figma, or by product managers’ access to stakeholders. The system prompt is a glorious combination of design spec, requirements doc, and programming language accessible to all.

As shown in Figure 13.3, this dramatically increases the overlap between disciplines, blending workspaces, time frames, and responsibilities that were once distinct. The boundaries blur not because roles disappear, but because fluency across them grows. An individual’s expertise becomes less about technical prowess and more about the lens they bring: how they frame problems, imagine solutions, and interpret the weirdness of AI systems.

Every discipline still maintains its own specific focus and domains, but far more work happens together. Teams dream up ideas, tailor requirements, design, develop, and test together. Individuals can still do their thinking wherever they do their best work—sketching in Figma or writing pseudo-code in an editor—but the end result comes together in the prompt.

p.span: The shared space of problem and solution is growing with Sentient Design, which promotes a unified product team over siloed disciplines.

level2.p: This overlapping arrangement turns out to be well suited to the unpredictable nature of machine intelligence as a design material. Its weirdness makes it risky to make assumptions, so you have to constantly validate and refine ideas.

It’s like the Apollo 13 engineers who had to design solutions based on the spare parts materials available onboard. They couldn’t assume they could send astronauts instructions for what to do until they built it themselves. As you learned in Chapter 12, building is part of the design process. “Work the problem.”

Machine intelligence allows teams to explore and evaluate concepts with remarkable speed. Concepts that once required extensive development can now be simulated and adjusted in a tight feedback loop. This enables testing not just of final products, but also of early assumptions, edge cases, and competing hypotheses. As a result, testing becomes less of a concluding phase and more of a continuous mode of inquiry that’s integral to the creative and strategic process itself.

The process is naturally improvisational. Things rarely work exactly as you first expect, so the team has to be prepared to shift to new solutions or even revisit business requirements. Make Sentient Design sprints a regular part of your cadence, not a one-off at the start of the project.

It requires trust and structure to let this new kind of collaboration thrive. It pays off not only in efficiency but in a fundamentally more human-centered way to make things. It also promotes a unifying worldview that’s unusual in traditional product organizations.

level2.h2: A Unifying Mission: Only One Deliverable Matters
level2.p: Designers have a document problem they can’t quit. Your authors have certainly been guilty of it. We’ve fretted over journey maps, wireframes, prototypes, and high-fidelity comps like precious jewels. We’ve enshrined them as key milestones, even calling them deliverables, as if those interim documents were ever a real goal.

But let’s be honest: those design artifacts are ultimately unimportant. The real deliverable is the product itself. Everything else gets thrown away when you ship. The only thing that matters is getting that product in front of users—and what happens next.

When teams work in silos, they tend to focus exclusively on their specific contribution. It’s easy to lose sight of the end state the whole team is trying to achieve. Buffing interim artifacts to a high shine costs time and energy that could be better put toward moving the shipping product forward. Design artifacts are important thinking tools, but somewhere along the line these files, which no user will ever see, somehow became the grand finale for designers.

When Apollo 13’s oxygen tank blew up, NASA didn’t convene separate meetings for propulsion, electrical, and life support teams to craft their respective responses or crank out detailed binders. Instead of isolating in separate units, they came together to rally for the only thing that mattered: bring the crew home alive.

Flight directors, engineers, and technicians huddled in “tiger teams” to sketch ideas on whiteboards and napkins, or rummage through spare parts. They built their fixes together in real time, tested them in the simulator, and relayed the results straight to the astronauts. At every stage, instead of creating perfect documentation, they figured out the smallest possible thing they could do to validate and communicate what to do next.

If Apollo 13 had instead been a software project run by UX and UI designers, the team might have wound up fretting over Figma designs for three weeks before proposing the solution. The designs would have been perfect, but they would have arrived too late for the astronauts.
As designers ourselves, we say this with love: don’t lose sight of the unifying mission.

level3.p: Don’t get us wrong: Up-front thinking and planning are critically important. It’s as important to build the right thing (strategy) as it is to build the thing right (tactics). That takes lots of thinking, talking, and experimentation. The original spirit of wireframes, journey maps, mockups, and the like was for exploration and to communicate ideas efficiently. They began as devices for conversation and experimentation, and in that loose context, they remain as useful as ever.

But over time, process calcified. Artifacts became shrines. Sketches turned into “milestones” and “deliverables.” It became the norm to design a website over and over again across several different formats. First, the UX designer sweats over a detailed wireframe. Then the UI designer replaces the wireframe with a detailed mockup (and then another and another and another). Then the interaction designer replaces those comps with a full-blown motion prototype. Our industry builds generations of carefully crafted, disposable facsimiles before even writing a line of code. It’s a waste of time, effort, and resources.

Instead of making software, this approach pours huge energy into making drawings of screens, aspirational watercolors of imagined interfaces. At the end of the day, the job is to ship software, not pictures of software. Or to put it in the words of the Agile Manifesto, favor “working software over comprehensive documentation.”

This does not mean that you should stop making wireframes and UI mockups. They’re necessary. Keep doing your planning. Keep doing your thinking where you can be most expressive. If that’s Figma, great. But save the high polish for the product itself. Treat your artifacts the way the Apollo 13 team did, as sketches and clipped conversations.
For the most part, design files exist to help developers understand what to build. Not all “instructions” require so much detail.

list.li: What’s the least I can do to effectively communicate what to do next?
What conversation, sketch, or prototype will get across what needs to be done so we can get on with it?

level3.p: Two things help a Sentient Design team be effective here. First, as you’ve seen, the team works in the same problem space. When you work closely together, communication comes easier and documentation can be lighter. Instead of creating separate documents you can move decisions directly into code and into product. The system prompt is simultaneously product and documentation.

Second, AI-powered tools can help build those traditional artifacts far more quickly. Use this new generation of design tools to generate “good enough” wireframes or design files—or shortcut them altogether with a working prototype. Claude could rough out a working prototype before you could even draw a handful of rectangles in Figma. It doesn’t have to be perfect; it just has to be enough to have a conversation and get the point across so that everyone understands what to build.

Not everything has to be done this way—if you need to noodle in Figma to figure something out, do that. But treat the activity as a thinking exercise, not a required piece of documentation. It’s okay to be messy as long as it’s expressive. Effective Sentient Design teams trust their colleagues to get it. In the end, when the Apollo 13 crew finally splashed down in the Pacific, nobody asked to see the documentation.

level2.h2: Machine Literacy Is Everyone’s Job Now
level2.p: The Sentient Design team achieves this kind of singular focus only when the whole team is comfortable using machine intelligence. Nimble process also leans on using the modern crop of available tools to spin up quick-and-dirty prototypes to keep things moving.

Machine literacy relies on working critically with machine-intelligent systems. Teams succeed based on the know-how of its individual members, and that’s you. To do Sentient Design, you have to be at ease with its material. The robot-powered product team relies on robot-powered designers.

Reading this book is a great start. But working literacy means more than book knowledge. You have to use the stuff on a regular basis and develop an intuition for how it works. Can you tell when the model is winging it? How to get formatted responses? When to trust or double-check an answer?

AI tools are becoming commonplace and accessible to all. But accessibility is not the same as expertise. You have to do the reps. Work regularly with AI tools in your everyday practice to get a sense of what’s easy, hard, or impossible for these models to do. Experiment, push the models around, don’t take a first failure as the end of the story. They constantly surprise.

Keep some lightness and play at the heart of your expertise-building efforts. Maintain space for toy projects and playful exploration between the hard problems. You can learn a lot when the stakes are low or by splashing in puddles. Every whimsical prompt and side project builds literacy.

The most high-performing teams also know how to play together. Turn some of these side projects into team projects so that you all learn together. Take interest in expanding the machine literacy of your colleagues, too.

That interest turns into more formal responsibility when it comes to your users. As the creator of Sentient Design interfaces, it’s your job to build experiences that cultivate ease, confidence, and intuition in the underlying technology. That doesn’t mean turning users into prompt engineers. They don’t have to understand the building blocks of machine intelligence in the way that you now understand them. Instead, the experiences you create should promote clear mental models that quietly make your users machine literate simply through confident use. Cultivate their practical skepticism and help them know when to engage critical thought and focus their judgment.

These have always been important goals in interaction design, but the strangeness of machine intelligence requires us to double down in the interest of our users. When machine-intelligent systems fall short of their promise, it’s design, not data science, that decides whether users feel lost or empowered.

level2.h2: You Are Needed More Than Ever
level2.p: The astronauts made it home, of course. The Apollo 13 rescue mission succeeded because it was a profoundly human endeavor disguised as a feat of engineering. The team approached its impossible challenge with urgency and technical know-how for sure, but even more so with curiosity, adaptability, and a compassionate focus on the human stakes. The mission was never really about technology.
The same goes for Sentient Design. Instead of celebrating the agency of the algorithm, Sentient Design focuses on amplifying the agency of its users—and your agency as a designer.

You have a remarkable new design material to work with. Sentient Design makes it possible to invent new categories of product and service. We’ve explored a wide range of interactions and experiences to support this effort, but plenty of invention remains.

It takes time to coax the nuances of a new design material. It took well over a decade to figure out what mobile “wants.” For years, mobile apps were just stunted versions of desktop software. Slowly designers learned that mobile wanted to be something else entirely: single-tap experiences like Uber, card-like feeds like Instagram, and camera-based interfaces like TikTok.

We’re still learning what intelligent interfaces will become. Best practices will continue to develop, but in the meantime, there’s also the opportunity and obligation to invent them. This is a time for wild ideas, for taking leaps beyond the familiar or incremental.

Many questions remain about how machine intelligence will rumble through our screens, our lives, our society—and with what effect. Its potential for good and bad are both fairly spectacular. Wayward algorithms will continue to deliver unintended consequences, just as they’ll deliver moments of genuine wonder. How it all plays out, of course, depends on how we use the technology. As a designer of this new generation of experiences, that’s up to you.

We’ve put together a map to guide you, but there’s still much to discover. “We can only see a short distance ahead,” Alan Turing wrote about AI in 1950, “but we can see plenty there that needs to be done.”
We believe in you. Go make something amazing.

level1.p: TABLE A.1 EXPERIENCE PATTERNS

td.p: Transformers of formats and interaction modes
New channel or medium for data or content
Task-focused helpers with light access to tools
Shape-shifting interface conceived in real time
Strategic coordinators of complex, multisystem workflows
Dynamic command center for plans and progress
Continuous, context-aware companions for an activity or system
Ambient presence that surfaces at the right moment
Deferential sidekick, always at the ready
Enhancer at the point of action
Manifesters of user ideas and intent
User-like presence in a shared interface
Caretakers of a scoped, ongoing business function
Specialists for executing complex but tightly scoped tasks
Gofer subordinate that does one job really well
Piped to adjacent steps or systems

level1.p: TABLE A.2 FUNCTIONAL PATTERNS

td.p: User assistance to help the system improve
Targeted edits that preserve the integrity of the whole
Easy access to a proposed set of predefined tasks
Suggestions or actions based on monitored context
Low-fidelity ideas transformed into high-fidelity results
Presentation of adjacent or competing results that together tell an aggregate story
Stash of previous versions for easy comparison and rollback

level1.p: TABLE A.3 INTERACTION PATTERNS

td.p: Inline prompt inserted where you want results to appear
Method to select content and trigger action
Adjacent chat to drive and discuss the primary workspace
Guided assembly of open-ended prompt instructions
Feedback to help users sharpen requests to the system
Real-time view of an agent’s activity and progress
Natural-language instructions for agentic procedures and processes
Indicator of the source data behind generated results
Choice between two or more generated outputs
Representation of agreement (or not) across sources or models
Quick access to relevant actions based on current context
Split view for overview and deep inspection of agent tasks
Fill-in-the-blank prompt interface for templated control
Suggestion for what the user should “say” in requests to the system
Explicit, fine-grained control over system output
Lightweight control to capture user sentiment at scale
Multistep method to report and categorize problems
Visualized, glanceable confidence levels for generated results
Simplified control of generated output by editing attributes
Visualization of an agent’s process steps
