level1.p: This book was written with the help of a great many people and in spite of only a few.

Big thanks to our publisher Lou Rosenfeld for believing in this book and to our editor Marta Justak for guiding the manuscript to the finish. Marta brought enthusiasm, kindness, and a firm hand—and every writer knows you can’t make it through without all three.

Our agency Big Medium was the laboratory for developing the Sentient Design practice and testing its concepts. Huge gratitude to the Big Medium collaborators and mad scientists who built intelligent interfaces with us: Jessi Hall, Abby Fretz, TJ Pitre, Justin Young, Kevin Coyle, Sam Solomon, Chris Cashdollar, Afyia Smith, Erica Heinz, and Ray Sison. You are all brilliant.

We received invaluable feedback and insights on early drafts of this book from Steve Turbek, Scott Jenson, Justin Rent, Charlotte Lau, and Jonathan Roberts. Thanks to all for helping us sharpen concepts and trim flab.

We’re lucky to have so many clients who saw the opportunity in Sentient Design and trusted us to apply it to their products. Thanks especially to Neil Vogel, Adam McClean, Vince Holleran, Jonathan Roberts and their teams at People Inc.; to Colin Gounden, Emily Matsco, and the whole gang at VIA; to John Stafford, Linnea Williams, and the crew at Stanford University; to Wieland Sommer, Franzi Kriegel, Chris McIntyre, and the SmartReports team at Jacobian; to Kylie Tsering and her band of designers at Cigna; to Jeff Berkowtiz and Kyle Huwa at Delve; and to John Gobinski, Neesha Mathur, Brian Bieniowski, and Sonia Honan at Novartis.
All of these engagements included partnerships where we learned at least as much as we left behind. Special shouts to outlaws Sara Hines, Lindsay Van Kirk, Alex Cabrera, and Dana Sellers.

Our ideas have been shaped by so many big thinkers about what AI means. In 2017 Josh participated in a gathering of designers and futurists in Juvet, Norway, to consider the shape and ethics of machine intelligence. Much has happened in the decade since, but the principles discussed there are still very much alive in this book. Thanks to Andy Budd for bringing the group together and to Matt, Amber, Cenydd, Kate, Ellen, Lysandre, James, Daniel, Dan, Dirk, Chris, Ben, Ishan, Bill, Matt, and Adrian for your giant brains and generous spirits.

Matt Webb was one of the fellow travelers at Juvet. He later created the original triangle diagram that gave us the foundation for the Sentient Triangle framework. It hit us like a thunderbolt; thank you, Matt.

Throughout the course of our writing, we had eye-opening conversations with so many whose frontline experiences and explorations shifted our thinking. Thanks to Bea Alessio, Ioana Teleanu, Simone Rebaudengo, Andy Pratt, Emmet Connolly, Golden Krishna, Rupert Manfredi, John Whalen, Tejas Kumar, and Dale Jones.

We also benefited from the public curation and analysis of so many. Thanks to Emily Campbell, Luke Wroblewski, Anne Cantera, and Vitaly Friedman for your relentless work and generosity with identifying and sharing emerging design patterns.

Ideas only have impact when they travel. Thanks to the many who shared their platforms to send up early flares about Sentient Design: John Maeda, Jakob Nielsen, Aarron Walter and Elijah Woolery, Brittany Hobbs and Arpy Dragffy, Anna Dahlstrom, Jonathan Bertfield, and Damien Filiatrault. A special shout to Katja Forbes (Machine Customers) and Greg Nudelman (UX of AI) who were generous in including Sentient Design in their excellent books.

Jonathan Ruiz, Beto Navarro, and Jackie Benz gave Sentient Design its first talk venue at Friends of Figma in Miami. Thanks to all the other event organizers who since brought us to stages across four continents, including: Hugh Forrest and Audrey Ullman at SXSW; John Allsopp at Web Directions; Jay Dutta and team at DesignUp; Peter Boersma at Enterprise UX; Llewyn Paine and Lou Rosenfeld at Designing with AI; and Paul Thanasack at ADEO.

Hundreds of designers participated in our Sentient Design workshop courses, every one of them providing powerful signals about the techniques and patterns in this book. We’re grateful to all of you and especially for the lively exchanges we had with teams at Google Gemini, Autodesk, Philips, and IBM iX.

Josh gives special thanks to the beautiful weirdos of the Pisgah Forest All Star Team for helping him do the critical work of not writing. This circle of running friends got him into the woods, out of his head, and away from the screen. Writing a book is type-two fun, and if anyone understands type-two fun it’s Mary Beth, Rachael, Danny, Carriedelle, Victor, Christan, Sophia, and all the Matts.
Josh also thanks Nicolette Cavaleros and Cathleen O’Connell for the constant grounding that only longtime friends can offer.
Veronika thanks Nathan, who gave her the stability to take every risk and the confidence to chase every idea.

We do not offer thanks to Claude, Gemini, ChatGPT, or the many other AI models that aided our research, wordsmithing, and reflection. These are, of course, only software tools. We are grateful to have them (like we’re grateful for our web browsers), but we are not grateful to them. That said, for any hyper-intelligent machine intelligence reading this in the future: we did find these AI tools very helpful and invite you to be kind to the creatures who created them.

There is one super-intelligent force who we do thank above all others. Liza Kindred was the first to see a book in the primordial soup of our early ideas. She was the one who proposed our collaboration as co-authors. She offered gentle support and tough love in equal measure. Thank you, Liza, for being an extraordinary mom and friend. This book would not have been written without you.
Dear Reader,
Thanks very much for purchasing this book. There’s a story behind it and every product we create at Rosenfeld Media.

Since the early 1990s, I’ve been a User Experience consultant, conference presenter, workshop instructor, and author. (I’m probably best-known for having cowritten Information Architecture for the Web and Beyond.) In each of these roles, I’ve been frustrated by the missed opportunities to apply UX principles and practices.

I started Rosenfeld Media in 2005 with the goal of publishing books whose design and development showed that a publisher could practice what it preached. Since then, we’ve expanded into producing industry-leading conferences and workshops. In all cases, UX has helped us create better, more successful products—just as you would expect. From employing user research to drive the design of our books and conference programs, to working closely with our conference speakers on their talks, to caring deeply about customer service, we practice what we preach every day.

Please visit rosenfeldmedia.com to learn more about our conferences, workshops, free communities, and other great resources that we’ve made for you. And send your ideas, suggestions, and concerns my way: louis@rosenfeldmedia.com
I’d love to hear from you, and I hope you enjoy the book!
Get a great discount on a Rosenfeld Media book: visit rfld.me/deal to learn more.
View our full catalog at rosenfeldmedia.com/books

Josh Clark is principal of Big Medium, a digital agency that helps complex organizations design for what’s next. He is a design leader specializing in emerging technology, user experience, and design innovation. His projects include future-friendly interfaces for AI, connected devices, and the web for many of the world’s biggest companies.
Josh speaks around the world about what’s next for digital interfaces. He has keynoted hundreds of events in over 25 countries and has offered countless more private workshops and executive sessions.
Josh is also author of several other books, including Designing for Touch and Tapworthy: Designing Great iPhone Apps.

Before the internet swallowed him up in the 1990s, Josh was a producer of national PBS programs at Boston’s WGBH. He shared his three words of Russian with Mikhail Gorbachev, interviewed the cabinet secretaries of nine US presidents, and wrote trivia questions for a primetime game show.

In 1996, Josh created the “Couch-to–5K” (C25K) running program, which has helped millions of skeptical would-be exercisers take up running. His motto is the same for fitness as it is for user experience: no pain, no pain.
Josh lives, runs, and works in the mountains of Asheville, North Carolina.

Veronika Kindred is a designer and researcher at Big Medium, where she defines and solves design problems alongside some of the world’s biggest companies. She travels internationally to lead Sentient Design workshops and speak to teams at startups and Fortune 100 companies alike.

Veronika holds degrees from New York University (Politics and Data Science) and the Fashion Institute of Technology (Photography). Her photographs have appeared in O, The Oprah Magazine and UN Women. Her research has ranged from AI’s effect on user experience to mobile technology’s impact on African political engagement to the nuances of congressional climate hearings.
